title: Penn State softball defeats Ohio State by run-rule, tying up series
author: Luci Palacios | For the Collegian
description:
After a heartbreaking 4-3 walk-off loss to Ohio State on Friday, Penn State returned to the field in Columbus for Game 2 of the three-game series, looking to bounce back.

The Nittany Lions defeated the Buckeyes in run-rule fashion in the second game of the series, 15-7.

Sophomore Lorin Boutte started in the circle for the Buckeyes and held the top of the Nittany Lion lineup silent despite walking Brooke Klosowicz.

Bridget Nemeth took to the circle for the blue and white. Nemeth’s start wasn’t as easy as Boutte’s, giving up a two-run homer to Abbie Wilhelm. After two straight hits, coach Clarisa Crowell checked on Nemeth, leaving her in.

The Buckeyes had tricks up their sleeve as Estrada attempted to steal second, distracting the Nittany Lion defense. While that happened, Bewick made it home, giving the Buckeyes a 3-0 lead, along with Estrada ending up in second.

Nemeth disrupted this momentum to get the third out, ending the first inning.

The blue and white struggled to start the second inning, and both Frankee Flesher and Nemeth hit the ball outfield and got out. With this, the Buckeyes showed how dominant their defense can be as the Nittany Lions were unable to secure any runs.

But the blue and white responded and did the same thing, leaving the Buckeyes with no runs in the second inning either.

The Nittany Lions stepped it up at the base to start the third. Natalie Lieto got hit by a pitch and was ensured a walk to first. Oneacre sent a base hit to left field to follow up, making it runners on first and third.

Klosowicz stepped to the plate with two runners on base and ripped a home run, sending everyone home, tying the game 3-3.

Taylor Cruse responded to this in the very next inning and got a solo home run to start for the Buckeyes, making the score 4-3. Bewick continued this reign by hitting it far out of the outfield and made it to first.

Coach Crowell noticed Nemeth struggling and called a timeout. Nemeth is taken out for Freshman Mackenzie Duncan.

Duncan was the turnaround the blue and white needed as she earned back-to-back strikeouts. Neal hit to third, but Gradishar was able to get her out, ending the Buckeyes' run.

The rain and wind seem to be a persistent issue for both teams on both sides of the ball.

With that being said, it caused an error for the Nittany Lion defense in the fourth. Limon was able to make it to third and Parisien to second.

Cruse takes the plate and sends it to center field. The Nittany Lions failed to catch it, and Limon and Parisien made it home, giving the Buckeyes a 6-3 lead.

Bewick took advantage of a weak Duncan and hit a base hit, securing an RBI single, bringing Cruse home, making the score 7-3.

Crowell called a timeout and substituted Duncan with another freshman, Brevyn Kellepouris. She finished the fourth inning with no more runs.

Oneacre started out the fifth with a solo homerun, closing the gap, making the score 7-4, giving the Nittany Lions a spark of hope. Also tied the Penn State single-season RBI record all-time with 53 RBI’s.

Both Flesher and Jordan George hit a single, with a fielding error by Landmesser. Barbanente hit a base hit, and with that, Flesher made it home with an error in right field. Then, another error by Landmesser on a throw to home, making the score now 7-5.

The Nittany Lions swapped pitchers for a third time in the bottom of the fifth, now with McKenna Young at the mound.

To start the sixth, Lieto hit a solo homerun and made the score 7-6, with a comeback in sight for the Nittany Lions. Oneacre hits a two-run home run, giving the Nittany Lions the lead 8-7.

The Nittany Lions were extremely dominant in the sixth, scoring 10 runs to come back and win 15-7 behind a whole team offensive approach.

Up Next

Penn State continues with its series finale in Columbus tomorrow at 4 p.m.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State women's basketball guard Moriah Murray commits to Florida
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After three years of serving Penn State, Moriah Murray is off to a new home.

Murray committed to Florida on Saturday, she posted on her Instagram.

The Dunmore, Pennsylvania, native entered the portal on April 1 and became the second player to commit to their next school.

During the 2025-26 season, Murray made 29 starts, averaging 9.4 points, 3.1 rebounds and 2.1 assists per game.

Throughout her Lady Lion career, Murray has been a 3-point weapon, totaling 188 triples during her three years at Penn State.

Now, Murray will have an opportunity to play for new coach Tammi Reiss.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: 'It’s been an honor to go out with these guys' | Penn State men's volleyball hosts Senior Day
author: Keira Albert | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State concluded its regular season with a big win and a Senior Day send-off.

The Blue and white’s last regular-season match of the year ended on a high note, leaving it undefeated in conference play ahead of the EIVA Tournament.

With the match also being Senior Day, the energy in Rec Hall was high as the stands were packed with family and friends of the four seniors being recognized.

In a brutal five-set game, the Nittany Lions pulled out a huge 3-2 win. The game concluded with a ceremony for seniors Miller Trubey, Jaxon Herr, Ryan Merk and Owen Rose.

“Pretty typical [George] Mason-Penn State match,” head coach Mark Pavlik said. “I thought we played pretty well.”

The blue and white did have a great game against the Patriots. It was a hard-fought battle, with both sides playing offense and defense.

This sets the Nittany Lions up well as they approach the postseason. Officially undefeated at 12-0 in the EIVA, the blue and white took the No. 1 seed ahead of the tournament.

They also enter the tournament on a nine-game winning streak.

There were several standouts from the match. Players like Sean Harvey and Tristan Hassell played a massive role in the success for Penn State. In fact, the two players had a combined 44 kills on Saturday.

However, with Pavlik’s final few games ahead, as well as the final few games for the veteran seniors on the roster, it was a high-stakes game.

“It’s been Groundhog Day for me for 30 plus years,” Pavlik said. “I think I’ve been somebody who’s been pretty good about being where my feet are and not looking down the road.

“It hasn’t hit me,” Pavlik continued. “But it’s been an honor to go out with these guys.”

While the season isn’t finished for the blue and white, the postseason is always uncertain and doesn’t always guarantee another game will be played.

“We don’t want to be that group that doesn’t win Pav’s final year,” Rose said.

“I don’t want to lose this game, but also it’s like, I don’t want to lose this game for Pav,” Merk said. “Pav’s given everything he’s got this year. I want to make him proud.”

Notably, Merk had a strong game on Saturday, proving that very point.

The libero had 10 digs against the George Mason squad and proved himself to be a leader on the court.

Rose was right there with him. The middle blocker hit .545, putting up eight blocks and seven kills.

“Everything he’s taught us and given us, you know, we want to give that back this year and really make it a memorable year for him,” Merk said.

“And that it has been,” Pavlik said.

“He’s been here the whole time we’ve been here,” Rose said. “Because we’re not coming back, we won’t know Penn State without Pav.”

The blue and white will now look ahead to the postseason. Having clinched the EIVA regular-season title, earning them home court advantage, it will look to take home its 38th win in the tournament in program history.

The Nittany Lions will face their first postseason matchup on Thursday.

title: Hunter Aquino’s five-point night fuels No. 14 Penn State men's lacrosse's trouncing of Rutgers
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
PISCATAWAY, N.J. – Hunter Aquino ended the regular season just like he started it.

Inside Holuba Hall on February 1, the newly forged attackman nearly tore a hole in the net, firing four goals into the nylon and notching a pair of assists as the Nittany Lions battled Colgate in an 18-14 victory.

He stayed hot afterward, recording 11 points and two hat tricks in the next four games. It seemed as if the reigning Big Ten Freshman of the Year was on his way to earning even more awards in his sophomore campaign, and first at attack.

In March, however, something changed. Aquino was ruled out of the lineup for a high-stakes matchup with Cornell, and due to a still-unspecified lower-body injury, the Easton, Pennsylvania native was sidelined for six games.

Aquino finally returned to action in last week’s dramatic loss to Michigan, recording just two points as the blue and white fell 9-8 in Happy Valley.

“Last week, it was the first time he played in-game competition in six, maybe six and a half weeks,” coach Jeff Tambroni said. “It’s hard to just jump on the field and play at that level.”

In his second game back, the 6-foot-5 sophomore found that same early-season form on Friday night against Rutgers. His lone assist on the night came with 6:17 remaining in the second quarter when he found Kyle Lehman, who ripped an on-the-run shot into the net.

Then, his scoring started.

Working behind the Rutgers cage, standout freshman Jack Iannantuono spun through two Rutgers defenders and found Aquino, who threw a hitch and drove towards the net before powering a shot past Cardin Stoller.

Less than a minute later he hitched again, this time past a pole after getting a pass from Michael Faraone, and ripped a sidearm shot past Stoller as he faded in front of the junior goaltender.

Good things seemed to come in pairs for the sophomore, as he got two more quick goals in the fourth quarter.

Luke Walstrum, who follows just behind Penn State’s assist leader Iannantuono with 14 feeds on the year, wrapped around from behind the crease and found Aquino, who was wide open at close range.

He dropped to a knee and rifled one between Stoller’s legs, securing the hat trick with a five-hole shot. And again, just 57 seconds after that worm-burner, Aquino face-dodged past his defender and tucked his fourth score of the night into Stoller’s net.

“He played with a lot of confidence,” Tambroni said. “I think he had a really good week of practice and got back into a rhythm. It was good to see him carry it through to the weekend.”

With four goals on the night, Aquino matched his total from the season-opener, bookmarking a turbulent second regular season as a Nittany Lion. He enters the postseason with the fourth-most points on the squad and second-most goals, totaling 32 and 18, respectively, despite his time missed.

After the win over Rutgers, the blue and white have secured the No. 2 seed and a first-round bye in the Big Ten tournament. Squarely on the bubble of the NCAA tournament, a win in their postseason-opening conference semifinal could push them over the hump.

Having Aquino back and firing will certainly help the cause.

“He gave the rest of the guys a lot of confidence (tonight),” Tambroni said.

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse finishes season with road win against Rutgers
author: Myles Hannak | Sports Editor
description:
After two consecutive losses, Penn State was presented with an opportunity on the road in Piscataway to end the regular season with positive momentum.

Ultimately, the Nittany Lions kicked the recent woes out to the curb with a decisive 18-15 win.

The blue and white opened with a starting lineup that included few surprises, but despite an early scramble, it lost the first possession to Rutgers.

The Scarlet Knights made quick work of the Penn State defense, drawing first after only three minutes of play. However, that didn’t last long, as the Nittany Lions quickly found an answer when offensive sparkplug and point leader Delaney Radin found Erika Ho.

Soon thereafter, the Nittany Lions kept on the offensive pressure. Star freshman Rocquette Allen got the ball behind the net, pivoted with a quick juke and wrapped around for another score, only 24 seconds after the first.

With the momentum, Penn State held the reins but soon relinquished them to Rutgers courtesy of a Kelly MacKinney yellow card, as the Scarlet Knights quickly scored with the advantage.

That leveled action stood for nearly three minutes until Ho scored her second goal of the game, giving Penn State a slight lead with five minutes to play in the quarter. Yet Rutgers amped up the pressure, Lily Dixon assisted the tying goal and then scored her first to give the Scarlet Knights the lead in under a minute of play.

There was a lengthy review after a Penn State challenge of the goal, but the score was upheld.

MacKinney soon after notched a free-position goal to tie things, with Peyton Wainman’s late first-quarter goal pulling the blue and white ahead after the first frame of action.

The Scarlet Knights opened the second quarter with a free-position goal of their own from Delainey Sutley. Despite Radin finding the net soon after herself, Rutgers scored another goal, which was later reversed after a review by the officials.

The Scarlet Nights scored soon after, but the pace of the Nittany Lions’ offense edged them out, as Ho and Wainman scored, lifting the blue and white to an 8-7 lead at halftime.

Rutgers began the second half with a slower pace after its shooting numbers before the break failed to reach the efficiency of Penn State’s figures. After an extensive possession, the Scarlet Knights tallied the tying goal.

The blue and white found an immediate answer on the other end when Wainman scored from just in front of the net to join Ho as the second blue and white scorer on the day with a hat trick.

Rutgers found an equalizer, and the margins between the two squads remained razor-thin after five minutes into the third quarter. Ho received a pass and shook through the Scarlet Knights’ defense to find her fourth goal of the day for the next scoring action, as she immediately tossed her stick away after the slick move.

Allen followed with a man-up goal, then Ho scored her fifth and Anna Salerno joined in to make it a four-point unanswered run for Penn State.

Ho continued to take over the game, notching her sixth goal of the game, smashing her previous career-high. The Penn State sideline and players continued to celebrate and express their lively nature of play after every score.

Scoring continued to go the blue and white’s way until Rutgers finally got back on the board to cut the deficit to 16-11. The Scarlet Knights found the net one more time before the quarter ended, with just 37 seconds left to play.

The fourth and final frame opened with a quick goal from MacKinney to extend Penn State’s lead. The blue and white then attempted to slow the pace of the game to secure its lead. Rutgers got a goal back, but it took nearly five minutes to do so. It served almost as a consolation and a sacrifice Penn State was willing to pay, as MacKinney joined the Nittany Lions with a hat trick soon after.

Rutgers got two more goals, but that was all the scoring that remained, as Penn State solidified its win.

Penn State concludes the regular season with a record of 12-5.

Up Next

The Big Ten Tournament begins Wednesday in Ann Arbor, Michigan, and will go through next Sunday, April 26. Seeding and exact times are yet to be finalized.

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse attacker Delaney Radin sets individual single-season assist record
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Attacker Delaney Radin’s high-scoring season continues to impress.

Against Johns Hopkins on Thursday, Radin broke the program’s individual single-season assist record with her 52nd assist of the season.

Radin broke the record with an assist to star freshman Rocquette Allen.

The previous assist record of 51 assists was set in 1990 by Megan Smith, who earned the mark in 18 games. Smith went on to become a first and second-team All-American.

Radin recorded three five-plus assist games during the 2026 season, including a career-high eight assists against Colgate in March.

description:
On this week’s episode of "The 1-0 Podcast," co-hosts Lexie Linderman, Avery Hill and Lyle Alenstein discuss their takeaways from Penn State’s media availability on Tuesday, including standouts on the defensive line and who will wear the green dot.

To round it up, the crew talks some NFL draft as the big day nears.

title: 2026 forward Jamison White decommits from Penn State men's basketball
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is losing its lone signee from the 2026 high school class.

247Sports composite 3-star forward Jamison White has decommitted from the Nittany Lions, he announced Saturday.

After a lot of thought, prayer, and talks with my family, I’ve made the decision to decommit from Penn State University and reopen my recruitment.I’m thankful for everything the program has shown me, this wasn’t easy, but I know it’s the right step for my journey. pic.twitter.com/DCYTABONkE— jamowhite0 (@jamowhite10) April 18, 2026

White ranked as the No. 241 player nationally, No. 41 power forward and the No. 2 player from Illinois. He plays at East St. Louis High School, where he guided the team to a Illinois Class 3A State Championship this past season.

The 6-foot-7, 215-pounder was named to the Illinois Class 3A All-State first team by the Illinois Basketball Coaches Association. White signed with Penn State last November, and Mike Rhoades spoke highly of him, saying his size and athleticism is at a Big Ten level.

"He’s sort of a late riser, late to the game type of kid, and the work and effort and approach he’s taken with it with great humility," Rhoades said on Nov. 17. "I think he’s only going to get better and better. I’m really excited about him."

White was offered by the Nittany Lions last summer, and he took a visit during the first Penn State football game in the fall on Aug. 30. He originally committed just days later on Sept. 3, but he'll now be reopening his recruitment.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s volleyball earns Senior Day win against George Mason, finishes EIVA undefeated
author: Myles Hannak | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State hosted its Senior Night and legendary head coach Mark Pavlik’s final regular-season match at Rec Hall, with all in line to end the regular season on a perfect note.

The blue and white were in a groove Saturday, claiming victory and ending its EIVA season undefeated with a 3-2 win over George Mason.

However, just as the green and gold did in Friday’s five-set thriller, the Patriots reenacted with a win in the first set. The blue and white offense got out to a quick 5-2 lead courtesy of a dominant offense, yet George Mason capitalized on errors and got its own kills by Robert Evangelista to tie things up.

Back-and-forth action resumed between the two sides until George Mason became the first team to reach the 20s via a rare Owen Rose attack error. The Patriots inched to set point with a kill by Alexander Lille, and then claimed the opening frame on a Penn State attack error by Tristan Hassell.

The leveled play at the net resumed in the second set until the Nittany Lions went on a four-point scoring run, eventually ended by a kill from George Mason. Soon thereafter, Hassell logged the first ace of the set to push Penn State ahead 7-3.

Strong serving continued to be a staple of the blue and white’s second set, as Bennett Wilson and Michael Schwob soon joined in on the action with aces of their own. Hassell then hammered a pair of kills over the net to push the blue and white’s lead to an emphatic 15-7.

The dominance continued as the Nittany Lions claimed the second set to tie up the action, 25-13. Wilson logged his second ace of the day to put the blue and white at set point.

The Nittany Lion momentum continued to open the third set, yet the Patriots logged a pair of kills in the midst of a three-point run to take a quick 3-2 lead. Hassell then notched his own pair of kills, with Wilson following with a kill of his own.

That quick spurt of offense was all that Penn State had going for it in the early moments of the set, as the green and gold obtained a three-point lead. That small gap didn’t hold for long, another Schwob ace and traded kills saw the scoreboard even at 14 a piece.

Neither side could pull ahead, despite a Hassell ace; things stood tied even into the early 20s, as both teams attempted a late push to claim the pivotal third set.

However, a Rose service error, and a pair of consecutive attack errors from the blue and white gave the Patriots set point on self-inflicted Penn State plays. Evangelista soon after logged the decisive kill to give George Mason the 25-22 set win.

That heartbreaking dropped set didn’t seem to faze Penn State, which stormed to a quick 5-1 lead in the fourth frame that was capped by a Sean Harvey ace. The momentous Nittany Lions’ offense continued to push them ahead, led by Harvey's kills.

The dominance over the net continued in the set, and a Hassell kill pushed the blue and white close to forcing a fifth frame with a 20-12 lead.

A George Mason rally was attempted, but Penn State ultimately claimed the fourth set 25-15.

The ferocity from Penn State’s offense continued with it pulling ahead 5-1 in the fifth frame behind early kills across its entire lineup. The fiery nature continued on the court, with the two sides exchanging words between exchanges of hits, but the Nittany Lions led in the transaction of points 8-5.

George Mason kept it close, tying the game at 13-13 with a four-point scoring run, and with a clear deflation on the court, Pavlik called a timeout.

Yet, Harvey called the game, notching a pair of kills to end the contest in Penn State’s favor. George Mason attempted to challenge whether the ball was in, but officials confirmed the result.

With the result, the blue and white finish the season at 20-7. Hassell finished with a career-high 26 kills, breaking the total he set in the series opener. The freshman tied Schwob and Wilson with a team-high two kills each. Harvey finished with 18 kills.

Up Next

Penn State clinched the top spot in the EIVA with its win on Friday, so the Nittany Lions got the No. 1 seed, a bye and the honors to host the conference tournament. Opponents and game time to be finalized, but postseason action begins Wednesday.

With the bye, the Nittany Lions' first postseason game will be Thursday, between the winner of the No. 4 seed and the No. 5 seed.

title: Penn State football draft profile | DT Zane Durant brings elite speed as undersized lineman
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
When Penn State brought in Jim Knowles as defensive coordinator for the 2025 season, many expected the Nittany Lions’ defense to prosper, especially Zane Durant.

But the defense declined, and most notably the defensive line — the Nittany Lions registered 12 less sacks than the season before. Durant was part of that disappointment, as he tallied just 4.5 tackles for loss after having 11 the year before.

The expectations for Durant were sky-high entering the 2025 season, earning preseason All-America honors from various outlets. Durant didn’t come close to living up to that and ended the year with just an All-Big Ten honorable mention.

Despite the disappointment, Durant finds himself in a favorable spot before the NFL draft after a stellar performance at the NFL Scouting Combine.

“It’s been a blessing to be honest with you,” Durant said at Penn State Pro Day. “I try to just showcase what I knew I could do.”

Metrics

Height: 6-foot-1

Weight: 293 pounds

Vertical jump: 33.5 inches

Broad jump: 9-foot-4

40-yard dash: 4.75 seconds

Projections

Projected round drafted: Rounds 5-7

Player comparison: Grady Jarrett

Scouting report

While Durant made small improvements during his senior season, he didn’t maximize his opportunities, going statless in four appearances.

However, his draft stock rose during the NFL Scouting Combine as he showed his speed as a nearly 300-pound defender. He led all defensive tackles in the 40-yard dash with a 4.75-second run, performing much better than the average time of 4.99 seconds. His time was the fastest for a defensive tackle since the 2023 combine.

That speed was on display during the 2025 season when he recorded the only interception of his career and returned it 14 yards, nearly for a touchdown. A few weeks later, he burst past the offensive line against Indiana and sacked projected No. 1 pick Fernando Mendoza to help the Nittany Lions almost upset the Hoosiers.

While his speed is a bright spot on Durant’s resume, he is undersized compared to the rest of his draft class. Of the 15 defensive tackles who participated in the combine, Durant was the second shortest, the lightest and had the fifth-shortest arms, something that could make teams a bit weary.

Best team fit: Carolina Panthers

Despite meeting with the Cincinnati Bengals, Philadelphia Eagles and other squads during the NFL Scouting Combine in February, the Carolina Panthers could also be a good fit for Durant.

While the Panthers have four defensive tackles already, they had six that saw playing time last season, including three that played in all 17 games.

Durant’s speed could be utilized in certain situations and formations as he takes a backup role throughout his early NFL career. As he continues to get comfortable in the big leagues, he could eventually take on a starting role on a team that finished in the bottom-half of the league for run defense last season.

title: Penn State men's lacrosse clinches share of Big Ten regular-season title, first-round bye in tournament
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State men’s lacrosse has claimed a share of the regular-season conference championship.

B1G news 🏆 pic.twitter.com/9tzQqN83aM— Penn State Men’s Lacrosse (@PennStateMLAX) April 18, 2026

Johns Hopkins’ win over Maryland on Saturday afternoon sealed a four-way tie for first place in the Big Ten, with the Nittany Lions, Blue Jays and Terrapins joining Ohio State at 3-2 in conference play.

After tiebreakers are applied, the blue and white are projected to slot in as the No. 2 in the conference tournament, meaning that they’ll receive a first-round bye before making their postseason debut in the Big Ten semifinal.

In that matchup, they’ll take on the winner of Saturday’s game between the No. 3 and No. 6 seed, currently projected to be Maryland and Rutgers, respectively.

The clash will see the Nittany Lions return to SHI Stadium, where they wrapped up their regular season, with a berth in the Big Ten championship on the line.

title: Penn State picks up commitment from 3-star S Jonathan Galette
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Deon Broomfield has struck in the 2027 cycle.

Safety Jonathan Galette has committed to Penn State, he announced via Instagram on Saturday.

Galette, a 3-star, visited the Nittany Lions on April 7, clearly liking what he saw. He has an official visit scheduled for June 12, and Broomfield took an in-house visit on Jan. 15.

The Montvale, New Jersey, native is ranked as the No. 17 player from his home state and No. 51 athlete nationally, although he projects to be a safety in college.

Galette chose Penn State over Wisconsin, which is the only other school he currently has an official visit scheduled. He holds offers from Ohio State, Michigan, Indiana, Washington, Virginia Tech and more.

As a junior at St. Joseph Regional in 2025, Galette tallied 46 total tackles, 6.5 tackles for loss, 3.5 sacks and three pass deflections in 10 games played.

title: Penn State baseball drops game against Rutgers in pitching duel
author: Zach Schroder | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State looked to get right in its second game in the series after a close defeat at the hands of the Scarlet Knights of Rutgers.

Ultimately, the Nittany Lions would lose 4-1 against the Scarlet Knights in a well-pitched game from both sides.

The Nittany Lions started the game on defense, with Ben Hudson on the mound first. Rutgers batters found the ball early, with Tristan Salinas and Jack Sweeney both making contact on their first pitches, but they ended in outs. Charlie Meglio was walked, then Matt Chantelle flew out for a quick inaugural inning.

Jayden Davis topped the order with a double through the left field gap. Vincent Borghese started on the mound for Rutgers. The Nittany Lions had no trouble making contact, but Michael Anderson, Bryce Molinaro and Spencer Barnett all grounded out.

Yomar Carreras began the second inning with a groundout. Gabriel Rivera singled, but Trey Wells struck out swinging and Chase Krewson grounded to first to switch the field.

The blue and white started its second inning strong. Jack Porter and Kevin Karstetter both singled and Avery Smith hit a sacrifice bunt to advance the runners to scoring position. Maddox McDonald singled, bringing in Porter to open up the scoring. The offense was halted after Preston Yaucher fouled out and Davis flew out. Penn State held a 1-0 lead going into the third inning.

Anderson singled through the right side to begin the bottom of the third, but Molinaro struck out swinging and Barnett grounded into a double play, making for a fast frame.

The fourth inning was just as quick, as Hudson made quick work of the Rutgers order, while the bats were silenced in the bottom half of the inning.

Rivera started the inning with a single to center field. After advancing on each groundout, Rivera reached home on a single from Ryan Jaros for Rutgers’ first run of the game.

Offensive struggles for both teams continued into the bottom of the fifth. The game was tied 1-1 moving into the sixth.

Hudson continued to shine into the sixth. After Sweeney grounded out to shortstop, Meglio struck out looking in four pitches and Chatelle struck out swinging in three. The Nittany Lion offense looked to continue its struggles. Barnett lined out and Porter popped up. But Karstetter and Smith both singled, Karstetter making it to third on Smith's hit. McDonald flew out to center, crushing any scoring chances again.

Rutgers got the bats going to top the seventh. Carreras singled, then advanced to second from a sacrifice bunt courtesy of Rivera. Wells then singled, moving the runner to third. Krewson popped up, which brought a pitching change for the Nittany Lions. Kyle Emmons replaced Hudson after a strong performance, allowing just one run. He only let up one run during his outing.

Wells would steal second before Jaros banged one through center field to bring in Wells and Carreras. Salinas would ground out to first, though the damage was done. Rutgers led 3-1.

Penn State’s offense kept the same tune into the seventh. Yaucher popped up to second base and Davis flew out to far right field. Anderson out at first made for a quick one, two, three for the blue and white.

The Scarlet Knights continued where they left off. Sweeney doubled, and Meglio singled off a bunt, advancing Wiltsey to third, who pinch ran for Sweeney. Chatelle struck out looking, but Meglio stole second. Carreras flew out to center, but Emmons walked Erace to load the bases.

Emmons continued to struggle, Wells was walked, which brought Wiltsey home. Krewson would fly out to limit the damage to one run. Rutgers led 4-1 going into the bottom of the eighth.

Borghese continued his good performance with a three-pitch swinging strikeout for Molinaro, but then walked Barnett. Porter fouled out to left field and Karstetter struck out swinging. Penn State had no offensive rhythm after its score in the second. Borghese would end his great outing with only one run allowed in eight innings.

The top of the ninth started with a pitching change; Emmons came out for Ben DeMell. Jaros flew out to center, and Salinas hit one to deep left field but was caught trying to make it a triple. Wiltsey would bunt right to DeMell, who made the play to first. The score was 4-1, Rutgers going into the bottom of the ninth.

Jason Masick came in for Borghese. Masick started his day by walking with Smith. McDonald struck out swinging on a full count. Justin Turcovski came in to pinch hit for Yaucher; he would go down swinging. Smith advanced to second and Davis was hit by his third pitch. Anderson flew one out to center to end the game.

Up next

Penn State will conclude its home series against Rutgers at 1 p.m. Sunday.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State track and field sets several top marks at Virginia Challenge
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
As Penn State continued to move through its outdoor season, several Nittany Lions made their outdoor debuts this weekend.

The Nittany Lions competed in the Virginia Challenge from Thursday to Saturday, setting several top marks with one school record.

Thursday

Junior Audrey Friedman kicks off the Virginia Challenge with a personal-best 49.93-meter throw in the women’s javelin for third place.

In the men’s javelin, freshman Nolan Carey also earned a personal best, throwing 67.14 meters for second place.

Friday

Penn State’s success stories started right off the bat on Friday, as senior Marie-Eloise Leclair earned the No. 4 mark in program history in the 100-meter prelims, running 11.39 to finish first. Later in the day, she won the finals as she ran 11.25 seconds, improving to the No. 2 mark.

In her second event of the 2026 outdoor season, sophomore Amelia McBain earned a personal-best with a fourth-place finish, jumping 3.75 meters.

Junior Claire Daniels competed in her first 1,500-meters in two years, finishing ninth in 4:28.61. In the men’s race, Jacob Smith finished eighth overall in 3:48.70.

For just the third time in her career, junior Jenna Young jumped over 1.70 meters in the high jump, winning the event with a mark of 1.74 meters for a personal best.

The women’s 800-meter invite featured another top mark in program history, with graduate student Allison Johnson finishing third in 2:04.55, the third-best time in school history. Sophomore Tayissa Buchanan finished just behind in 2:04.96 for fifth place.

In his second-ever collegiate meet, sophomore Kyle Moore set another personal best in the long jump, leaping 7.39 meters for second place.

The men' s 800m featured even more excitement than the women’s, as six Nittany Lions earned personal bests, with three earning top-10 marks in school history.

Senior Niko Schultz led the way with a second-place finish, running 1:45.233 for the third-best mark in school history. Allon Clay finished third in 1:45.235 for the fourth-best mark, while Yukichi Ishii finished fourth in 1:46.21 for the ninth-best time.

In the women’s 400-meter hurdles, sophomore Addyson White earned a personal best, running 1:00.31 for seventh, while Abasiono Akpan finished ninth in 1:01.03.

Graduate student Collin Burkhart continued his impressive career with a mark of 66.39 meters in the men’s hammer invite, finishing in fifth place.

Senior Hayley Kitching earned the second school record of the season in the women’s 1,500m, running 4:05.81 for first place, shaving over five seconds off the previous mark. Sophomore Ada Rand set the sixth-best mark in school history as she finished in 4:14.74 for ninth overall.

In the 3,000-meter steeplechase, senior Allison Dougherty finished sixth with a time of 10:38.51. In the men’s race, Justin Healey also finished sixth, running 8:59.18.

Penn State earned another top-10 mark in the women’s shot put as sophomore Sasha Garnett threw 16.44 meters for third place and the No. 6 mark in program history. Skye Petrie-Cameron threw 15.76 meters to finish sixth in the event.

That success continued into the men’s shot put as senior Ryan Henry earned the seventh-best mark in school history, throwing 19.48 meters for third place.

Graduate student Dylan Lambrecht finished just shy of that mark, throwing the eighth-best in program history, finishing fourth with 19.45 meters. Freshman Bryce Thompson continued his standout season with a 18.81-meter throw for sixth place.

Saturday

Kitching continued her weekend dominance on Saturday in the women’s 800m, running 2:02.65 to win the race. Freshman Boh Ritchie finished in second in 2:07.03.

The Nittany Lions earned another victory in the men’s high jump invite, as freshman Trip Campbell set a personal best with 2.14 meters, while Robert Allen jumped 2.01 meters for eighth place.

In the men’s pole vault, Penn State earned another top mark as sophomore Mason Bucks jumped 5.02 meters for third place.

Sophomore Matthew Sarnowski also earned a personal best in the men’s discus throw, finishing 10th with a best throw of 51.04 meters.

That success continued into the men’s 400m, where freshman Peyton Sommers finished fifth in a race of over 60 people, running a personal-best 47.11 seconds.

The Nittany Lions capped off the day in the men’s 200-meter invite, as sophomore Ajani Dwyer set the No. 4 mark in school history with a second-place finish in 20.42 seconds.

Up next

The Nittany Lions travel to Philadelphia for the historic Penn Relays from Thursday to Saturday.

title: No. 14 Penn State finds flow offensively to top Rutgers in blowout primetime victory
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
PISCATAWAY, N.J. — The dark night sky above SHI Stadium mirrored the theme of the fans that filled its stands as No. 14 Penn State battled Rutgers on Friday night.

The clash, labeled as the Scarlet Knights’ “blackout,” came under the highest stakes of any regular season game to date for the Nittany Lions, with Big Ten Tournament seeding and the Friendship Cup on the line.

In contrast to the theme, Penn State lit up offensively to earn a 16-8 victory in the regular season finale.

“It was a long week of practice,” coach Jeff Tambroni said. “I’m proud of the way the guys responded tonight.”

True to the matchup’s rivalrous history, the fireworks started early. Jon King leveled a Rutgers midfielder seconds after the opening faceoff, earning possession for the Nittany Lions.

However, another big hit in the offensive zone resulted in a Penn State turnover, and Rutgers took advantage. With his first touch of the game, Colin Zeller cut down the alley and flicked an underhand shot past Preston Hawkins to open the scoring.

The blue and white found its response with 9:02 left in the first quarter when a driving Chase Robertson found Liam Matthews wide open on the crease, the Canadian smoothly tucking the ball past Rutgers goaltender Cardin Stoller.

In one of the smoothest plays all season, Patrick Carragher set a pick for a dodging Michael Faraone, who caught a pass after his defender left to address the ball and found Jack Iannantuono, who was wide open five yards in front of the cage and had a prime opportunity for an easy goal.

Rutgers responded seconds later when Jack Lonsinger got a give-and-go feed and powered in front of the net to fire the ball into Hawkins’ net. Not long after, a second consecutive Scarlet Knight goal found the nylon when JJ Aiello beat Hawkins high on a slick feed from Colin Kurdyla.

With under 30 seconds remaining in the frame, Andrew Beard beat several Rutgers defenders down the alley and flicked a low shot to beat Stoller, to level the score at 3-3 at the end of the first quarter.

The second frame began with strong defense from both sides, highlighted by a wild exchange towards the midpoint where Brendan Leary lost the ball in front of the crease, only for the short-range shot from Colin Kelly to be stuffed by Hawkins.

Kyle Lehman finally notched the first score of the second frame with 6:17 to go, receiving a pass from Hunter Aquino and firing an on-the-run shot past Stoller to break the deadlock.

Working at X, Iannantuono spun through a pair of Scarlet Knights, threading a pass to an expecting Aquino, who drove through the defense and powered a strong shot into the top of the net. He struck again seconds later to put the Nittany Lions up 6-3.

As the public address announcer called Aquino’s name for the second time, Reid Gills took matters into his own hands, scoring off the faceoff to record the blue and white’s third goal in under a minute.

The floodgates remained open for Penn State, with Robertson getting a strong man-up look and burying his first of the night to give the blue and white a five-goal lead.

With time bleeding off the clock, Leary flattened Aiello, scooping the ground ball and working it to Lehman, who went airborne to deliver a resounding dunk on Stoller to beat the buzzer, bringing the game into halftime with Penn State up 9-3.

Lehman got his second of the night, putting the Nittany Lions into double digits with a long-range step-down blazer that bounced off the Scarlet Knight goaltender and into the net.

Penn State’s seven-goal run came to an end not long after Lehman’s second-half opener when Greyson Vorgang, whose errant shot drilled a bystander on the sideline in the first half, found the net and ripped one past Hawkins early in the frame.

Rutgers nearly sparked a run of their own when Kelly fired another past Hawkins, but the blue and white answered back in less than a minute with an unassisted goal from Beard, who scored his second on the night.

The Scarlet Knights cut the lead back to five after a shot deflected off a Rutgers helmet in front and snuck between Hawkins’ legs. The Nittany Lions’ six-goal lead was reinstated on the ensuing possession, however, as Beard found Matthews in his favorite spot on the field, who faked out the goaltender and tucked it in the net.

With the wind starting to pick up, several Penn State shots sailed wide of the cage, but quick passing around the offensive zone and a low, powerful shot from Hunter Aquino finally led to another score and secured a hat trick for the Easton, Pennsylvania, native.

“Last week was the first time (Aquino) really played in-game competition in six, maybe six-and-a-half weeks,” Tambroni said. “He played with a lot of confidence tonight.”

He’d get his fourth less than a minute later on a similar play that saw him dodge around a defender and bounce one past Stoller.

Matthews joined Aquino with a hat trick of his own when Stoller tried to run up the field with the ball. The goaltender couldn’t hang on, and the Canadian pounced on his mistake and lobbed the rock into the gaping cage.

Penn State’s lead ballooned to ten goals with a worm-burning goal from Carragher that skipped past Stoller’s left foot and into the net.

“If they can rely on each other,” Tambroni said. “The goalie and the defense, it’s a much better relationship and I thought those guys played really well together tonight.”

Rutgers got two late consolation goals from Vorgang and Brock Desmarais, but otherwise cruised to a 16-8 bounce-back victory.

Up Next

With this win, Penn State will be the No. 2 seed in the Big Ten Tournament and return to Piscataway on April 30 to play the winner of April 25’s matchup between the No. 3 and No. 6 seed.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball's comeback bid falls short, loses to Rutgers in high-scoring thriller
author: Ava Aguiar | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State kicked off its weekend series against Rutgers at home, in one of its most tenacious games so far this season.

What was sure to be a nightmare of a loss with the team seeing its worst inning of the season in the third, went down as one of the strongest rallies the team has had so far this year, but the blue and white came up just short, falling to the Scarlet Knights, 13-11.

While both teams remained off the board through the second, the Nittany Lions spiraled out of control in the third, seeing their worst inning so far this season. The Scarlet Knights' bats were on fire, putting ten runs up on the board, as Penn State continuously made error after error.

Jack Sweeney started it off for Rutgers as a strong single up the middle let in the first run of the night for left fielder Tristan Salinas. The blue and white saw their first error of the inning after a poor throw by pitcher Colin Fitzgerald allowed another run in, and began the first round of loaded bases the Scarlet Knights would have of the inning.

Things continued to go downhill for Penn State, with multiple missed catches and throwing errors, saw run after run on the board, and if the team wasn’t struggling enough, Sweeney closed out the nightmare inning for the Nittany Lions with a homer to centerfield, officially putting the score at 10-0.

“Fitz deserved way better than that; that wasn’t a pitching issue today, honestly, it was something that hurts my soul, because it’s the exact opposite of who we have been, who we want to be, who we're trying to be and to put it kindly, not ok,” coach Gambino said.

Penn State failed to make a statement after, with three straight strikeouts by Rutgers pitcher Zack Konstantinovsky, but were able to bring some momentum back and settle in in the bottom of the fourth. Junior Jack Porter scored a two-run homer for him and Spencer Barnett, before Kevin Karstetter made a break for second after a strong hit into right centerfield.

Frankie Sanchez, one of the blue and white’s strongest on the mound, came in to relieve Mason Horwat in the fifth, marking the team’s third pitching change of the game. Sweeney, for Rutgers, once again established himself as a key player for the team, putting up his second home run of the game, moments before Matt Chatelle launched another home run in almost the exact same spot. The team capped off the top of the fifth with a 13-2 lead.

One of the strongest parts of the lineup came up for the Nittany Lions to do some damage in the bottom of the fifth as Jayden Davis, Michael Anderson and Spencer Barnett made their way onto the bases. Davis kicked it off, off of Porter’s single into left field. With the bases loaded, Karstetter singled up the middle for Anderson to run home.

Maddox McDonald doubled down the left field line, with a strong hit, bringing in two runs from Porter and Barnett, closing the gap to seven runs with the score set at 13-6.

After a quiet sixth inning for both teams, Penn State was able to breathe as Sanchez pulled off a three-player strikeout on Rutgers’ lineup, giving the team a glimpse at a comeback after managing to shake off what happened in the third.

With two outs on the board, Preston Yaucher managed to pull through, hitting a power single through left field for Karstetter to run home and continue the fight, slowly continuing to close the gap now 13-7.

Sanchez continued on the mound into the eighth, having one of the best games of his season so far, getting three outs off of three balls hit right to his defense. He closed off his strong inning against Rutgers’ third hitter, Yomar Carrera, as he grounded out a ball to the mound, where Sanchez picked it up and ran it to first himself.

After Barnett and Porter were left stranded on the bases in the bottom of the eighth, it all came down to the ninth. Penn State’s bullpen dominated in the top of the inning with Ethan Bauerschmidt coming in to relieve Sanchez after a fantastic run, continuing to hold the Scarlet Knights scoreless since the sixth inning.

“Sanch did a great job, he played two fastballs in that first inning and then after that, the shape of his fastball, he executed better, and Ethan, I’m really proud of Ethan," Gambino said. "He went through some freshman stuff that freshmen go through, but I think he’s got a tremendously great future ahead of him."

With tensions high, the bases loaded, and no outs, everyone at Medlar Field held their breath as a walk from Michael Anderson to first sent McDonald across home plate. Fans continued to wait as Bryce Molinaro, who struggled all game, striking out each time he went up to bat, walked to first, sending in another run to walk in, this time Preston Yaucher.

Rutgers called for a pitching change in the middle of the inning, with Jordan Savinon coming onto the mound for Chris Sand. A single through the left for Porter put the game at a three-run gap, as Karstetter was able to reach on a fielder’s choice, allowing Anderson in, while Porter got out on a throw from second to shortstop.

On a pop-up by Avery Smith, the Nittany Lions came up just short, finishing the first game with a 13-11.

“I’m aggravated with the group, and I still believe in the group," Gambino said. "As a coach and parent, when you’re young, you don’t think you can have two opposing emotions at the same time, but I love them, but I’m really mad at them."

Up Next

Penn State and Rutgers returns to Medlar Field for the second game of the series at 2 p.m. Saturday.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s volleyball clinches EIVA regular-season title, to host conference tournament
author: Chase Mitchell | The Daily Collegian
description:
After taking down George Mason on Friday night, No. 15 Penn State has clinched the EIVA regular-season title and the rights to host the conference tournament, where a champion will be crowned.

The Nittany Lions have won the EIVA Tournament 37 times in program history and have won the tournament for three years straight, now looking to add another on their home court. The last time the blue and white topped the EIVA standings season was in the 2024 season.

Not only will the blue and white have home-court advantage throughout the tournament, but it will also be the No. 1 seed and have a favorable path to the championship.

The surefire future Hall of Famer Mark Pavlik has led the Nittany Lions to 26 EIVA Tournament titles and 25 NCAA Tournament appearances and walks into the 2026 edition of the EIVA Tournament with a résumé that speaks for itself.

The regular-season crown adds to an already impressive résumé for a program that has long set the standard of dominance in the conference, but Penn State must now look ahead and shift its focus to the postseason.

With momentum on their side and the conference title secured, the Nittany Lions will look to carry their strong play into the EIVA Tournament as they pursue yet another championship, hoping to once again go dancing in the NCAA Tournament.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's volleyball defeats George Mason in five-set thriller
author: Liana Tiamzon I For the Collegian
description:
Penn State entered Rec Hall anticipating a historic contest against George Mason.

After a dominant seven consecutive wins and tough matches against Sacred Heart. The Nittany Lions stood in line for a chance to clinch the EIVA regular season title on the same night as the celebration of legendary head coach Mark Pavlik.

Penn State won a difficult match against George Mason, 3-2.

In the program’s 50th season, the Nittany Lions had to start their final series of the year in style.

The Nittany Lions started this match off with Matthew Schwob with an amazing serve that came back but got blocked by the great defenses of Sean Harvey, Owen Rose and Matthew Luoma, creating a service error by Michael Schwob. Penn State led 3-1 early, but the Patriots slowly crept back, never trailing by much.

Another assist from Schwob as Rose, a premier blocker, served and helped Bennet Wilson deliver a kill to put the Nittany Lions up 6-4. Luoma and Schwob were unstoppable on the first front of the first set tonight as the latter got another kill.

A block assist by Schwob, Harvey and Rose resulted in an attack error by Jackson Herbert that forced George Mason's forwards for a timeout as it fell behind by five points.

The Patriots showed their determination to stay in the game with multiple substitutions, but this did not deter the Nittany Lions as they delivered a back-to-back kill by Luoma and Harvey. This resulted in George Mason using their second timeout of the first set.

Wilson notched another kill, assisted by Ryan Merk, which made this game a dominant 8-point lead for the blue and white. Although the Patriots showed great defense on the net.

The Nittany Lions fought to a 10-point lead against the Patriots, with Schwob serving an ace.

The set ended with a serve by George Mason’s Herbert, which resulted in an error. Penn State won the first set 25-16.

The second set started with a serve by Aiden Weltin. Penn State earned the first point of this set with an assist from Schwob and a kill by Luoma. The Nittany Lions put up their second point shortly after another serve by Schwob.

George Mason got its first lead of the match on an attack error by Wilson. The Patriots got a successful block assist by Robert Evangelista on Luoma. Evangelista proved to be dominant in this set as he followed with a kill, but then logged a service error, continuing the one-point lead of 6-5.

Penn State gained another point by Luoma’s kill, followed by two attack errors. More back-and-forth offensive errors continued for a deadlocked score of 9-9. This second set showed George Mason’s urgency and awareness of the stakes.

The Patriots slowly gained an 11-9 lead by utilizing continuous block assists. They worked to stay aggressive offensively and looked to disrupt the Nittany Lions' rhythm. The match remained competitive with a five-point lead going to the visitors.

George Mason’s Herbert and Evangelista showed up even more with block assists and kills, helping their team gain an even larger gap from Penn State. An attack error by Harvey forced Penn State into their second time out of the second set, 19-13.

The Nittany Lions slowly crept back, an attack error by Herbert and a block assist by George Mason’s Alexander Lillie helped build the Nittany Lions a bridge to close the gap at 19-17.

Penn State brought out new faces from the benches, Jaxon Herr and Derek Nagel. A kill by Gaige Gabriel and Rose helped the Nittany Lions get back at a two-point deficit, 23-21, after the Patriots fired back.

Ultimately, the Patriots won the second set by an attack error by Rose, 25- 22.

Schwob started the third set with a serve; the Patriots earned the first point with a kill from Elijah Lazor, but Penn State quickly earned that point back with an attack error by Herbert.

Harvey smashed a kill to close Penn State’s gap to one point. George Mason continued to swap new and old faces back on the court.

Penn State tied the set 11-11, and that matched pace from both sides continued.

A service error by Georgi Zahariev and a block assist from Gabriel to Harvey caused an attack error by Lillie, providing Penn State a one-point lead. George Mason called a timeout, 15-14.

Gabriel committed a service error, which tied up the set. Luoma, with another attack error, caused the Patriots to lead by a point, followed by a kill from Weltin and they earned another point.

After Lillie had a service error and an attack error from Lazor, the Patriots were forced into a timeout as Penn State tied the set once more.

Whilst being up by two points, George Mason strategically called for another timeout in the third set. Shortly after the play started, Penn State earned the point by an attack error by Herbert.

The Nittany Lions tie the set, with a block assist from Wilson and Gabriel, 22-22.

George Mason’s Michel Schofield got a serve error and tied the game once more, creating intense last moments of the set. An attack error by Harvey ended the third set 25-23.

The fourth set started with a serve from Weltin. Penn State fought back and earned the first two points of the set with two kills from Harvey.

Penn State showed to be dominant at the start of this set, 4-0. George Mason earned its first point of this set by a service error from Schwob.

This Nittany Lion lead came close to a loss as the Patriots continued with consecutive kills by Weltin and Lillie, which caused Penn State's four-point deficit to a two-point close set once more, 9-7.

Another kill by Harvey kept the Nittany Lions afloat, and service errors created their original four-point gap once again. Penn State called for its first time out in the fourth set, while still being ahead by four points.

George Mason, falling behind five points, called for its first time out shortly after. The Patriots reentered the game and lost another point to a kill by Harvey.

Penn State’s momentum changed this set, reminding everyone what’s in the line with this potential win against George Mason now they led 22-16. The Nittany Lions stayed consistent with attack errors from George Mason only one point away from the set win.

A serve by Wilson and a kill by Gabriel sealed the Nittany Lion set win, 25-18.

The final set started with a serve and successful service ace by Luoma, which earned the first Nittany Lion point. A block assist by Harvey, Sean, and Rose caused an attack error by Weltin, earning another point for Penn State.

Penn State completely shifted their energy and momentum in these last two sets as they led by four points again from the start of the fifth set like they similarly did in the fourth set. A block assist by Wilson, Bennett, and Rose caused an attack error by Herbert that caused George Mason to call for an early timeout.

A dominant early set for Penn State with continuous block assists from Wilson, Bennett and Gabriel. As well as kills from Harvey and Luoma who have had an outstanding joint 42 kills in this match against George Mason. Luoma reached his career high with 22 kills, forcing George Mason into another timeout.

Penn State ends the last set with dominance, 15-6.

This game continued Penn State's win streak, which reached eight, and clinched the EIVA regular-season title. They now host the EIVA tournament next week at the Rec Hall.

Up Next

Penn State faces George Mason again, when the Patriots and Nittany Lions return to the court at 3 p.m. tomorrow, April 18th, in Rec Hall. Fans can also watch on Big Ten Plus.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State track and field star Hayley Kitching shatters 1,500m school record
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Despite not usually running the 1,500-meter, Hayley Kitching took it head-on at the Virginia Challenge.

Kitching broke the program record on Friday, running 4:05.81 to break the previous record by over five seconds.

Virginia Challenge | 1500mRECORDS LEFT AND RIGHT!Hayley Kitching earns the school record and facility record, while Ada Rand improves to sixth all-time in program history!🥇| Kitching | 4:05.819 | Rand | 4:14.74#WeAre #PSUTF pic.twitter.com/uOQIWVEd1A— Penn State Track & Field/Cross Country (@PennStateTFXC) April 18, 2026

title: Penn State softball suffers walk-off loss in series opener against Ohio State
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State took a road trip for the first time in April, traveling to face Ohio State. The Nittany Lions faced the Buckeyes in search of their first Big Ten win in two weeks.

The Nittany Lions fell in heartbreaking fashion, as Ohio State earned a 4-3 walkoff victory.

Freshman right-hander Jenna Molk got the nod for the Buckeyes, quickly earning a strikeout to start the game. However, a walk and two infield errors allowed Penn State to load up the bases with just one out, and the Buckeye miscues proved to be costly

On a two-strike offering, Molk hit sophomore centerfielder Natalie Lieto with a pitch, bringing in a Nittany Lion run.

Two more walks surrendered by the Ohio State freshman allowed for the blue and white to take an early 3-0 lead without recording a hit.

Junior right-hander McKenna Young took to the circle for Penn State in the bottom of the first, already with a cushion on her side. Young kept the Nittany Lions intact, forcing a three-up, three-down inning with help from her defense.

It almost looked like deja vu for the Buckeyes at the top of the second inning as another error gave the blue and white two on with one out. Unlike the previous frame, Molk and the Ohio State defense escaped the jam.

Young and the Penn State defense stood tall in the second, with another one, two, three inning, capped off by the right-hander's first strikeout of the night.

Molk dialed in to start the third, striking out Michela Barbanente and Madison Seidel. Junior right fielder Frankee Flesher came up with the first hit of the game on a single to left field, but Ohio State ended the threat quickly.

An error and a walk allowed the Buckeyes to earn their first base runners of the game in the bottom of the third frame. Now threatening, Ohio State welcomed its top of the order to the box with one out.

Young set down Hadley Parisien on a full count strikeout, but a long center field single from Abbie Wilhelm brought in the first Buckeye run of the game.

To start the fourth, the Nittany Lions brought the top of its lineup to the plate for the third time of the night. After Breanna Hanik grounded out to start the frame, Allison Oneacre looked to have an extra base hit for the blue and white, but Ohio State center fielder Skylar Limon had other plans.

Limon played the ball as it bounced off the wall, shooting to throw Oneacre out and keep a runner out of scoring position.

The Buckeyes chipped into the Penn State lead once more in the bottom of the fourth, as Taylor Cruse led off the frame with a home run, cutting the score to 3-2.

Molk and the Ohio State defense continued to settle in and remain comfortable in the top of the fifth inning, forcing the Nittany Lions to leave two runners on base.

The blue and white turned to its bullpen in the bottom of the fifth, bringing in freshman left-hander Brevyn Kellepouris to face the Buckeyes’ eight, nine and one batters.

The leash was short for Kellepouris, allowing two baserunners via a hit and fielder's choice. As Ohio State welcomed three-hole hitter Sami Bewick to the plate, coach Clarisa Crowell turned to sophomore right-hander Abigail Britton to tally the final out of the frame.

Britton answered the call as Bewick succumbed to a strikeout after seeing just one pitch.

The Buckeyes made a pitching change themselves to start the sixth inning, bringing in sophomore right-hander Lorin Boutte, who allowed nothing but a walk.

Britton returned to the circle in the top of the sixth, opening the frame by striking out the home run-hitting Cruse. This was followed by a couple of groundouts to Barbanente at second base, as the Nittany Lions' defense remained strong and held a one-run lead heading into their final at-bats.

Boutte started her second inning of relief off strong, retiring the first two Penn State batters before allowing a single to Flesher. One hit wasn’t enough for the Nittany Lions to hold a two-out rally, as Boutte struck out Macy Chamberlin for her second of the inning.

The blue and white still led 3-2 heading into the bottom of the seventh, with Britton set to face the bottom of the Ohio State order.

Britton recorded a strikeout to start the game, but a walk to Izzy Neal and a double from Limon put two runners in scoring position for Parisien at the top of the order.

The Penn State right-hander came up with a big strikeout to set down Parisien, and Crowell opted to intentionally walk Wilhelm.

Bewick stepped up as the Buckeyes’ hero with a single through the five-six hole that brought in two base runners and completed Ohio State’s comeback victory.

Up Next

Game 2 between the Nittany Lions and the Buckeyes is set for 2 p.m. Saturday.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State football draft profile | OL Drew Shelton offers experience, agility
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
For offensive linemen, it’s not about themselves, it’s about how they can best shine a spotlight on their teammates coming out of the backfield.

So for left tackle Drew Shelton, it was about how he could give Drew Allar enough time or how he could help keep a gap wide enough for Nick Singleton and Kaytron Allen to blaze through for a gain.

Shelton earned a full-time spot as Penn State’s starting left tackle in 2024 and never looked back, as he once again earned the starting job his senior year.

Despite a rough start for him and the offensive line as a whole in 2025, Shelton improved in the second half of the schedule en route to Penn State’s three-game winning streak to close out the regular season.

“It's been great getting in front of teams,” Shelton said at pro day. “It's a dream come true, like every little kid grows up wanting to be in this position, getting to be able to be in front of teams and spotlight, kind of who I am as a player, the athleticism, kind of leader and competitor they can get has been big for me.”

Metrics

Height: 6-foot-5

Weight: 313 pounds

40-yard dash: 5.16 seconds

Vertical jump: 31 inches

Broad jump: 9-foot-4

Projection

Projected round drafted: Rounds 5-7

Player comparison: Geron Christian

Scouting report

Shelton offers speed and agility, allowing him to make up for a lack of raw strength with quick recoveries when he needs to improvise his footwork, a skill he gained from his previous basketball experience.

He has a tendency to overstep laterally, unintentionally creating interior gaps for his matchup to fill en route to the quarterback. Additionally, Shelton can be slow on the uptake with T-E stunts, leading to missed assignments and free rushers on the edge.

That said, Shelton’s presence and ability to accurately progress from the trenches to the secondary give his running backs the space they need to cut through the gaps.

In the 2024 College Football Playoff semifinals, Shelton and the offensive line helped Nick Singleton tie the Orange Bowl record for most rushing touchdowns in history, scoring three. While Singleton was the scoring threat, Kaytron Allen was the driving force with 82 rushing yards.

Shelton pulled his hamstring while running his second 40-yard dash at the NFL Scouting Combine and was unable to work out at pro day. He should be cleared to run by the draft, but the lack of extra work at pro day could result in his stock falling.

Best team fit: New York Giants

While Andrew Thomas is an elite left tackle, protecting Jaxson Dart’s blindside as one of the NFL’s top pass protectors, he’s injury prone and has spent much of his six-year professional career on the sidelines.

Thomas hasn’t played a full NFL season since 2022, but Shelton went his entire collegiate career without an in-season injury, which could make him a reliable depth option if Thomas continues to be injury-prone.

Additionally, whether Thomas is injured or not, Shelton can still benefit from the veteran’s mentorship through the early stages of his professional career.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse closes season at No. 22 Rutgers
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Amidst a two-game losing streak, Penn State has one final shot at the win column before the postseason comes around.

The Nittany Lions travel to Piscataway, New Jersey, to face No. 22 Rutgers at 4:30 p.m. Saturday.

While Penn State has earned its spot in the Big Ten Tournament, a win on Saturday would be a step in the right direction for an NCAA Tournament appearance.

All-time matchups

Penn State’s all-time series with the Scarlet Knights hasn’t been competitive, as the Nittany Lions hold a dominant, 25-2 all-time record.

Starting in 1978, Penn State won 21 straight games against Rutgers, including an 18-0 shutout, a 25-3 beatdown and another 20-5 domination. In 2021, the switch flipped as the Scarlet Knights earned two straight wins.

Rutgers beat the blue and white by one goal in 2021 and by five goals in 2022, both in Piscataway.

Last season, the Nittany Lions earned one of their closest wins in series history, winning 12-10 at home.

Former Nittany Lion Lauren Saltz was the difference-maker in last year’s contest, scoring a career-high five goals. Erika Ho and Kelly MacKinney each added to the success with two goals each.

2026 Scarlet Knights

Rutgers is experiencing one of its best seasons in recent years, coming into the season finale with a 10-6 record.

After losing the season-opener to then-No. 21 Army, the Scarlet Knights bounced back and forth with big wins and tough losses.

Rutgers has faced three top-10 teams throughout April in No. 1 Maryland, No. 2 Northwestern and No. 6 Michigan.

While the Scarlet Knights came out on the losing end against the Terrapins and Wildcats, they upset the Wolverines 13-12. Following the win, Rutgers entered the top-25 for the first time this season.

A main contributor to Rutgers’ success has been sophomore Caroline Ling, who ranks third in the Big Ten with 53 goals this season.

Outside of the young star, an even younger freshman, Kate Theofield, sits second on the team and eighth in the conference with 40 goals, despite coming off the bench in every contest.

Saturday’s contest

While Penn State is coming off two straight losses, it has a high-powered offense that has the capability to strike at any minute.

Fortunately for the Nittany Lions, Rutgers has had goalkeeping issues all season, bouncing back and forth between two keepers.

Graduate student Simone Koryszewski started the season in net before being replaced by sophomore Stella Quilty on March 11. Despite starting in each game since, she has only played for a portion of each game lately, including 11:43 against Maryland and 30 minutes against both Michigan and Northwestern.

For the Nittany Lions, goalkeeper Sydney Manning has been a solid starter, recording a 49.2% save percentage.

On the attack, Penn State doesn’t have any top-five goal-scorers in the conference like Rutgers. Instead, the team has experienced a much more balanced attack.

MacKinney, Payton Wainman and Rocquette Allen lead the way for the blue and white with 41, 39 and 38 goals, respectively. In addition, junior Delaney Radin leads the Big Ten with 49 assists in addition to her 25 goals on the year.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State earns first OL commitment in Class of 2027
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Ryan Clanton has struck for the first time in the 2027 class.

Owen Reilly has reportedly committed to Penn State, he announced via Instagram on Friday.

Reilly visited the Nittany Lions on April 16 after picking up an offer on Jan. 23. He's a 3-star ranked as the No. 36 offensive lineman nationally and No. 12 player from Maryland, per 247Sports composite.

He spent his junior season in 2025 primarily at left tackle, but also earned some snaps on the defensive side of the ball where he tallied two tackles for loss and one sack.

Reilly is a multi-sport athlete, as he also partakes in track and wrestling. He's qualified for states twice on the mat and once clocked a 14.61-second 100-meter dash.

The Maryland native plays high school football at Bel Air and sits at 6-foot-3.5, 290 pounds.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's hockey adds 2 new signings to its roster
author: Sami Altmire | The Daily Collegian
description:
Forward Alanna Devlin from Boston College and defender Ashley Kokavec from Vermont are set to join the Nittany Lions in Hockey Valley for this upcoming season.

Devlin, a sophomore from Marshfield, Maryland, played 59 games during her time in Boston. She was also a member of the U.S. Under-18 Women's National Team.

Welcome to #HockeyValley, Alanna ✍️🏒#WeAre pic.twitter.com/O8LJPEA4pK— Penn State Women’s Hockey (@PennStateWHKY) April 17, 2026

Kokavec, a Junior from Parkland, Florida, played for Maine her freshman season before heading up to Vermont, where she played all 36 games for the Catamounts. Her junior year also saw a career high of 38 games played. She was also a NEHWA All-Star (2024-25) and Hockey East Third Team All-Conference (2024-25).

Welcome to #HockeyValley, Ashley ✍️🏒#WeAre pic.twitter.com/gaHVswJc43— Penn State Women’s Hockey (@PennStateWHKY) April 17, 2026

Both of these players have decorated history and will look to add to the growing roster for the Nittany Lions.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State football draft profile | OT Nolan Rucci brings size, run blocking
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Entering the 2025 season, Nolan Rucci was amidst a battle for the starting right tackle position, a spot he held for four games in 2024 after Anthony Donkoh picked up a long-term injury toward the end of the year.

Fast forward eight months and Rucci has a chance to hear his name called in his home state at the 2026 NFL Draft.

Rucci was one of the most sought-after recruits in the 2021 class. A 5-star and the top player in Pennsylvania, Rucci chose to go to Wisconsin despite Penn State being in contention for him.

After the 2023 season, Rucci entered the transfer portal and returned to his home state to play his final two seasons.

In 2025, Rucci earned the starting spot at right tackle and started in all 12 regular season games during his redshirt senior year.

“Football has given me everything I've had,” Rucci said at pro day.”Just the opportunities that you get to play in front of crowds like this, and have those opportunities to step out on the field and win big games for your team. That's always been a thrill for me.”

Metrics

Height: 6-foot-8

Weight: 314 pounds

Vertical jump: 30 inches

Broad jump: 9-feet

40-yard dash: 5.44 seconds

Pro shuttle: 4.87 seconds

Projections

Projected round drafted: Round 7

Player comparison: Jack Nelson

Scouting report

Rucci grew into his 6-foot-8 frame when he joined Penn State. At Wisconsin, Rucci was listed at just 297 pounds for his redshirt freshman season.

The former Nittany Lion fits more into his body now at 314 pounds and became a mauler in the run game. His style of blocking fits a run-heavy offense like the Nittany Lions had with Nick Singleton and Kaytron Allen.

The pass game is where Rucci struggled more, often getting beat in his matchup, but he allowed just one sack during his time as the right tackle.

If Rucci can find more success in pass protection, he could carve out a nice professional career, adding to recent success with Penn State tackles.

Best team fit: Indianapolis Colts

Rucci’s large size will likely get him a spot on an NFL roster entering training camp. The Indianapolis Colts have consistently had strong offensive line units, no matter the results of the team.

The current listed starting tackles are Bernhard Raimann and Jalen Travis, both of which line up size wise with Rucci. Behind them, the depth is a bit more thin and the Colts could add to it potentially late in the draft with the selection of the Nittany Lion.

Rucci would join a former teammate Tyler Warren in Indianapolis and the familiarity with Warren could help the transition from his home state to Indiana much easier.

In addition, Rucci has had multiple meetings and conversations with the Colts, showcasing their potential mutual interest.

The Colts have one of the top running backs in the NFL in Jonathan Taylor, and adding a run blocking tackle, someone who could even be added to bigger sets on the field, could grow on the success Taylor has had with Indianapolis.

No matter where Rucci ends up, his run blocking talents will be key in a game that has featured more running over the last couple of seasons.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State women's basketball guard Kamaria McDaniel signs contract with Washington Mystics
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Former Lady Lion Kamaria McDaniel's big break may finally be here.

McDaniel signed a training camp contract with the Washington Mystics of the WNBA, according to All Things WNBA on Thursday.

Washington Mystics announced they have signed forward Mya Hollingshed (8th overall pick in 2022, LVA) and guard Kamaria McDaniel to training camp contractsThey have also signed forward Christeen Iwuala (Ole Miss) to a rookie scale contract pic.twitter.com/9wXVDYpf06— AllThingsWNBA (@AllThingsWNBA) April 16, 2026

The Inkster, Michigan, native spent three seasons with Penn State, with a breakout 19.8 points per game her junior year.

McDaniel transferred to Baylor and ultimately to Michigan State to finish her career.

After graduation, McDaniel played for various international teams, including Cangrejeras De Santurce and HeNan. In 2024, she also participated in training camp with the Las Vegas Aces, but was waived before the start of the season.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's basketball adds Olivier Cadet as assistant coach
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State coach Tanisha Wright continues to assemble her squad.

Wright added Olivier Cadet to serve as an assistant coach and video coordinator on Thursday.

Welcome to Happy Valley, @CoachOcadet! 🦁#WeAre pic.twitter.com/XWmBRwkljV— Penn State Women's Basketball (@PennStateWBB) April 17, 2026

Cadet is coming off a three-year stint at Georgia, where he served as the director of skill development and video analytics.

With the Bulldogs, he helped them to a 47-47 record, including an NCAA Tournament first-round appearance this year.

Before his collegiate coaching career, Cadet served in numerous internships with the NBA and volunteered with the Atlanta Dream of the WNBA from 2022-2024, where he met Wright.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State introduces new fall Greek life recruitment timeline option
author: Rachael Keller | News Editor
description:
Penn State will implement a new fraternity and sorority recruitment framework during the 2026-27 academic year, allowing individual organizations and governing councils to begin their recruitment process several weeks into the fall semester or continue with the current spring timeline.

The Interfraternity and Panhellenic Councils will determine whether to implement the new model or maintain the spring recruitment timeline, according to a Penn State news article. The Multicultural Greek Council and National Pan-Hellenic Council will have the option to implement whichever timeline is believed to fit best.

Councils and chapters that choose to pursue the fall framework will be expected to meet additional university requirements, including clearly defined recruitment timelines, adhering to social event moratorium periods and other safety measures.

The article said the decision was the result of “informed insights” from RISE Partnerships, a consulting company, reports. The new framework also addresses concerns of “ghost pledging” or other unsanctioned, unsafe recruitment methods.

MORE CAMPUS COVERAGE

title: Senior Column | Doing it scared
author: Julia Frederick | The Daily Collegian
description:
It’s a bit of an understatement to say I’m an anxious person.

Anxiety is the invisible devil on my shoulder, influencing my life with whispers echoing in my head. It’s my alarm clock, default setting and last thought before I drift off to sleep.

Freshman through junior year, I did a decent job of keeping it at bay. It was there, of course, but manageable.

This year, for whatever reason, my anxiety decided it was the perfect time to come and knock me square on my butt. It pushed me over, kicked me while I was on the ground, stole my lunch money and called me names – classic bully style.

I was frequently on the verge of panic, desperately trying not to provoke the beast living in my mind.

There were times I spent the day in bed because I was shaking so bad. I’ve canceled plans because I couldn’t fathom leaving my apartment. Certain everyday things became nearly impossible.

To put it bluntly, it really sucked.

I tell you this for no other reason than this: Don’t let it win.
