If I can boil down what my senior year of college has taught me into one thing, it’s perseverance. It’s getting up, dusting yourself off and going again.

Whatever it is you’re going through — be it mental, physical, emotional, or other– don’t let it keep you from living your life. Believe me, I realize that’s much easier said than done. When you’re in the thick of it, it can feel like you’ll never see the light again.

There is no simple fix. It’s not as easy as deciding “hmm, that’s it! I’m not going to be anxious anymore!”

Like many things in life, it’s hard work. There’s trial and error. Sometimes you’ll wake up and feel like you’re back where you started.

Keep going. It’s worth it. You’re worth it.

If you need to take a step back, do it. If you need help, please make that phone call, schedule that appointment.

Whatever it takes for you to live your life to its fullest, do it.

In my English 472: Current Theories of Writing and Reading class (shoutout Professor Tripp), we read “In the Cemetery Where Al Jolson is Buried” by Amy Hempel. The story has a few lines that really stuck with me. They read:

“‘What is your worst fear?’ the instructor asked, and I answered, ‘That I will finish this course and still be afraid.’”

My worst fear is slightly different. I’m terrified that my anxiety will keep me from accomplishing everything that I want to. What scares me to death is living my life passively, having not done anything.

In a matter of weeks, I will walk across the stage at the Bryce Jordan Center having earned a bachelor's degree in English. I will have earned a four-year degree from a university 173 miles away from home, something I doubted I could do more times than I can count.

During those four years, I’ve done more than earn a degree. I’ve pushed myself further than I thought I could go. I was anxious during it all.

Despite that, so much good came of it.

I’ve made peace with the fact that I will likely always be afraid. My anxiety isn’t going anywhere. That’s OK.

I’ve decided that I will live my life doing things scared. It’s better than not doing them at all.

Of course, there are a series of people I would like to thank. You’ve enriched my life more than you know.

To my family, Mom, Dad, Michael and Maggie, thank you for always supporting me. I am who I am because of you. I love you all so much.

To my freshman through senior year roommate, Sydney, thank you for being by my side through it all. I’m beyond proud of all you’ve accomplished and know that you’re going to do great things. Please always keep in touch.

To Brynn, my best friend, thank you for being my cheerleader even though you’re states away. Having you as a friend is something I’m thankful for every day.

To my friends, Dimitri, Connor and Ava, you’ve made college feel like what I’ve seen in the movies. You're some of the best people I’ve ever met.

To Jaz, my English department bestie, I’m in awe of your work. Never stop writing. I’m looking forward to more shenanigans next year.

To my Webster’s family, Jeff, Zeb, Larry and Jodi, thank you for welcoming me and giving me a place to call my home away from home. You’re living proof of the power of music.

Finally, to all who read this, thank you. I’ve loved writing my silly little columns over the past few semesters. As the Beatles say in my namesake song, “Half of what I say is meaningless, but I say it just to reach you.”

To all, do it scared. These years are what you make them. Live them to their fullest.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Senior Column | Goodbye to all that
author: Emma Strickling | The Daily Collegian
description:
When it came time to start thinking about college, Penn State was where I wanted to be. My mother was an alumna, and she only ever had complimentary things to say about her experience. In the time leading up to when I would step foot onto this campus for my first year, I built a lot of it up in my head, making me believe this was going to be the greatest experience in my life. With graduation vastly approaching in only a few weeks, I can confidently say that these weren't the best years of my life. Not even close.

It was often lonely and isolating. I kept questioning what I was doing wrong and whether I made the right choice or not by coming here, waiting for a sign that would tell me so. Those things didn’t help. While I recognize there are things I could’ve done differently, that’s not to say I didn’t try. I tried really hard. Sometimes things just don’t work out how you imagined them to be.

While this entire column could just be one big pity party, I would rather focus on a few positive things throughout my time here.

Primarily, my academic evolution. I came into college a finance major, which, when I acknowledge that now, it seems strange to me. I found the material uninteresting, I felt out of place and I dreaded any future with finance as a career choice.

My saving grace was a Shakespeare class that I took as a humanities general education credit. It was everything my finance classes weren’t and what I found was what I was looking for. I ended up changing my major to English (yes, you heard me, an English major) and I still consider this one of the better decisions I’ve made, as it has been incredibly meaningful and rewarding. It was fairly risky, considering it was later in my collegiate career and there was a possibility of not working out again. But sometimes you just have to take that leap and go for it.

That same vein of “just going for it” is how I ended up at The Daily Collegian. Leading up to that point, I read it pretty much every day, but then I thought why not try writing for it. As you can probably deduce from me writing this for the Collegian, I got in, where I would spend a year and a half on the lifestyle staff and then move on to the opinion staff this semester.

The Collegian has definitely been something that has pushed me outside of my comfort zone, in that it challenged me to put myself out there more. I’ve never been one to seek the spotlight, mostly since I was too busy trying to avoid it, but being on the Collegian was like my own exposure therapy. It allowed me to face what I deemed in my head as too scary and realize that what I’d been fearing and avoiding wasn’t as scary as I thought. It eventually helped me to raise the stakes and I got my own radio show with The Lion 90.7 FM where I’ve been playing music once a week during this school year.

If you had told me freshman year that I would be writing pieces for anyone to see and having a radio show that anyone could listen to, I would have thought you were confusing me for someone else. I think if there’s one thing I can take away from these years is the personal growth I’ve experienced. It might have been something that took me to the end to realize, but regardless, I’m proud of the progress I have made.

At this point, I would like to make two acknowledgements of gratitude.

To The Daily Collegian. Thanks for taking a chance on me, especially for the opinion section. My pieces were sometimes obscure, but thanks for publishing them anyway.

To my family, especially my parents. Thank you for your patience and support during this time. I am incredibly grateful for all that you’ve done and all that you continue to do for me. I love you more than words can describe.

College might not have been what I hoped it would be, but that’s OK. I’m still grateful for the chance to receive higher education. I don’t know what’s next, but I’m not worried. I found my way before and I know I can do it again. In terms of my time at Penn State, I’m saying goodbye to all that. While I could look back with anger and disappointment, I’m choosing to look forward with hope, faith in myself and the satisfaction of knowing I didn’t peak in college.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Senior Column | ‘I’d like to thank the Academy.’
author: Skylar Williamson I The Daily Collegian
description:
When I was a little girl, I used to walk around my house delivering acceptance speeches to absolutely no one.

I would say, “I’d like to thank the Academy,” probably standing on the coffee table, holding an old dance trophy from the prior year. I was fully convinced that whatever living-room performance I just staged was Oscar-worthy.

I didn’t know what the Academy actually was — I still don’t really know. I didn’t even know what I was winning. I just knew that one day I wanted to stand somewhere and feel like I made it big.

Looking back, I think the instinct I had to create, perform and express my personality was always there. I just didn’t know what shape it would take.

Growing up, I was involved in everything there was to offer.

I started dance classes when I was two years old, where I met my best friend, and it shaped all of my important developmental years. After high school, I let go of it to pursue other things.

I played a few sports. By “play” I mean tried. After my first day of tennis practice, I told my mom I was “ready to play a real game” — obviously, that wasn’t an option. I was over it, so I left.

After she saw me doing pirouettes in the outfield at a softball game, she recommended I just stick with dance anyway.

I took art classes, acting classes and anything that sounded remotely interesting. If there was an opportunity to do something creative or competitive, I wanted in.

Unfortunately, all these activities gave me the attitude that I needed to try everything and hopefully become good at it — even though I didn’t give tennis a fair chance. I don’t know why I needed to be an overachiever as a little girl.

This mindset poured into my academics. I was the most overachieving student to the point that it was annoying for everyone around me, including myself.

For a while, it worked out in my favor. Although I definitely got heat from being a “teacher’s pet.”

Eventually I had to sit down and decide what I actually wanted to do in the future. I learned the hard way that no one really knows exactly what they’re doing until they’re in it.

When it came time to choose a college, I was convinced of only one thing: I wanted to go far away. There was nothing left at home for a girl who’s practically tried everything.

That’s where Penn State comes in.

I accepted my offer on a whim, and I didn’t expect that decision to change my 18-year-old self completely. For such a sure girl, I still can’t believe how much college can confuse a person.

Remember that overachiever attitude I mentioned — college ripped that right out of my soul and stomped on it. In college, no one is the best, and while sometimes that attitude still lingers, it’s manifested as motivation instead.

I started at Penn State as an architecture major.

On paper, it made sense. It was creative, structured and respected — all things I wanted to be. It was nothing like being the infamous and “overly easy” communications major.

Spoiler alert: I’m graduating from the College of Communications.

It was all the things I thought I wanted, but something was off. It was the first time I found something that limited me, but something I also couldn’t walk away from — completely different from the tennis debacle.

So I pushed through until the second semester of sophomore year, until I had to accept that I didn’t shine at something. I also accepted that it was OK.

No college student wants to switch majors. But switching to journalism made me feel personal growth for the first time in a long time.

I realized that journalism reshaped not only that negative, go-getter attitude I adapted from my childhood, but my outlook on my life as a whole.

I joined journalism-related extracurriculars to explore my own writing style, even though I felt unqualified. All these people started the major as a freshman, and I was a year and a half behind.

Then the first article with my name on the byline appeared. Was that the Oscar-worthy performance I’ve always wanted?

Those bylines mattered more than I expected them to. And then, eventually, they didn’t.

Not because it stopped being meaningful, but because it became normal. It became a part of my everyday life.

Somewhere along the way, I stopped waiting for the big cinematic "I've made it moment.” As it turns out, it doesn’t happen like that.

There’s no single speech, award or specific moment where everything suddenly works out in your favor.

My passion for architecture is still there, with architectural studies as my minor. The funny thing about college is that I had that option all along. It’s the best of both worlds.

So, this brings me back to that little girl standing in her living room, annoying her parents, thanking the Academy.

I don’t have an Oscar. But I do have something to say.

So instead of thanking the Academy, I’d like to thank Penn State.

And I guess this is my unofficial goodbye speech to college:

Hello everyone — or everyone who’s reading.

First, I’d like to thank Penn State for being the place I ended up randomly, and the place that humbled me.

You’ve taught me that it’s okay to let my guard down, fail at things and stop being so uptight with life all the time. You let Skylar figure out Skylar when she didn’t know what to do.

You’ve given me some of my best and worst days, and for that, I’m forever grateful.

To The Daily Collegian, all my editors and my co-writers — thank you for breaking me out of my shell and allowing me to try something new, even when I probably sucked. I never had any thought that I didn’t want to be a part of this organization, and that’s how I knew I ended up exactly where I was supposed to be.

You’ve all helped me fall in love with writing, even when I wanted to take a fist to my laptop.

To my parents (mom, don’t cry) — I can honestly say I wouldn’t be here without any of your help and talking me down when I needed it the most. Most girls make their parents worry as teenagers. Not me.

I got a little bit sidetracked at times, and I give credit to you both for putting up with all my craziness. This goes for you too, Pop. I hope I've made all the worrying you’ve all done worth it.

To my sister and brother, my bestest friends — thankfully, I have two siblings who make it so easy for me to be the best in the family. I’m absolutely joking, and the comedic delivery of that would’ve sounded so much better if this were a spoken speech.

In all seriousness, I always know that I have two people to go to for a laugh, to vent, or just to talk about my shenanigans with, even when we’re 200 miles away.

To my friends, old and new — thank you for letting me go crazy and dragging you into all of my good (and bad) ideas. You’ve all kept me sane while driving me insane, and I wouldn’t change these last four years for the world.

Let’s cherish these last few short moments we all have together as undergrads and make them worthwhile.

This isn’t an Oscar speech. I’m not standing in front of a crowd with a golden statue. In fact, maybe I’m writing this, and no one will even read it.

I’m just accepting a degree in journalism. But in my own way, it’s just as important.

Because this time, I know what I’m accepting and who I’m thanking.

So maybe that little girl wasn’t entirely wrong. The only thing she was wrong about was putting her bare feet on her dad’s Pottery Barn coffee table.

And with that, it's time to open a bottle of something strong after saying — I mean writing — this speech.

Cheers to the class of 2026!

MORE COLUMNS

title: Senior Column | Do what you love
author: Greg Finberg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Many students enter college anxious about making new friends or whether their fake ID will work at bars.

That wasn’t me.

Mainly because there are only a few people I enjoy talking to, and I hate going out to loud, cramped places. But also because I was concerned with something else.

How would I break into the sports industry?

At 5-foot-8 and four years past my basketball prime, my NBA dreams had succumbed to reality. I knew playing wasn’t my destiny. So what next? What avenues could I explore to find a job in sports?

I knew I wanted to cover the Washington Wizards, which I had done briefly in high school. So I created a blog called “Wizards Wave,” where I wrote weekly stories about the team and its happenings. But I still wanted more. I wanted to be on-site and experience true reporting. I wanted to learn “real” sports journalism.

So, as a sophomore, I applied for a role on the sports staff at The Daily Collegian.

I’ll never forget walking into the Willard Building, decked in a full suit (I purchased it the day before after I realized none of my nice clothes fit me anymore) and with my newly crafted resume in hand to greet Liam Wichser, the sports editor at the time.

Liam was wearing a hoodie, shorts and a grin that told me everything I needed to know about how stupid I was for wearing a suit to this clearly relaxed interview.

I felt like a clown for dressing up when it wasn’t required (as someone who only wears hoodies and sweatpants when given the option) and was overcome with concern that my interview went poorly. But it didn’t.

I got a job as a reporter on the men’s hockey beat, one I was excited to dive into. But to be honest, I thought I was above the Collegian.

I had built a small but strong following on X through my Wizards coverage, which gave me a warped view of what really mattered in this industry. I thought social media engagement was everything. I thought your status as a journalist revolved around the opinions of others online.

So it was a rude awakening when my hockey tweets got zero engagement (except for the occasional like from my fellow beatmates). My articles were wordy, my coverage was that of someone simply going through the motions and I again found myself asking: Is this what I want to do?

I had quickly lost my passion for a profession I’d only recently found. I needed someone to show me why I should continue, someone who embodied everything great about sports journalism.

Enter Avery Hill, my candidate buddy on the men’s hockey beat. From our first interaction, Avery’s dedication and passion for journalism impressed me. I noticed how much he enjoyed his coverage. I witnessed the care he showed each word he typed into his stories.

More importantly, Avery had fun with journalism. He enjoyed his job, which is the main thing I sought when entering Penn State. So I adopted that same mentality.

I attended every weekly media session and became comfortable asking Guy Gadowsky why goaltender Liam Souliere couldn’t save anything that season or why his team underperformed after nearly making the Frozen Four the season prior.

I wrote countless stories and covered every possible game. I learned from my beatmates Avery and Lexie Linderman — two of the most talented reporters I know. I grinded, and it was in that grind that I, too, found my passion for sports journalism.

My Collegian journey took off from there.

I entered my second year with the Collegian — my junior year of college — hoping to grace the men’s basketball staff. Mike Rhoades had just finished his first season at Penn State, and as someone hoping to cover basketball professionally, I wanted to gain experience at the collegiate level.

But sometimes, better opportunities present themselves. I was contacted by Sam Woloson, my old sports editor and the Collegian’s managing editor at the time, about applying for the vacant sports editor position for the fall 2024 semester.

I was honored that Sam considered me a worthy candidate. I was also terrified.

I had mapped out my Collegian trajectory and confined editor status to my senior year. Quite frankly, I didn’t think my credentials fit those of an editor overseeing 50 reporters after just two semesters on staff.

I remember texting Daniel Mader, who edited alongside Sam during my sophomore year, about the workload required of Collegian sports editors. Dan didn’t sugarcoat it. He said it would be hard and would consume most of my time. But I knew I could do it.

So I applied. And I got the role alongside my friend, Zach Karpf, whom I personally asked to be my co-editor. The next nine months were the hardest I had worked in my life.

I felt like I could never relax because there was always a read or an update or a staff issue that needed handling. But that’s sports journalism, and I loved every second of it. I loved the grind. I loved the leadership. And I loved churning out sports news to thousands of readers.

While those months included days with double-digit reads, Noah Aberegg’s 10,000-word feature stories and late nights at Willard making the newspaper, they were the most rewarding in my journalism career. I felt like I had learned to write all over again, and I left my post in April 2025 with a nuanced view of news writing.

There’s a reason sports editing is a two-man job at the Collegian. I wouldn’t have successfully edited and published thousands of stories during those nine months without Zach, who helped me budget weekly coverage, run our staff meetings and handle the several (it was actually way more than I anticipated) issues we had on staff.

I always enjoyed our weekly trips to Irving’s for breakfast before our budget meetings, where I would ask people to take more updates and you would happily tell the staff what they were doing wrong. You made editing fun, and I thank you for that.

I also have to shout out Will Horstman, who wrote hundreds of updates that made my life so much easier. Among the many talented writers I worked with, some of my favorite stories included Adam Waxman’s John Seifarth feature, Noah’s Kathy Drysdale feature, Andrew Deal’s Carter Starocci feature, Maggie Hardy’s Reese Laubach feature and Chase Fisher’s story on Matt DiMarsico’s game-winner that sent Penn State to the Frozen Four.

I took a summer off after editing before returning to the men’s hockey beat. Some likened my return to LeBron’s homecoming with the Cleveland Cavaliers in 2014. I’ll take the modest route and say my return was more like King Mack’s second stint at Penn State — brief but effective.

It took some time to reacclimate to being on a beat rather than editing stories for that beat, but after media day, I was already locked in. Unlike my first season two years prior, the men’s hockey team was actually quite good. They had Gavin McKenna, the consensus No. 1 pick in the 2026 NHL Entry Draft, and several key returners following last season’s Frozen Four run.

And this season didn’t disappoint. They made the NCAA Tournament and trekked to Albany, New York, for the regional round. My beatmates and I followed suit, staying in a luxury hotel in beautiful Albany for my first trip with the Collegian (it’s kind of wild it took until my last game coverage to make this happen, but I digress).

Penn State lost to Minnesota Duluth — a shocking first-round exit that concluded my game coverage at the Collegian. It was a weird feeling leaving MVP Arena. I was sad my coverage had ended. But I was more grateful for the experience the Collegian had given me alongside so many talented writers.

The men’s hockey beat was truly awesome. Chase was always grinding and showcasing his passion for hockey coverage. Maggie is one of the most talented writers I know — and someone whose writing, even when I was an editor, taught me so much. And Evan Smith…

Evan is so talented (when he actually writes stories instead of making wild jokes that make the whole beat laugh when they probably shouldn’t). All jokes aside, Evan made going to Pegula Ice Arena fun. He made media sessions funny. And I appreciate him for that.

While I probably have more people to thank, someone I couldn’t finish this column without praising is Lyle Alenstein, with whom I’ve grown close during my time at the Collegian.

Lyle showed me how much fun I could have on my beat. He always exuded enthusiasm for his coverage and found interest in the smallest things, like Dollar Dog Night at Medlar Field at Lubrano Park or Meatball Monday at McLanahan’s or the “Elite Ball Knowledge” guy on TikTok.

From Day 1, I noticed how meticulous Lyle was in his coverage of Penn State football. He had notifications on for accounts I had never heard of and was always happy to take an update or an extra media story. Bottom line: He was plugged into his beat the way all beat reporters should be. It also helped that he’s a tremendously gifted writer whom I’ve learned so much from.

Lyle’s passion for whatever interested him — a passion that extended beyond journalism and into everyday life — taught me how rewarding it is to love what you do.

It’s something I’d chased since my first day on campus. And thanks to Lyle and countless others at The Daily Collegian, I close my three-year chapter, passionate about doing what I love: telling sports stories to those who care enough to read them.

— 30 —

MORE COLUMNS

title: ‘It's a hard time to be queer’ | Queer students discuss LGBTQ+ resources on campus, call for improvements
author: Vasco Chen I For the Collegian
description:
Penn State is consistently ranked among the top LGBTQ+ friendly universities in the country, due to its offering of resources such as gender-inclusive housing, gender-affirming clothing and gender diverse care. However, some queer students say there’s room for improvement.

Samrat Sharma said the university should improve resource availability for queer students who don’t fall in the 18-22 age range.

“A lot of grad students, they’re not the typical age,” Sharma, a fourth-year doctoral candidate in education and women, gender and sexuality studies, said. “There are students like me who are queer, who are international, and we are above the age of 30. And we have no resources and it feels isolated sometimes.”

Sharma said introducing group sessions for older queer students would be helpful, as it's important to facilitate spaces where people can relate with one another. He said these sessions are especially necessary because what queer people in their thirties experienced growing up differs from what those belonging to younger generations experienced.

“Imagine if a 30-year-old gay person just goes up to a 19-year-old gay person and starts talking about ‘What does it mean to be queer?’” Sharma said. “And what happens if one of them becomes my student in the classroom? That is a very personal level of sharing, which I would rather not share with my students.”

Due to Penn State’s status as a predominantly white institution, Sharma said a lot of times the issues of queer people of color aren’t addressed and microaggressions often seep into conversations.

“I've had experiences with professors who are white, but they're extremely aware of how to talk to people of color and how to take into consideration the different experiences people have and how whiteness can play a role in shaping the perception of one's experiences,” Sharma said. “But I've also had professors who have been completely ignorant, and through that ignorance, they're dismissive about experiences.”

He said the university should encourage its faculty, staff and students to think about the world through an intersectional framework.

“University kind of puts you in touch with people from all over United States as well as the world,” Sharma said. “If students are just slightly pushed, or slightly stretched in terms of their understanding of queer and racial differences, they can do wonders.”

In 24 Ritenour Building and HUB-Robeson Center Room LL011 queer students have access to Clothing Transits, which offers queer students a place to get free clothing that aligns with their gender expression. Joshua Colvin said it’s due for an upgrade.

“Finding the clothing that feels right on you is like feeling complete, feeling like you belong in your own body,” Colvin, a third-year studying economics, said. “It certainly has a lot, but it doesn't have a lot of each kind. A larger capacity of clothing would be better. Maybe give more selection options.”

Similarly, J Carvalho said Clothing Transit should be moved to a better location.

“There's been no shortage of stuff that I've gotten from there and that people I know have gotten from there,” Carvalho, a fourth-year studying political science and sociology, said. “It's just unfortunate that it's in the basement of one of the oldest buildings on campus. It's a little rank in there.”

Carvalho is the vice president of Lion PRIDE Caucus, an umbrella organization serving gender diverse students on campus by amplifying their voices and promoting inclusivity.

“It's a hard time to be queer, and these resources existing give some form of stability, some form of comfort and some form of safety,” Carvalho said. “I know former presidents of this organization used these resources because of the stress of running an organization and have gotten better because of it.”

Julian Mintz said they think there should be a larger university effort to raise transgender awareness on campus.

“It would be helpful if they could make sure that professors had a better understanding of things, like trans people exist, for instance,” Mintz, a graduate student studying physics, said. “Ideally, you would have some sort of training for it, but then you'd have the issue that the people who are most likely to show up to that training are the ones who are least likely to benefit from it.”

Mintz said Penn State has a responsibility to provide resources for LGBTQ+ students, as many don’t have access to them elsewhere.

“It would be important for students who either do not have supportive family or friends back home, or who are just recently coming out as LGBT and kind of discovering that about themselves, that it is nice to have other people who have been through some of what they're going through and can help them with it."

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: Review | Ranking smoothie bowls in State College
author: Talia Rittenhouse | The Daily Collegian
description:
It’s time to imagine yourself at the beach while sampling the acai bowls of State College.

Even though we live in the middle of Pennsylvania, there are a surprising number of restaurants where you can purchase acai or smoothie bowls. There are even more than the ones I rank below.

If you love fruit or want to try a new food, I recommend one of these bowls, as they are popular among Gen Z.

One might even consider these part of their own food group depending on who you talk to. With all of the lovely places to choose from, let’s dive into just a few in this review.

Jamba Juice - Mango Sunshine Bowl

After looking at the menu, I was happy to see this item as it includes my favorite fruits and acai bowl toppings: strawberries, blueberries, bananas and my extra customization of coconut flakes.

The Mango Sunshine Bowl at Jamba Juice in the HUB-Robeson Center had a very strong, sweet mango flavor; however, the base was thin.

I wish the consistency were thicker and less smoothie-like, so the bowl would be a little more uniform and put together for each bite. The granola provided a great crunch with each bite, but there could have been a bit more, since the bowl was fairly large.

You can easily order Jamba Juice via the PSU Mobile Order app, and it’s also located in the center of campus, perfect for a refreshing meal on these warm days we’ve been having — it’s about time.

Rating: 4/10

K2 Roots - Forever Young Bowl

While the Forever Young bowl was refreshing, with its inclusion of healthy greens and pineapple, it wasn’t my favorite because of the hemp and other seeds.

The inclusion of these made the texture less smooth, which didn’t align with my taste in smoothie or acai bowls. However, I did really enjoy the base itself, as I frequently have spinach and pineapple smoothies, so this was a great replication of that.

In addition, I enjoyed the fresh pineapple and banana slices decorating the top of the bowl, as they helped bring out the same flavors in the base.

Rating: 4/10

Playa Bowls - Nutella Bowl

This one hands down takes the cake.

The Nutella Bowl from Playa Bowls tastes like a chocolate-covered strawberry, with Nutella drizzled over the berries and bananas.

Along with these flavors, the blueberry flax granola is delicious, soaking up the acai base and bringing the crunch you need in the bowl. There was the perfect amount of granola in this, creating a lovely ratio of base to toppings.

Playa Bowls will forever be my go-to acai bowl place, as it reminds me of beach trips, even while sitting in State College, far away from the Jersey Shore.

Rating: 10/10

Tropical Smoothie - Berry Tropiboba Bowl

Talk about a diverse mix of flavors.

This bowl brings in coconut, chocolate, acai and bursting strawberry popping bubbles to each bite.

At times, all of these seemed a bit overwhelming, as the Coco Whip reminds you of homemade whipped cream, the popping bubbles of frozen yogurt shops and lastly, the acai sitting as the foundation of the bowl itself.

I think I would prefer these ingredients separately, compared to altogether, as the acai was extra sweet on its own. I don’t love foods that are too sweet, so I may be alone on this. But if you have a big sweet tooth, you should give this one a try.

The granola brought a great natural honey flavor to this dish and is one of my favorites I have tried from many different locations.

Rating: 3/10

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: Defrost the semester with these spring hits
author: Brennan Kramp I The Daily Collegian
description:
Spring is finally here. The trees are growing and the sun has decided to stick around after a long winter. Now, Happy Valley is even more enjoyable after a brutal winter.

For months, we were forced to hustle from building to building to try and stay warm. Now, we’re free to roam campus with shorts and sunglasses, embracing every second of the outdoors after being caged inside for all those months.

Once it starts to get warmer, everything about life gets a little better. Food is tastier, walking becomes enjoyable, work is not as stressful (even though spring decides to arrive right around finals) and the music sounds better with every note.

Here are some songs to help you feel as sunny as the weather outside.

“A-Punk” by Vampire Weekend

First up is Vampire Weekend’s golden hit off their self-titled debut album, “A-Punk.” The song starts off with a quick and catchy guitar riff that really captures the mood of the entire album. The vibe of this song is pretty predictable from the second lead singer/guitarist Ezra Koenig rips into the intro. That vibe being, “linking up with some friends on the beach with a cooler of beers and fish tacos.”

“A-Punk” is a pure early 2000’s jam. It’s as nostalgic as hearing those owls that coo during the day once the warm weather starts coming back around. Once you hear those familiar sounds, it’s as if the winter didn’t even exist and you’ve just time-traveled to warmer days.

“Fly” by Sugar Ray

Sugar Ray, known for being one of the most made-fun-of bands of all time, does have a few diamonds in their very large rough. “Fly,” specifically the one that features Super Cat, is a cringy yet essential warm-weather anthem.

“Fly” was meant to be sung as you wave your hand up and down outside the car window while your friend drives you to Sheetz for some mid-day grub after skipping class. A very low effort, low profile song, that doesn’t do much except put you into a free and easy state of mind. With some underlying romantic tones, Mark McGrath puts you in a Southern California trance as he weaves through the chorus.

“I just wanna fly / put your arms around me baby / put your arms around me baby.”

It’s not an amazing song, but it’s bad in all the fun and cheesy ways. It’s just bad enough to make you feel ashamed to add it to your playlist, but just good enough to embrace its charm and turn the volume up when it starts, just to spite your musically elitist friends.

“You Get What You Give” by New Radicals

Times like these seem to be a bit scary and confusing at best. That’s what this song was made for.

Released in 1998, closing in on the turn of the century, there was panic amid the uncertainty of Y2K, the music and movie industry taking a popularized and corporate turn for the worse and it seemed like everything in general was just going to implode on itself.

So, New Radicals decided to call it all out. A radio friendly, middle finger to all the noise.

Now by this point, you’re probably wondering, “Jeez what a bummer this is, why would I put this song on my spring playlist,” and while that is a fair point, I would say this: “You Get What You Give,” offers you a chance to accept the circumstances of our current lives amidst the warmth of another new day. It’s not nihilism, but understanding.

I also think it’s crucial to note that it was the closing song on Ash Brannon's, 2007 film masterpiece, “Surf’s Up.”

“Graduate” by Third Eye Blind

With the end of the semester coming just around the corner, this song is sure to put you in the end of year spirit.

For the seniors saying farewell to Happy Valley, Third Eye Blind helps remind them of all the grueling and rewarding work they have put in throughout their four or five years here. For those still grinding through their college career, it’s an empathetic cry for the end of papers, projects and finals so that they can go on into summer.

“Graduate,” is one of those songs that you would hear your Dad talk about listening to all the time back when he was in college. It makes you feel niche but also a part of a huge collective of college students trying to embrace as much of this time as possible, before eventually graduating.

This track off their 1997 self-titled album is a choice pick for sitting in chairs on the HUB- Robeson Center lawn with friends, sparking a bonfire on a late spring night, barbecues, end of the day rituals and anything that brings you together with your people.

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: Penn State football draft profile | TE Khalil Dinkins showcases strength, run-blocking abilities
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
In the 2018 NFL Draft, former Penn Stater Mike Gesicki was drafted to the Miami Dolphins. In 2021, it was Pat Freiermuth to the Pittsburgh Steelers, then Brenton Strange to the Jacksonville Jaguars in 2023, Theo Johnson to the New York Giants a year later and most recently, Tyler Warren to the Indianapolis Colts in 2025.

Penn State has a history of producing NFL-level tight ends, and Khalil Dinkins is hoping to be next in line.

Dinkins has spent the last five years with the Nittany Lions after redshirting his freshman year. During his time in Happy Valley, Dinkins recorded 37 receptions for 399 yards and seven touchdowns.

While his stats aren’t necessarily eye-catching, he’s proven to be a high-level athlete, something that lies in his DNA.

Dinkins’ father, Darnell Dinkins, is an eight-year NFL veteran, having played for the Giants, Baltimore Ravens, Cleveland Browns and New Orleans Saints. As a pro, Darnell played in 87 games, tallying 30 receptions for 250 yards with four touchdowns.

At Penn State, Dinkins made the most of his starting opportunities during his redshirt junior and redshirt senior years, recording nearly three-times the receptions and yardage as his redshirt sophomore season.

Metrics

Height: 6-foot-4

Weight: 251 pounds

Bench press: 25 reps

Vertical jump: 32.5 inches

Broad jump: 9-foot-11

40-yard dash: 4.72 seconds

Pro shuttle: 4.33 seconds

3-cone drill: 7.28 seconds

Projections

Projected round drafted: Undrafted

Player comparison: Benjamin Watson

Scouting report

Dinkins was one of nine Nittany Lions invited to the NFL Scouting Combine, where he showcased the same strength he used on the football field in his physical catching and blocking.

Dinkins recorded the third-best bench press among tight ends at the combine and outperformed Strange and Gesicki when they performed in their respective combines.

As for on-the-field talent, Dinkins played a big part in Penn State’s run game, serving as a consistent blocker throughout his career.

During Penn State’s matchup with UCLA in 2024, Dinkins showed his strength as he pushed 270-pound lineman Devin Aupiu into the endzone, making way for an Allen touchdown.

While tight end playing time was split between himself, Luke Reynolds and Andrew Rappleyea this season, Dinkins made the most of his limited receptions, averaging 11.9 yards per reception.

Dinkins had one of the biggest games of his career against the Bruins in 2025, when he hauled in three receptions for 49 yards and a touchdown. Despite having the third-slowest 40-yard dash time among tight ends at the combine, Dinkins put on the jets for a 40-yard touchdown, igniting Penn State’s attempted comeback.

The Pennsylvania native proved to be a reliable end zone target during his time at Penn State, with nearly 20% of his career catches going for touchdowns — that kind of scoring production could catch the eye of some NFL squads.

Best team fit: New York Jets

While Dinkins likely won’t be a starter early in his NFL career, he could be a good fit for the New York Jets.

The Jets featured a pair of younger tight ends during the 2025 season in Mason Taylor and Jeremy Ruckert. However, the two combined for just 548 receiving yards, leaving some room for competition farther down the depth chart.

Tight ends contributed for just two of the Jets 15 total receiving touchdowns, a stat Dinkins could help out with. With a new quarterback likely to take the starting role in Geno Smith, Dinkins could be a new target on a rebuilding team.

The three tight ends currently on the squad are all relatively Dinkins’ size, giving him an opportunity to fit in with a system of similar-built options.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Counselors, resource centers meet sexual assault survivors where they’re at
author: Sara O'Connell | The Daily Collegian
description:
Content warning: This article contains mention of sexual assault. Resources and support are available through Penn State’s Counseling and Psychological Services.

Though counseling for sexual assault survivors and those with general mental health concerns share valuable similarities, utilizing similar techniques to help clients with trauma response, they still vary in complexity and training requirements.

There are a variety of resources on and off campus for sexual assault survivors looking for specialized counseling, including Penn State’s Counseling & Psychological Services (CAPS), Centre Helps, Relationship Violence, Outreach, Intervention, and Community Education Center (R-VOICE) and other confidential medical care through University Health Services.

At Centre Helps, a local non-profit providing crisis intervention and resources for those in need, they utilize the empowerment model, according to Sarah Allison, a Centre Helps hotline counselor.

This method allows survivors to build their own story, proceed at their own pace and follow what’s best for themselves. She said it’s a proactive form of trauma-informed care.

“You have to take the extra time and precautions to make sure that you're allowing their story to be heard, and allowing them to have control over that story,” Allison,a fourth-year studying psychology, said. “A lot of the time, it can be the first time they ever choose to speak out.”

Furthermore, Allison said avoiding triggers by using clients’ preferred terminology is a key way to gain trust, as it allows clients to regain their power and avoids retraumatization.

“If they say sexual assault, we'll say sexual assault,” Allison said. “It’s important to allow survivors control. You're taking a seat in the passenger, not the driver’s.”

Stephanie Stama,the assistant director of community education & outreach at CAPS, said each session’s pace is determined by the client and they employ healing-centered approaches.

“Regardless of where a student may be in their process of healing, a counselor will provide the space and pacing that an individual needs,” Stama said.

Stama said each survivor may need different treatment because they all individually have different emotions and don’t process situations the same.

“The end goal is for a student to feel empowered when moving through the therapeutic work of a traumatic experience,” Stama said.

Denise McCann said sexual assault counseling focuses on restoring an individual’s feeling of safety, trust and self-worth. McCann, the executive director at Centre Helps, said counselors try to build trust with survivors by staying with them in the moment while reflecting and validating their feelings.

“For some people, [it’s] a new experience,” McCann said. “They aren't used to people really listening to them, and that's just what they need.”

McCann said there are common misconceptions regarding counseling, such as people believing it makes them weak.

“Everybody needs help,” McCann said. “It's okay to reach out for help.”

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: SEE IT: Former Penn State men’s hockey goalie Arsenii Sergeev takes rookie lap ahead of NHL debut
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
A former Penn Stater has made his NHL debut.

Goalie Arsenii Sergeev is making his first career start for the Calgary Flames on Thursday and took his rookie lap ahead of warmups for the Flames' final game of the regular season against the Los Angeles Kings.

Goalie rookie lap alert 👀Welcome to the @NHL, Arsenii! pic.twitter.com/gnDCNWb1Oi— Calgary Flames (@NHLFlames) April 17, 2026

Sergeev is the fourth former Nittany Lion to appear in the NHL, joining Brandon Biro, Casey Bailey and Brett Murray. He’s the first goalie from Penn State, however, to play in the pros.

The Russian transferred to Happy Valley ahead of the 2024-25 season, helping lead the Nittany Lions to their first Frozen Four appearance with a .919 save percentage and a 2.54 goals-against average — both marks were the best in program history at the time.

Sergeev was drafted by the Flames in the seventh round of the 2021 NHL Entry Draft.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse falls to No. 6 Johns Hopkins in White Out, home finale
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
For the last time this season, Penn State took the field at Panzer Stadium as it looked to end its home season with a victory.

The Nittany Lions hosted No. 6 Johns Hopkins for the White Out game, falling to the Baltimore squad 10-9 in overtime.

“It was a great lacrosse game, really back and forth,” coach Kayla Treanor said. “I’m proud of this group. Credit to Hopkins, they made plays when they needed to.”

The first six minutes of action were very defensive for both sides, with only two shots being taken overall. After a green card was issued to Johns Hopkins’ Kendal Crawford, Payton Wainman scored her 38th goal of the year to take an early lead.

After nearly five minutes with no score change, Taylor Hoss evened things for the Blue Jays before earning a green card less than a minute later.

With the man-up opportunity, Wainman scored her second goal of the year, assisted by Delaney Radin. Her assist broke the team’s single-season assist record, tallying the 128th assist of the season.

“Overall, they really share the ball,” Treanor said. “Everybody’s a threat. It’s not a one-man show.”

Following a 15-minute stoppage due to shot clock issues, the first quarter concluded with a slight Penn State advantage.

The second quarter started very similarly to the first, with slow, methodical play from each side. After four minutes of back-and-forth defensive stops, Johns Hopkins tied the game from a free-position goal by freshman Paige Willard.

Less than a minute later, it had looked like the Nittany Lions had retaken the lead courtesy of Radin. However, she was charged with a yellow card for a dangerous follow-through.

Despite the man-down situation, goalkeeper Sydney Manning earned a big save on a Blue Jay free position attempt, followed by a goal from sophomore Maggie Rezza to give the blue and white the lead back.

“I’m so pumped for Maggie,” Treanor said. “Maggie Rezza stepped up, and we’ve been working her into the lineup all year, but she was just excellent.”

Things were momentarily tied as Johns Hopkins’ Lacey Downey scored her 42nd goal of the year. However, two straight goals from the Nittany Lions — one by Anna Salerno and another by Rezza — gave them a two-goal lead.

Just 50 seconds later, the Blue Jays brought things back within one goal as they earned another free position attempt, this time by freshman Sienna Chierieleison.

After a few minutes of missed shots to open the second half, Hoss scored her second goal of the game to tie things up at 5-5.

Johns Hopkins’ defense was on lock throughout the third period, allowing just one shot from the Nittany Lions in nearly 10 minutes with four turnovers. However, Kelly MacKinney earned her first goal of the game and 40th on the season as Penn State retook the lead with the shot clock expiring.

“They have a really good defensive unit,” Treanor said. “I think we had a good game plan, and we changed things up throughout the game.”

The same sort of long offensive drive happened just minutes later for the Nittany Lions, with Erika Ho scoring off an assist from MacKinney to take a two-goal lead.

With less than a minute left in the quarter, Willard scored her second goal of the game for the Blue Jays, ending the third quarter with a 7-6 Nittany Lion lead.

A little less than two minutes into the final period, Hoss earned a hat trick on the day as she tied things up. However, things went back in the Nittany Lions' favor with Radin’s 25th goal of the season just a minute later.

Both sides continued to battle defensively, with five turnovers over the next seven minutes. With 5:28 left in the game, MacKinney widened Penn State’s lead with her second goal of the game.

The Blue Jays had a chance to trim their deficit with a free position attempt, but it was saved by Manning for her eighth save of the game.

With a little over two minutes remaining, the Nittany Lions went man-up from a Johns Hopkins green card and began milking the clock with a two-goal lead.

A costly turnover by MacKinney gave the Blue Jays a shot at offense with just over a minute remaining. As Downey worked her way on offense, she earned a free position shot, bringing the game within one goal with less than a minute left.

“With a minute left on the clock and you’re up two, you’re usually in a position where you’re going to win it,” Treanor said. “You can’t leave it up to officiating.”

After earning the following draw, Ava Angello tied the game 9-9 for Johns Hopkins with her first goal of the game.

The Blue Jays earned another draw and went on a man-up advantage, but missed the game-winning goal, sending things to overtime.

Penn State opened overtime with the draw control, followed by a quick penalty on Downey. With things all tied up, Emma Kelly took the first shot on goal in overtime but missed.

Salerno and Kelly proceeded to each take a shot, but missed. After a review of a potential shot clock violation, the call stood and Johns Hopkins took control.

After the Blue Jays missed their first shot, Downey earned a hat trick on the day, winning in overtime.

The Nittany Lions fell to 11-5 on the year and 3-4 in conference play.

“We’re not one for moral victories,” Treanor said. “I think if I was satisfied, I think that shows that I don’t believe in them.”

Up next

The Nittany Lions travel to Piscataway, New Jersey, to face No. 22 Rutgers at noon Saturday.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's lacrosse breaks single-season assist record
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Amidst a high-scoring season, Penn State continues to set records.

The Nittany Lions set the school record for the most assists in a single season on Thursday against Johns Hopkins, recording the 128th assist of the year.

Delaney broke the record with a pass to Payton Wainman, taking the lead 2-1 while breaking the program record.

The previous record of 127 assists in a season was set in 1983, when the blue and white finished third in the NCAA, beating UMass 7-6 in overtime in the third-place game.

Delaney Radin and Kelly MacKinney have led the way individually for the blue and white, recording 48 and 36 assists, respectively, at the time the record was broken.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Yoga for a Cause | 5 Springs Pilates hosts fundraiser for Lionettes
author: Ava Aguiar | The Daily Collegian
description:
Whether they’re dancing on the sidelines of Beaver Stadium or on the THON stage, the Penn State Lionettes have been a core part of the Penn State community since 1995.

The team made headlines this year after an adversity-filled season, when the University cut their funding to compete against some of the biggest names in collegiate dance at the Universal Dance Association National Championships.

Through their own efforts, the team successfully raised the $70,000 needed to travel to Orlando, but are still fighting for funding and their recognition as athletes on campus. Five students in Rachelle Pavelko’s PR Campaigns class wanted to help, centering their capstone project around fundraising for the team.

Ashtyn Webb, Ava Nociforo, Alex Servagno, Madison Leeth and Tyler Mantone made up their capstone’s agency, Blue and White PR, putting together “Yoga for a Cause” in collaboration with 5 Springs Pilates downtown in State College.

Webb, a fourth-year student studying public relations, reflected on why choosing to focus on the dance team was so important to their agency.

“I think we all saw just online there was lots of talks about dance teams, and so we were all kind of aware of it and once we started to look into it, we realized that not only are they not getting adequate funding from the school, but they also aren’t recognized as a D1 sport by NCAA,” said Webb.

Nociforo, a fourth-year student studying public relations and communication arts and sciences, believes that the class was a small glimpse into the training the team does on a daily basis.

“Obviously, the Lionettes train for three hours a week and a pilates class is only an hour at most, but that hour really pushes your body to ways you may have never known you could do,” Nociforo said. “So we really wanted to give people an experience that a Lionette has every day, so they have more of a greater appreciation for what they do.”

The event cost $20 to attend, and had around 13 attendees to follow studio capacity. Along with a 45-minute pilates class, guests received a complimentary goodie bag filled with a gift card for a free Pilates class, a K2 Roots coupon, as well as things like scrunchies, eye patches, Poppi and more.

Advertised by social media and word of mouth, friends of the group, like Zitarra Irechukwu, came out in support of the group members and in support of the team.

“I found out their funding got cut, so that was a really cool way to give back. I was surprised, because I thought the University funded most if not all of our sports teams, so that took me by surprise,” Irechukwu, a fourth-year student studying biomedical engineering, said.

Before the workout began, Lionette Sophia DeRienzo gave the class some insight into her experience on the team through a Q&A session with Nociforo.

“It was a little bit discouraging. I think when our coach told us the news, we were kind of in shock, and just upset at the whole problem. But after a couple days, we kind of realized this is going to light a fire in us,” DeRienzo, a third-year student studying kinesiology and minoring in biology, said.

The Lionettes do more than just perform during football games for the University, showing up for a range of athletic teams such as basketball and wrestling, alongside community service events, and most especially THON.

“I feel like just getting the rest of the Penn State community to see that side of us and just recognize that we truly are athletes and we push our bodies. We walk out of practice with bruises, bleeding, everything that other athletes have. Just being able to share that with the community is our main goal, because it’s something that I wish everyone could know,” said DeRienzo.

The Lionettes can be supported through the excellence fund found in their Instagram bio.

MORE SPORTS COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State DC Manny Diaz earns extension from Duke
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
A former Penn State defensive coordinator is staying in Durham, North Carolina, for a while.

Duke announced Thursday that it's signed head coach Manny Diaz to a contract extension through 2032.

CONTRACT. EXTENDED. 😈 pic.twitter.com/2HLfLo50nJ— Duke Football (@DukeFOOTBALL) April 16, 2026

Diaz led Duke to an ACC championship win in just his second year as the Blue Devils' head coach, marking their first outright conference championship since 1962. He capped off the historic 2025 season with a victory in the Sun Bowl against Arizona State.

Across his two years at Duke, Diaz has accumulated 18 wins and guided the Blue Devils to back-to-back nine win seasons for just the second time in program history.

After a brief stint as the head coach at Miami, Diaz came to Penn State as its defensive coordinator in 2022 before departing for the Duke head-coaching job ahead of the 2024 season. Diaz put together some of the best defenses the Nittany Lions have had in recent years during his brief stint in Happy Valley.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball returns to Happy Valley for conference series against Rutgers
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
After dropping two games to Ohio State in Columbus and losing its lone game against No. 15 West Virginia at a neutral site, Penn State returns to Happy Valley for an important Big Ten series against Rutgers.

First pitch for Game 1 is set for 5:30 p.m. It also kicks off an eventful weekend at Medlar Field at Lubrano Park as the series consists of three different promotional games.

Both teams sit towards the bottom of the standings in the Big Ten and have had equal troubles staying in the win column. The Scarlet Knights’ most recent conference series came against No. 1 UCLA, ultimately suffering a sweep.

With both teams fighting for a spot in the Big Ten playoffs as the season begins to wind down, there is sure to be a lot of offense from both sides. Rutgers currently holds the No. 15 spot in the conference in OPS with a .785, one spot behind Penn State.

However, the Scarlet Knights have found more success at the plate. Their .273 batting average puts them into the top 10 in the conference, three spots ahead of the Nittany Lions.

Peyton Bonds, the nephew of career MLB home run leader Barry Bonds, leads the lineup with a .358 batting average through 33 games. Additionally, the Franklin, New Jersey, native leads the offense with a .547 slugging percentage and .981 OPS.

The junior leads a Rutgers lineup that isn’t known for its power at the plate, but for contact hitting. With a team home run total toward the bottom of the conference, the Scarlet Knights are among the top teams in hits.

The Rutgers rotation sits in the middle of the pack in the conference, posting a 5.25 ERA. Zack Konstantinovsky leads the rotation with a 5.40 ERA and a 1.41 WHIP.

The bullpen is where the Scarlet Knights find most success, as four of their relievers are currently under a 4.00 ERA. Andrew Rondini has been a stellar arm coming out of the bullpen after earning a 2.16 ERA through his 11 appearances.

While production on the mound has been evident throughout the season for Rutgers, the pitching staff is put up against a Penn State lineup that has been hot as of late. Michael Anderson continued to dominate at the plate through a five-RBI weekend against Ohio State. The Havertown, Pennsylvania, native continues to hold the top spot in home runs in the Big Ten with 15.

However, it's been an opposite showing from its pitching staff. The Nittany Lions are last in the Big Ten with an 8.43 ERA. Colin Fitzgerald leads the rotation with a 6.56 ERA. A recent bright light in what has been a struggling pitching staff is reliever Kyle Emmons.

Although being unable to finish an inning in his last outing, the Pasadena, Maryland, native has been a reliable arm out of the bullpen. Emmons has only allowed five earned runs in his last four outings, earning nine strikeouts in 7.6 innings.

With both teams coming off a conference series loss and looking to get back into the win column before it’s too late, the weekend series should be a competitive battle.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State RB Quinton Martin Jr. embracing added weight, potential role as receiver
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Quinton Martin Jr. was nervous as he took the field at Yankee Stadium in December — he knew the Pinstripe Bowl was his last shot to prove he could be a starting running back at his dream school.

Martin proved that and then some with a 101-yard performance behind an offensive line missing four of its five starters. That game put Martin on the map, and he’s been continuing to build off it after deciding to stay at Penn State this offseason under Matt Campbell.

“He's done an amazing job,” running back Carson Hansen said Tuesday. “Just a lot of really highlights that you've seen in the spring. He does a great job. Has an amazing speed to power. He does a great job of picking the holes and being decisive and getting his pads low through the hole. So he's done a great job so far.”

Offensive coordinator Taylor Mouser said last week that Martin has been one of the surprises of the spring as Hansen, Martin and James Peoples continue to battle for the starting running back spot in 2025.

But the new staff has been challenging Martin as well. Campbell said at the beginning of the spring that he’s challenged Martin to become a bigger, more physical running back. As a result, Martin has gained about 20 pounds this offseason and is five away from his goal of 220 pounds by the fall.

“I definitely kept my speed 100% putting the weight on. It takes a lot of consistency and a lot of discipline,” Martin said. “The past two years, it's been a struggle for me putting that weight on. But the new program, the new habits we've been doing, the new program, everything. It's very disciplined and consistent … It's all good weight.”

Martin added running with the new weight was an adjustment at first, calling it a “process.” But he’s at the point now where he’s happy with his size and movement as he continues to work toward his goal.

As for his on-field performance, Martin feels he’s making strides as he fights to fill Kaytron Allen and Nick Singleton’s shoes and earn significant playing time this year.

“I think I've taken a big step forward in just running back all around, getting more downhill, pad level, producing and just staying consistent,” Martin said. “At times I seem to be not consistent in the past with my track or making the wrong reads, you know, not identifying the pressure to put some linebacker when I need to. So I definitely think I took a big step forward on consistency and my intent in practice.”

Martin’s now on his third running backs coach in three years with Savon Huggins, as he spent his first season under Ja’Juan Seider and last year under Stan Drayton. But Huggins is the youngest of them all, providing a sense of relatability.

Huggins also understands what Martin has been through, as the former Rutgers running back had five offensive coordinators in five seasons during his collegiate career, which has helped the two gain a strong connection.

“Quinton and I set up some goals for himself, right? Really find out what he does well, what are his gaps? So that way, again, we could really attack him,” Huggins said. “He's incrementally making better eating choices and his habits have been really, really good over the course of spring. So it's been encouraging to see where he started to where he's at, and his level of consistency in his preparation.”

Although he’s in the thick of the battle for running back, Martin has been getting some playing time out wide. He said playing receiver is “amazing,” and having Martin as an option out wide could provide him with some more playing time as he lives out his childhood dream of being a running back at Penn State.

“I love being a receiver. I love running back. Obviously, I'm here to play it, but receiver, man, it's amazing,” Martin said. “... I just feel like it's a mismatch. I don't feel like any linebacker can really go over me, and that's just me. That's my confidence in myself.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: ‘He is Penn State football’ | Dan Connor may have changed roles, but what didn’t change? His impact
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
In reference to Dwight Schrute’s role as assistant to the regional manager in the TV show “The Office,” Dan Connor’s X bio says "Assistant to the LB coach.”

Much like Schrute playing second-fiddle to Michael Scott at Dunder Mifflin Paper Company, Connor is no longer the main leader in the linebacker room, going from linebackers coach in 2025 to the assistant in the room upon the hiring of ex-Cincinnati defensive coordinator Tyson Veidt for 2026.

Despite Connor no longer holding the title of linebackers coach, Matt Campbell knew how important it was to retain Connor on his first staff.

“It took two seconds to say, ‘this human gets it,’” Campbell said in February. “He is Penn State football, what he stands for, what he talks about excellence looking like, what’s gone well, what are some of the areas that need to move forward. I think Dan was so articulate in where this program is, why he loves Penn State football, what it did for him, and honestly, how he envisions this program moving forward. It was such a great match.”

Connor acted as the de facto coach of the room under former defensive coordinator Tom Allen in 2024.

Despite not being the main voice, the services of the former NFL linebacker and Penn State’s all-time tackles leader are much appreciated by the staff and players.

“When you have a guy like Dan, who has played the position at the very highest level, and he has a view that many would not, so that's super valuable for us,” Veidt said.

His playing days have provided valuable feedback to the unit, as players have picked up on finite details to improve their game.

“He's a great technician, he knows a lot about man, if you're aligning here, it should draw these blocks and allow you to kind of scrape over the top a little bit faster,” linebacker Cael Brezina said. “And he's very knowledgeable about all the in-game stuff and the techniques and all that stuff that will allow us to be successful.”

Of the eight linebackers in the room, five of them are transfers.

Brezina, Caleb Bacon, Kooper Ebel and John Klosterman come from Iowa State, while Chris Fileppo transferred in from West Virginia, where he previously played safety. Tony Rojas, Alex Tatsch and Cam Smith are the returning trio.

Upon the transfer group's arrival in the winter, Connor helped them navigate State College and learn the history of playing linebacker at Penn State.

Now that the Nittany Lions are back on the practice field, Connor’s expressing his love of the game to the members of “LBU.”

“He loves football. I feel like that's his home,” Bacon said. “He definitely feels comfortable being on the football field, in his element, teaching, being hands-on. It's really, really good to have him with us and be able to give us that look at what an elite linebacker should look like.”

Connor didn’t need to stick around after his role change, but the players and staff are certainly glad he did. He understands what it takes to play and coach at Penn State, and despite a new title, his importance is still on display.

“It's the first coach that I've had that's actually played linebacker, and not only played linebacker, but played linebacker at a really high level,” Ebel said. “I've been trying to be a sponge from him. I know the rest of the guys in the room have done a great job as well meeting with him, but he's an outstanding resource to have in the room. There's no one else we'd rather have in there.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Report: Penn State men's basketball transfer Freddie Dilione V has signed with Georgia
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Guard Freddie Dilione V is headed back to the SEC.

The Penn State transfer has signed with Georgia, according to Chris Hummer and Matt Zenitz of CBS Sports.

Georgia has signed Penn State guard transfer Freddie Dilione, @mzenitz and I have learned for @CBSSports. Dilione ranks as the No. 66 overall transfer and the No. 7 SG in the @247SportsPortal transfer rankings. Averaged 14 ppg this year. https://t.co/EAIaZz6Le4 pic.twitter.com/DHxyGM8TAO— Chris Hummer (@chris_hummer) April 16, 2026

This is Dilione's third collegiate team in his career, as he spent his first two seasons at Tennessee before transferring to Penn State ahead of the 2024-25 campaign. He's played in 81 career games, averaging 9.5 points per game.

Dilione had a career year as a scorer for the Nittany Lions this past season, averaging a personal-best 14.0 points per game with 47.3% shooting from the field. He thrived in mid-range shooting and often created shots for himself.

Dilione will have one season of eligibility remaining to use with the Bulldogs, who have made the NCAA Tournament the past two seasons.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's hockey loses commitment from Vancouver Canucks prospect Kieren Dervin
author: Chase Fisher | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has lost a big-time commitment from its 2026-27 class.

Forward Kieren Dervin announced his decommitment from the team on Instagram on Thursday. Further, Dervin pledged to Michigan, joining one of the Nittany Lions' fiercest competitors.

Forward Kieren Dervin has decommitted from Penn State and pledged to Michigan, he announced on Instagram.Dervin, a Vancouver Canucks prospect, was selected in the third round of the 2025 NHL Entry Draft. Tough loss for Penn State as he was on pace to join the team next year. pic.twitter.com/pgbOZTjYHw— Chase Fisher (@chase_fisher4) April 16, 2026

A third-round pick by the Vancouver Canucks in the 2025 NHL Entry Draft, Dervin had a strong year in the OHL with the Kingston Frontenacs this season. He posted 42 points, 17 goals and 25 assists in 53 games.

Losing Dervin is a tough hit to the Penn State 2026-27 recruiting class. An already-thin roster gets quite weaker after he flipped his commitment to the Wolverines.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Big Ten announces inaugural women's volleyball tournament to close 2026 season
author: Ava Aguiar | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Big Ten announced on Thursday its inaugural volleyball tournament to close out the season.

One Floor. Whatever it takes. 🏐🏆The Inaugural @B1GVolleyball Tournament. Coming in November. 🎙️ @emilyehman pic.twitter.com/4JWcU1qSt0— Big Ten Conference (@bigten) April 16, 2026

The top 15 teams in the conference will automatically be entered into the tournament, where they will face off against each other throughout the week of Nov. 20. The games will begin with three matches in the first round, followed by four second-round matchups. There will be a day off between the quarterfinals and the semifinals.

The championship game is set for Nov. 25, where the winner will punch their ticket to the NCAA Tournament and be the first conference tournament champion.

Due to the tournament, the regular season will be shortened by three games, going from 20 to 17 in a “single round robin” format, with each team playing every other team once, according to the Big Ten.

title: Former Penn State men's hockey goalie Arsenii Sergeev recalled by Calgary Flames
author: Maggie Hardy | The Daily Collegian
description:
Former Penn State netminder Arsenii Sergeev received his call-up to the big leagues on Thursday, being recalled on an emergency basis for the team's final game of the season.

#Flames Roster Update: Goaltender Arsenii Sergeev has been recalled from the @AHLWranglers on an emergency basis.Devin Cooley is out with illness.— Calgary Flames (@NHLFlames) April 16, 2026

Sergeev signed with the Flames after the conclusion of the Nittany Lions' historic Frozen Four run in 2025, reporting to the team's AHL affiliate, the Calgary Wranglers. In 28 games with the Wranglers, Sergeev posted a 0.898 save percentage and a 3.29 goals against average.

The goaltender played a crucial role in the blue and white's success during the 2024-25 season, putting up a .919 save percentage and a 19-9 record in his only season with the team.

Sergeev's NHL debut seems likely for Thursday night, as he was reported to be in the starter's net during the Flames' morning practice, per Brendan Parker.

Goaltender Arsenii Sergeev leading the stretch after morning skate. Looks like he’ll make his NHL debut tonight, getting the start in Game #82. pic.twitter.com/Dm6n0UNjf9— Brendan Parker (@BParkerTV) April 16, 2026

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s basketball transfer Kayden Mingo commits to Baylor
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
The highest-rated recruit in Penn State history has found his next school.

After just one season with the Nittany Lions, guard Kayden Mingo has committed to transfer to Baylor, per DraftExpress.

NEWS: Penn State transfer Kayden Mingo has committed to Baylor, Excel Sports tells DraftExpress.Mingo started every game in the Big Ten last season, averaging 13.7 points, 4.3 assists and 2.1 steals per game.Scott Drew reloading in the backcourt. pic.twitter.com/yx9BAlu6mI— Jonathan Givony (@DraftExpress) April 16, 2026

Mingo also reportedly visited Oklahoma during his portal recruitment. He has three years of eligibility remaining.

The Farmingdale, New York, native averaged 13.7 points, 3.5 rebounds, 4.3 assists and 2.1 steals per game across 28 appearances as a freshman in 2025-26. Despite alluding to a return during a postgame press conference after the Big Ten Tournament loss to Northwestern, Mingo entered the transfer portal on April 8.

Mingo was a 247Sports composite 4-star and was the crown jewel of Penn State’s 2025 recruiting class.

title: Penn State men's hockey forward JJ Wiebusch transfers to Wisconsin

JJ Wiebusch is returning home.

The former Penn State winger has transferred to Wisconsin after two seasons in Happy Valley, per Brad Elliott Schlossman of the Grand Forks Herald.

Updating the board with J.J. Wiebusch to Wisconsin: https://t.co/CT4S5Ps7ry— Brad Elliott Schlossman (@SchlossmanGF) April 16, 2026

The Verona, Wisconsin, native tallied 77 points in 69 games with the Nittany Lions, including a 36-point sophomore campaign. Wiebusch was named to the Allentown Regional All-Tournament Team during the team's 2025 Frozen Four run.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s volleyball looks to clinch EIVA regular season title in series against George Mason
author: Keira Albert | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 15 Penn State looks to close out the regular season with a few more wins ahead of its last two-game home series. And the results of this weekend will determine the fate of the Nittany Lions ahead of the postseason conference tournament.

The blue and white gear up to play the in-conference George Mason squad at 6 p.m. Friday and 3 p.m. Saturday from Rec Hall, with Saturday’s match being Senior Day.

At 10-0 in conference play this year, the Nittany Lions will look to finish the regular season with an undefeated record in the EIVA.

If the Nittany Lions take at least one win against George Mason, they will clinch the EIVA regular-season title ahead of the EIVA Tournament. This will also give them hosting rights for the tournament.

However, it’s also a high-stakes game for George Mason. Currently second in the EIVA, two wins this weekend would allow the Patriots to clinch that same title the blue and white are gunning for.

But it won’t be an easy feat.

George Mason enters the contest at 8-2 in conference play. At 20-7 on the year, while also on a 10-game winning streak, the Patriots will surely give the blue and white a run for its money.

Owen Rose has been a massive piece of the blue and white’s defense.

Monday, the middle blocker earned the EIVA Defensive Player of the Week title for the third time this season, marking his seventh time earning the honor in his career.

He currently ranks No. 1 in not only the EIVA, but also the NCAA in blocks per set. The redshirt senior has 129 blocks on the year, with an average of 1.48 blocks per set.

But where there’s Rose for the Nittany Lions, there’s Alexander Lillie for the Patriots.

The middle blocker currently leads the EIVA in hitting percentage at .391.

Lillie has 86 blocks and 141 kills on the year. He poses a major threat to the blue and white, both offensively and defensively.

Aidan Weltin also brings a set of offensive skills to the match. The sophomore currently ranks No. 1 in the EIVA in kills per set. He totals 327 kills on the year and averages 3.94 kills per set.

But not too far behind Weltin is Penn State’s Sean Harvey.

Harvey has been a force to be reckoned with this season. He currently ranks No. 2 in hitting percentage and No. 3 in kills per set in the EIVA.

The redshirt sophomore has earned the EIVA Offensive Player of the Week title three times this season. He currently tallies 301 kills and an average of 3.54 kills per set.

However, Georgi Zahariev of George Mason earned the EIVA Offensive Player of the Week title heading into this match up. This was his second time receiving the honor this season.

Over the weekend, Zahariev marked 2,000 career assists while also totaling 11 digs, nine kills and 14.5 points.

He currently ranks No. 1 in assists per set in the EIVA with an average of 10.03 per set.

With talented rosters on both ends of the court, and both teams looking to clinch the EIVA regular season title, the in-conference matchup will be the perfect close to the schedule for the two teams.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State football draft profile | C Nick Dawkins brings elite leadership, consistency
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Nick Dawkins comes from a lineage of athletes in his family, with him having the chance to become the third football player in his family to make the NFL.

Dawkins is the cousin of NFL Hall of Famer Brian Dawkins and current Buffalo Bills offensive lineman Dion Dawkins. His father, Darryl Dawkins, was a 14-year NBA player and his other cousin, Karl-Anthony Towns is the current starting center with the New York Knicks.

At Penn State, Dawkins was a player who stayed true to the Nittany Lions and waited three years before getting the starting role in 2024.

Dawkins entered Penn State as a long-term development player, coming in as a 3-star recruit and the No. 7 player in Pennsylvania in the 2020 class. In his freshman season, he didn’t see any playing time but was named the Developmental Squad Offensive Player of the Year.

That honor helped him see the field consistently in two of his next three seasons before becoming the starter. Dawkins started and played in every game besides the Pinstripe Bowl from 2024-25 despite finishing his final campaign with some lingering injuries.

“I really wish I was able to show more athleticism,” Dawkins said at pro day. “Like some lingering issues and injuries that I wasn't able to finish out a lot of the change of direction stuff.”

Metrics

Height: 6-foot-4

Weight: 305 pounds

40-yard dash: 5.16 seconds

Vertical jump: 32 inches
