title: Penn State track and field earns numerous personal bests at Bison Outdoor Classic
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After competing in three elite invitationals to start the season, Penn State traveled just an hour away to Bucknell for the Bison Outdoor Classic.

Bringing the most athletes they have all season, the Nittany Lions earned many personal bests and three top-10 marks in school history over the weekend.

Saturday

MIT transfer Sam Engebretson got things going on Saturday morning in the men’s discus, throwing 49.65 meters for fifth place.

In the men’s long jump, sophomore Kyle Moore made his collegiate debut, winning the event with a distance of 7.23 meters. Junior Joe White finished third, earning a season-best of 7.14 meters.

The Nittany Lions earned another top finish in the women’s shot put, as Sasha Garnett threw 15.96 meters, followed by Skye Petrie-Cameron in fifth with 14.28 meters.

In the men’s shot put, freshman Bryce Thompson earned a personal best as he finished second overall, throwing 18.56 meters.

The women’s 800-meter had multiple entries for the Nittany Lions, including a trio of athletes who finished top-five with personal bests.

Sophomore Ada Rand led the way in 2:06.96 for second place, followed by Sarah Pickering in third in 2:07.30 and Lea Hatcher in 2:08.44 for fifth.

Senior Carter Fitzgerald also finished top-five in the men’s 800m, running 1:49.11 for third place.

Penn State’s first top-10 mark in program history came in the women’s high jump, where sophomore Hannah Riolo jumped 1.80 meters for the seventh-best mark in school history and first place in the event.

Gwen Cudmore finished second with a jump of 1.70 meters, while sophomore Katie Becker finished fifth with a season-best 1.65 meters.

The Nittany Lions continued to earn personal bests in the women’s 1,500-meters, as senior Katie Dallas led the way in 4:23.37 for the win.

The men’s 1,500m saw even greater success, as junior Nick Sloff ran the fifth-best mark in Penn State history, recording a time of 3:39.56 for second overall, while Ryan Watson ran 3:40.35 for third and the eighth-best mark in program history.

The blue and white wrapped up the evening in the 5,000 meters, where sophomore Eva Kynaston won the women’s race in 16:20.00 for a personal best, while senior Brendan Flynn finished fourth in the men’s race with a personal best of 14:09.48.

Sunday

The Nittany Lions kicked off Day 2 of the Bison Outdoor Classic with the women’s hammer throw, where senior Alex Pancoast threw a personal-best 55.50 meters for first place. Gabi Deglau finished third with a throw of 53.19 meters.

In the women’s 400 meters, sophomore Alexandria North won with a personal-best 53.75 seconds, while Ryleigh Hearn finished fifth in 55.53 seconds — another personal-best.

The women’s 400-meter hurdles saw similar results, as junior Abasiono Akpan earned a personal-best 1:00.06 for second place. Sophomore Addyson White finished fourth with a personal-best 1:00.72.

North competed in her second event in three hours, running 24.35 seconds in the women’s 200-meter, finishing fourth overall.

Freshman Peyton Sommers wrapped up the day in the men’s 200m, winning the race with a personal-best 20.84 seconds.

Up next

The Nittany Lions will travel to Charlottesville, Virginia, to compete in the Virginia Challenge on Friday and Saturday.

MORE TRACK AND FIELD COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball loses tough battle against Ohio State in final game of series
author: Ava Aguiar | The Daily Collegian
description:
After a tied series in Columbus, Penn State returned for its third and final game in Nick Swisher Field at Bill Davis Stadium, looking to take the series win against Ohio State.

After a long battle, the Nittany Lions fell 15-10, putting their record at 10-23 overall, and 4-11 in the Big Ten.

Ohio State came in swinging, scoring three runs against the Nittany Lions, with freshman pitcher Isaiah Shayter being the first one on the mound. Buckeye Maddix Simpson doubled down the left field line, allowing Noah Furcht to score with the bases loaded, before Buckeye Jacob Parr knocked a ball into center field for a two-run homer.

Looking to bring it back, Penn State was able to make some moves in the second, started by a setup from Jayden Davis with a double to right center field, allowing Jack Porter to wait on third before running home off an out on Kevin Karstetter. Davis was able to score another run off an error in the outfield, leaving the score 6-2.

With another two runs in by the Buckeyes in the second, Mike Gambino sent in junior Mason Horwat to take over, a player who had a decent year so far, letting in only one run and throwing a strikeout.

A quiet third inning for both teams was taken over by a home run in the bottom of the fourth for Ohio State, with Dane Harvey launching a homer into center field, leaving the blue and white in a race to catch up with a 7-2 score.

Michael Anderson, one of Penn State’s top hitters, came into the fifth strong after a home run hit into center field. But just as the team started to bring it back, Horwat was relieved by Kyle Emmons just as Ohio State took over, dominating with a powerful grand slam by Henry Kaczmar, followed by another run from Noah Furcht to put the score at 12-3.

Penn State did not hold back going into the sixth as Karstetter singled to third with the bases loaded for Spencer Barnett to run home. Porter and Karstetter were let in off of other hits to pull back 13-6.

The Buckeyes didn’t let up, as the team tacked two more runs on the board off another home run by Parr, marking the team’s third home run of the game. Just before another run would have sent the blue and white home after a mercy rule loss, Ohio State dropped the ball in the bottom of the seventh, leaving Penn State one last chance at a comeback.

Once again with their top hitting lineup, Karstetter set up Davis to run home from third before Cohl Mercado, who has been struggling in the last few games, doubled to right center field for Preston Yaucher and Karstetter to put the team up two more runs, with Anderson finishing it off as Mercado ran home to put the game 15-10.

After a huge win in the first game of the series, 10-6, and a close loss in the second, 3-2, Penn State lost in the bottom of the ninth after a close matchup with the Buckeyes.

Up Next

Penn State will take on West Virginia in a one-game neutral-site face-off this Wednesday, Apr. 15, at 5:30 p.m.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball finds ways to reach base, struggles with timely hits in loss to Northwestern
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State was overwhelmed at home this weekend by Northwestern, dropping all three of its games and being outscored by a combined margin of 38-9.

“My job as a head coach is to get our team ready, and we just weren't ready to play at the level that we needed to play today or this weekend,” coach Clarisa Crowell said. “We got to get back to work tomorrow, and I know our kids will, because they're tough kids.”

Sunday’s series finale marked the Nittany Lions' most forceful defeat of the weekend, falling to the Wildcats 17-3.

The blue and white got off to a promising start, leading 1-0 after the opening frame and recording two hits. However, the bats went quiet, and Northwestern proceeded to score seven runs before Penn State could add another of its own.

“We just have to take this thing one day at a time, and right now, it's let's just talk about some things we need to get better at, and we just have to get back to work,” Crowell said.

Despite the large margin of defeat, one thing the Nittany Lions did well in Game 3 was putting runners on base via walks.

Free bases and patience at the plate have been a key part of Penn State’s approach in the box this season. The Nittany Lions lead the Big Ten with 223 walks on the year after working seven on Sunday.

“I think if you look at the amount of walks that we have just on the year, that's something we do a really good job of,” Crowell said. “I think where we fell short is we didn't get those big hits, but yeah, we still strung together quality at-bats.”

In total, the blue and white worked 20 walks in the three games against the Wildcats. However, Penn State only managed to produce nine total hits, five of which came in the finale.

Even though the Nittany Lions’ bats had more life in Game 3, they couldn’t find timely hits. The blue and white ultimately left 10 runners on base, which was the most of the weekend.

“We just have to step up with the big hit; we left a lot of runners on throughout the weekend,” Crowell said. “We just have to clutch up and get the timely hits.”

Looking at specifics, in the fifth inning, trailing 7-1, Penn State welcomed its top of the order to the plate. Brooke Klosowicz, Allison Oneacre and Breanna Hanik all reached base on walks, with Oneacre and Hanik working Northwestern pitcher Marina Mason to full counts.

However, despite a bases-loaded and no-outs situation, the Nittany Lions failed to record a hit in this frame, leaving two runners on base. Only one run was brought in after Klosowicz scored on a sacrifice fly from Jordan George.

“I think the biggest thing for us is to just stay together and get through this,” Crowell said. “ We got to be ready to go on Tuesday, and then, obviously, we have a big series coming up next weekend on the road against Ohio State.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball suffers second consecutive run-rule defeat to Northwestern, ends series winless
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
To close out their fifth consecutive Big Ten conference weekend series, Penn State hosted the Northwestern Wildcats for their series final. The Nittany Lions suffered back-to-back losses against the Wildcats. Returning to Beard Field to host their Family Day and second Bark in the Park.

The blue and white got run ruled for its second straight game, suffering a 17-3 defeat.

In honor of Family Day, Penn State’s very own Happy Valley Hannah threw the honorary first pitch of the game.

Right-hander Abigail Britton got the start in the circle for Penn State. A quick inning, as Britton got her first strikeout of the game to close out the top of the first.

Northwestern gave freshman righty Marina Mason the start on the mound.

Starting in the bottom of the first, the blue and white bats went off, with junior Brooke Klosowicz starting things strong with a triple to left field.

Mason hit Freshman Allison Oneacre, who gave her first base. Freshman Breanna Hanik stepped into the batter's box and hit a double to get Klosowicz home for the first run of the game.

Giving up her first walk of the game, Mason walked Freshman Gabby Gradishar to get bases loaded.

Unfortunately, the Nittany Lions ended the first inning with three runners on base but were able to get the first tally on the scoreboard. Penn State led 1-0.

Another quick inning: the Penn State defense silenced the Northwestern lineup, and Britton got her second strikeout of the game in the top of the second.

After two strikes, junior Frankee Flesher kept her composure and was able to muster up a free base. The blue and white bats remained strong, but a trio of pop-ups ended the inning.

Britton continued her dominance in the circle as she shut down the Wildcats, recording her third and fourth strikeout of the game.

Mason was able to keep Penn State's offense tamed with a quick inning, getting her second strikeout of the game.

Sophomore Kaylie Avvisato started things off for Northwestern with a single to left field. Junior Emma Raye homered to left field to give the Wildcats the lead. Senior Kansas Robinson continued the momentum with another home run to right field, taking the lead in the top of the fourth with no outs.

The Nittany Lions pitching staff made a switch, adding junior McKenna Young with hopes to silence the Northwestern lineup.

Young surrendered her first walk, then junior Isabel Cunnea singled to right field. The Wildcats had two runners in scoring position. But Young was successful and got her first strikeout of the game and was able to close out the top of the fourth.

Mason continued to dominate in the circle, securing a clean scoreboard and securing her third strikeout. The Wildcats led 3-1 heading to the fifth.

Errors from the blue and white defense gave Northwestern momentum, and Avvisato was able to get a solo homer to enhance the lead 4-1.

With only one out, coach Clarissa Crowell made the change in the circle, giving freshman lefty Brevyn Kellepouris the opportunity to close out the top of the fifth.

Kellepouris hit a Northwestern batter, giving up a free base, and it went downhill for the Penn State defense. Four runs in the fifth for the Wildcats as they led 7-1.

Entering the bottom of the fifth, the blue and white were set to start with the top of the order.

As Mason got close to 85 pitches, she allowed three back-to-back walks to give the Nittany Lions bases loaded with no outs. Junior Jordan George hit a lineout that allowed Klosowicz to get home.

But that was all from the blue and whites' offense as they trailed 7-2 going into the sixth inning.

Freshman Mackenzie Ducan was the fourth pitcher in the circle for the series finale for Penn State. Oneacre moved to third and freshman Kalli Karwowski entered the game to take the place at shortstop.

With only one out in the top of the sixth and two runners on base, Duncan gave up her first walk of the game. Robinson stepped up to the plate to nail a double that allowed all three runners on base to score.

The blue and white trailed with an ever-larger deficit of 12-2 in the sixth. Junior right-hander Bridget Nemeth came into the top of the sixth with only one out and bases loaded.

Avvisato got her second homer in the game after two strikes, extending the Wildcats' lead 17-2 in the top of the sixth.

Northwestern scored 10 runs in the sixth, and Nemeth was able to close it out with her first strikeout of the game.

Mason was close to a complete game, exceeding 100 pitches that shut down the Nittany Lions' offense.

The Wildcats gave junior righty Renae Cunningham the last inning in the circle.

Cara Bohner entered the Penn State lineup with hopes to creating a last-minute rally, but struck out. Flesher walked and sophomore Macy Chamberlin singled to right center.

Oneacre was able to get a single through the left to get Flesher home, the blue and white trailed 17-3. With two runners on, Hanik was successful at the plate with a single, bases loaded with two outs in the bottom of the sixth.

Unfortunately, that was all for the Nittany Lions as they fall in a final 17-3 to the Wildcats.

Up next

Penn State returns to Nittany Lion Softball Park at Beard Field Tuesday, April 14th. The blue and white are set to face the Bucknell Bison at 6 p.m.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse falls to No. 4 Northwestern after blowout 2nd half
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Coming off two straight wins, Penn State welcomed No. 4 Northwestern for a Sunday morning matchup.

Despite a strong showing against one of the top teams in the country, the Nittany Lions fell 17-12 to the Wildcats, losing their fourth game of the season.

While the blue and white moved back to the losing column, Youth Day brought a new attendance record to Panzer Stadium with 1,115 fans in attendance.

“I think it's a lot of credit to what we're trying to build here and people coming out and supporting us,” coach Kayla Treanor said. “We're so appreciative of all these young kids coming out and supporting us, and, you know, just trying to put on a good performance for them to enjoy.”

Northwestern took control from the faceoff without much of a fight, but an early turnover gave Penn State the ball. Following a miss by the blue and white and a save from Sydney Manning on the other end, Rocquette Allen struck first with her 38th goal of the season.

After another unsuccessful offensive drive from the Wildcats, it was Erika Ho who scored for the Nittany Lions, courtesy of Delaney Radin’s 45th assist of the year.

Northwestern’s offense couldn’t get anything going through the first half of the first quarter, missing eight straight shots. However, the Wildcats quickly tied things up with back-to-back free position goals.

After a penalty on Northwestern’s Aditi Foster, Kelly MacKinney took the lead back with her first goal of the game, assisted by Allen.

With a little over one minute left in the first quarter, Taylor Lapointe tied things up for the Wildcats to end the period.

The Nittany Lions opened the second quarter with two opportunities with a two-man-up advantage, falling short each time. Fifteen seconds after the latter of the penalties were released, Ho scored her second goal of the game to retake the lead.

That lead didn’t last long, though, as Kate Ratanaproeksa scored her fifth goal of the season to tie things up 18 seconds later.

Penn State’s offensive success continued as it retook the lead once again, this time with graduate transfer Anna Salerno’s first goal of the game, assisted again by Allen.

Following a penalty on Payton Wainman, Taylor scored again for the Wildcats, once again bringing the game to a tie. Northwestern scored its second straight goal four minutes later, taking the lead for the first time.

That lead continued to grow for the Wildcats as Hannah Rudolph scored her first goal of the game to take a 7-5 lead into halftime.

“Lots of credit to Northwestern, I thought they played really well today,” Treanor said. “Towards the end of the second, we were getting a couple stops that we kept failing to clear.”

Coming out of the second half, Northwestern scored two straight goals from Taylor, taking a 9-5 advantage. That success continued from the Wildcats with their sixth straight goal, scored halfway through the third quarter.

Taylor scored again less than a minute later, marking her fifth goal of the game.

After 17 minutes of silence from Penn State’s offense, Wainman recorded her first goal of the game, cutting the deficit to five goals.

However, that sign of life was short-lived as the Wildcats scored their 12th goal of the game, this time from Abby LoCascio. Just over a minute later, Emma Kelly fired back for the blue and white, scoring her first goal of the game.

Things quickly moved back into Northwestern’s favor with Annabel Child’s first goal of the game, giving her squad a 13-7 lead.

Coming out of the final quarter break, MacKinney scored her second goal of the game to cut the deficit to five goals. But just as Penn State found momentum, a green card on Elizabeth Anne gave the Wildcats an opportunity to score and take a six-goal lead.

That lead was once again extended with another goal from Foster, this time in a man-down situation. Two minutes later, Maddie Epke scored her first of the game, doubling the Nittany Lions’ goals in the game.

After three straight goals from the Wildcats, Radin scored her first goal of the game off a free position opportunity, cutting the deficit to 16-9.

MacKinney earned her sixth hat trick of the season as she trimmed the deficit to six goals, and Radin scored again to cut the deficit to five goals.

With 2:07 left in the game, Radin recorded her third hat trick of the year, along with her three assists on the day. However, Penn State’s offense couldn’t overcome the large deficit, falling to 11-4 on the year.

“We can't change the way we play offense because we're down,” Treanor said. “It's got to be disciplined, aggressive Penn State lacrosse, no matter what the scoreboard is.”

Injury report

Midfielder Maddie Greco went down with an injury 30 seconds into the game, but returned shortly after.

Up next

Penn State will play its final home game of the season at 6 p.m. Thursday when it hosts No. 9 Johns Hopkins for the White Out game.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Central Connecticut State transfer Jay Rodgers commits to Penn State men’s basketball
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is bringing in a guard from the transfer portal.

Central Connecticut State transfer Jay Rodgers has committed to Penn State, he announced via social media.

#commited #WeAre @PennStateMBB pic.twitter.com/KHOF1kh8ZF— jay rod (@Jayrodgers33) April 12, 2026

With the current state of the Nittany Lions’ 2026-27 roster, Rodgers slots in as the projected starting point guard as he averaged 6.9 assists per game this past year. His 206 assists led the NEC and set a single-season school record at Central Connecticut State.

As a scorer, Rodgers averaged 11.0 points per game in 2025-26 with 42.6% shooting from the field, 36.2% shooting from 3-point range and 81.7% shooting at the free-throw line. The 6-foot-3, 185-pounder was named to the All-NEC second team for his efforts.

During his portal recruitment, Rodgers took a visit to Happy Valley on the weekend of April 10-12. He’s a grad transfer with one year of eligibility left.

Rodgers has spent the last four seasons at Central Connecticut State, where he averaged 9.5 points, 2.2 rebounds and 4.3 assists per game across 68 appearances. He only played in nine games in 2023-24 due to injury before missing the entire 2024-25 season as he was recovering.

The Pickerington, Ohio, native began his career at New Orleans in 2020-21 before spending a year in JUCO at Cochise College. He’ll now get his first opportunity at a power-conference team with the Nittany Lions.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Alpha Phi Alpha celebrates return of Miss Black and Gold Scholarship pageant
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
Alumni Hall came to life on Friday as the Penn State chapter of Alpha Phi Alpha fraternity held its first Miss Black and Gold Scholarship Pageant since 2015.

The first national Miss Black and Gold pageant was held during the fraternity's 1976 General Convention in New York City, before being reinstated in 1982 following its deferment. The pageant is inspired by fraternity chapters traditionally appointing “outstanding young ladies to serve as ‘Chapter Sweethearts.’”

Gamma Nu, Penn State’s chapter, held the university’s first Miss Black and Gold pageant in 1999.

Alpha Phi Alpha member Nathaniel Cosme-Ortiz, who helped organize the pageant, said there had been several challenges that prevented the fraternity from holding the event previously, such as a lack of funds or support from alumni.

“You could spend months planning it, organizing it, but some elements just come out of nowhere,” Cosme-Ortiz, a fourth-year studying mechanical engineering, said. “This is something we were able to do because we planned almost over a year ahead and even then it took multiple tries to get to the point that we're at right now.”

Cosme-Ortiz said he hoped the pageant’s return helps to bring people together while appreciating differences in others.

“In here, each of the ladies have the opportunity to personalize themselves,” Cosme-Ortiz said. “To just give a performance for the people so that they understand that there's a bigger community here and even though we all have our distinctive personalities, if we come together, we can do a lot of change.”

Pageant contestants participated in a series of events throughout the night, showcasing their backgrounds and aspirations. Following the conclusion of all events, a panel of judges voted to crown Milan Dukes as Miss Black and Gold with Jaelee Hewitt and Bree Boyd being crowned Miss Gold and Miss 1947.

Dukes, a fourth-year studying psychology, said she saw the pageant as a way to challenge herself as well as put herself out there.

“The black community here, although it can be something that really brings us together, at some point made me feel kind of alienated at times or even just being in Penn State as a student, regardless of my race, I've felt somewhat ostracized at times,” Dukes said. “I saw (the pageant) as a way for me to put all those things to the side, to really maximize my time here in Penn State and also just allow myself to apply myself to something that I knew would challenge me.”

Dukes said she hopes to use her platform as Miss Black and Gold to both help others and as a stepping stone to what she pursues next.

“I only have about a month left until I graduate, so I'm excited to use that time to not only enrich the projects and activities that I'm in currently, but I also hope to have the opportunity to come back to help them with the next Miss Black and Gold as well as continue to use the principles behind the crown to fuel the type of work that I want to do in my community most importantly,” Dukes said. “The crown is only one stop on that journey and the lighting of the spark to that journey as well.

Kevin Guzman said he enjoyed all of the pageant events, particularly a salon performance by Dayisha Busch.

“That was very inspiring and very beautiful because I also have inspiration on being an entrepreneur of some kind and it's inspirational for those who want to be an entrepreneur, too,” Guzman, a second-year studying marketing, said.

Guzman said while he believes the pageant should continue into the future, he would prefer it not be an annual event to prevent it from becoming repetitive over time.

“It should definitely be something repeated, I would say. Not very often, but obviously it should get repeated so others can also have a place to shine,” Guzman said. “It would be best to have it after some years, not more than five, so it will have that feeling of ‘oh, his back. I want to apply. I wanna try it out, and give it a try.’”

MORE NEWS CONTENT

title: 3-star LB Blake Betton commits to Penn State
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
“LBU” has a new addition.

Penn State has earned a commitment from 3-star 2027 linebacker Blake Betton, he confirmed on Saturday via X.

Betton was in State College at the end of January for Penn State’s second Junior Day, and seemingly came away with a great impression.

The Shakopee, Minnesota, native is rated as the No. 417 player nationally, No. 35 linebacker and No. 4 overall player in Minnesota per the 247Sports Composite rankings.

Betton was offered on Dec. 14, but previously held an offer from Iowa State, so the familiarity with the coaching staff likely played a role in his decision.

He chose Penn State over Wisconsin, Michigan, Minnesota and Missouri.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball falls to Ohio State on the road despite comeback attempt, tying up series
author: Zach Schroder | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State Baseball looked to continue its success after securing a victory in its first matchup in a three-game series versus Ohio State. The team has not been strong on the road or against conference opponents. Ultimately, the Nittany Lions lost 3-2 to the Buckeyes.

Righty pitcher Chris Domke started on the mound for the Buckeyes. Jayden Davis topped the order for the Nittany. Michael Anderson and Davis made for two quick outs and Bryce Molinaro singled to get the hitting started. Spencer Barnett lined out to center to flip the field.

On the mound first for Penn State was Ben Hudson, a right-handed pitcher who transferred from West Virginia. Alex Bemis was walked on a full count and Henry Kaczmar was hit by a pitch, leading to a double steal off a bunt from Noah Furcht. Furcht was then also hit by a pitch. Dane Harvey hit a sacrifice fly for Bemis for the first score of the game. Maddix Simpson grounded out to end the inaugural inning. Ohio State led 1-0.

Jack Porter singled to center field for the Nittany Lions' second hit of the game. Kevin Karstetter popped out and Avery Smith lined out to left field for two quick outs. Maddox McDonald struck out looking, stranding a runner for the second inning in a row.

Hudson recovered from his rough first inning. CJ Reid flew out to right field, Lee Ellis and Miles Vandenheuvel both grounded to third, with Barnett making great plays to first. A quick second inning to start the game, Penn State still trailed 1-0.

Preston Yaucher grounded out to shortstop with a nice play on first from Harvey. Back to the top of the order, Davis was walked on four straight balls from Domke, his first for the day. Anderson and Molinaro both flew out to right and left field consecutively. A third-stranded runner for the Nittany Lions.

Bemis got the first hit for the Buckeyes with a single to center field, but got caught going to second on a fielder's choice from Kaczmar, who advanced to second on a wild pitch. Furcht hit a double, which allowed Kaczmar to get home and Mason Eckelman grounded out. Furcht got to third. Harvey was hit by a pitch, Hudson’s third batter hit for the game and Simpson struck out swinging to end the third. Penn State down 2-0.

The Nittany Lions offense improved slightly, stranding two runners this inning. Karstetter was hit by a pitch and made it to second off a single from Smith, though McDonald struck out swinging to end the batting. Penn State made quick work on defense. Reid fouled and Ellis grounded out. Vandenheuval struck out looking.

Yaucher flew out a foot away from outside the park. Davis reached first for his second time in a row but got caught in a double play from an Anderson groundout. Kaczmar reached base for the third time without a hit. Hudson finished his day on the mound after Furcht flew out. Righty Mason Butash replaced him after 79 pitches. Kaczmar got caught stealing second to end the inning.

Molinaro walked to start the 6th, Barnett singled and Molinaro got to third. Porter hit a sacrifice fly that led to Penn State's first run of the game by Molinaro. Smith singled, followed by a McDonald single off a bunt. With bases loaded, the Buckeyes swapped Domke for Nick Sawyer on the mound. Sawyer struck out Yaucher swinging. Penn State now trailed 2-1.

Eckelman lined out to shortstop, and Harvey grounded out to third. Simpson singled to left field, but Reid fouled out to end the 6th. The Nittany Lions fell back on their offensive struggles. Davis lined out, Anderson grounded out, and Molinaro struck out swinging.

The Buckeyes followed Penn State's lead with a quick three-and-out for their batters. Ellis and Vandenheuvel both flew out and Bemis grounded out. A very fast seventh frame, with an unchanged score.

A pitching change started the 8th, Sawyer came out and righty Ryan Zamora came in. Zamora made quick work of Barnett and Porter. Karstetter singled to right field. Smith struck out swinging to end the penultimate at-bat for Penn State.

Kaczmar walked and advanced to third after a single from Furcht and a sacrifice bunt from Grant Mangrum. Harvey was intentionally walked and Butash was swapped for DeMell. Simpson hit a sacrifice fly for a Kaczmar score. DeMell struck out Ellis, looking for minimal damage. Ohio State led 3-1.

Justin Turcovski came in to pinch hit for McDonald, but grounded out. Yaucher was walked and scored off a Davis double to left center. Davis advanced to third on a wild pitch. Molinaro struck out swinging to end the Nittany Lions' chances.

Up next

Penn State will conclude its away series in Columbus tomorrow at 1 p.m.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Late offense not enough as No. 8 Penn State men's lacrosse loses Senior Day meeting with Michigan
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
Early in Penn State’s Saturday clash with Michigan, star attackman Hunter Aquino cut in front of the Wolverine crease, caught a quick feed from midfielder Andrew Beard, and flicked the ball into the nylon behind the Michigan netminder.

Aquino, who was playing his first minutes since the Nittany Lions’ February 28 blowout victory over Yale, pumped his fist in the air and was engulfed by his teammates after scoring the game’s opening goal. The crowd at Panzer Stadium erupted, not aware that it would be a long time until they’d see another score from the blue and white.

“You’ve got to give Michigan’s defense credit,” coach Jeff Tambroni said. “They were on our gloves, making the shots more challenging, and I thought (Taylor) responded really well behind them.”

The unit that, entering Saturday, had allowed the most goals in the Big Ten held the usually explosive Penn State offense to just two first-half goals, taking a 5-2 advantage into halftime.

“I don’t think we played that hard and together,” Tambroni said. “That was across the board. Michigan slid a lot early, probably took us by a little bit of surprise, and I don’t think we reacted well. I felt like they were a half a step ahead.”

In addition to the strong defense from their poles, the Wolverines got an uncharacteristically positive performance from goaltender Hunter Taylor, who posted a save percentage of just .487 before the meeting with Penn State.

The 6-foot-1 senior made 11 saves to shut down a Nittany Lion attack that previously led the conference in goals per game and shooting percentage. The latter mark in Saturday’s meeting finished at just 25% for the Nittany Lions, whose eight goals are tied for the lowest total on the season, matching their March 14 loss to North Carolina.

However, a late surge nearly flipped the script for the Nittany Lions. A transition goal from defensive midfielder Jon King electrified the fans in Happy Valley, before a man-up goal by Michael Faraone brought the blue and white within one at the end of the third quarter.

A high-flying crease dive by Luke Walstrum and a heater from Aquino sparked a three-goal run that put Penn State in the lead with under ten minutes remaining in the contest. Aquino’s shot signified a return to form for the reigning Big Ten Freshman of the Year, but Tambroni noted that the Easton, Pennsylvania native still has a lot of work to do.

“He’s missed six weeks of competitive lacrosse,” Tambroni said. “I thought he did a nice job, but it does take some time to get him back into what a game feels like. I know he’ll continue to grow as the days and weeks go on.”

Leading 8-6 in the dying moments of the final frame, goaltender Preston Hawkins and the Nittany Lion defense couldn’t hold on to the late lead, surrendering two goals to knot the score at 8-8 before Luke Shannehan sealed the Michigan victory with 13 seconds left on the clock.

Following his standout performance against Maryland, Hawkins made seven saves on Saturday but posted just a .438 save percentage.

“I thought it was unfortunate,” Tambroni said, “Especially late, most of those goals were right on top of him. They’ve got some really good shooters, and as a freshman, he’s still learning, so I give him credit.”

After giving up a significant lead late in last week’s loss to Johns Hopkins, Saturday’s meeting with the Wolverines marked the second straight game in which Penn State lost a game to a lower-ranked team where they held a fourth-quarter lead.

With a season-closing ranked matchup with Rutgers next week and postseason play on the horizon beyond, Tambroni’s squad will look to be more consistent in the late stages of the campaign.

“I feel like we’ve done a pretty good job in our shooting efforts throughout the course of the year,” Tambroni said. “These last two games were a little bit outside the box, and we’ve got to figure it out here before we get to Rutgers next Friday.”

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: No. 15 Penn State men’s volleyball escapes with five-set victory over Sacred Heart
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State and Sacred Heart returned to the court in Fairfield, Connecticut, following a five-set thriller on Friday night.

The Nittany Lions pulled out another hard-fought victory, coming back from 2-1 for the second straight day to defeat the Pioneers in five sets.

Outside hitter Sean Harvey kicked things off for the blue and white with a kill to put the first point on the board. The teams traded points, keeping it even until the Pioneers went on a 4-0 run, fueled by multiple Penn State errors.

The Pioneers held onto the early lead, going on a 3-0 run to expand the lead. Kills from Zach Flynn and Jaron Popp doubled the advantage, giving Sacred Heart a 14-7 lead.

The deficit was too much for the Nittany Lions in the opening set, as three straight attack errors from Tristan Hassell and Harvey gave the Pioneers the first set victory. Sacred Heart took the opening set 25-15, giving Penn State a lot to work for in the next frame.

The key to the lead was attack errors; the Pioneers forced nine on the blue and white, giving away those free points.

The second set started the same, as neither team found a significant advantage. The Nittany Lions took their first multi-point advantage after the blocking unit forced multiple errors.

Two double blocks, both with the NCAA’s block per set leader, Owen Rose, as a part of it, gave the blue and white a 10-6 lead.

Sacred Heart didn’t go away without a fight, as Aleksa Mandic put down back-to-back service aces to pull his team to a tie with the No. 15 team in the country.

With a change at setter for the blue and white — switching from Michael Schwob to Jaxon Herr — the offense started to flow more. A kill from Hassell before Herr tallied a service ace gave Penn State an 18-15 advantage.

That run continued through a Sacred Heart timeout with the blue and white’s blocking unit coming alive again, forcing multiple attack errors and a block, as well.

A double block from Hassell and Rose and a setting mishap gave the blue and white the response it needed. The Nittany Lions finished off the set with a 25-16 victory, tying the match back up at 1-1.

Through two sets, Hassell led the way for Penn State. His seven kills paced the team with a .308 hitting percentage. Outside hitter Bennett Wilson was the most efficient with six kills on a .625 percentage.

The third set continued the hard-fought battle between the two teams.

The teams traded points to begin the set before Penn State earned the first run for the second consecutive set.

Harvey notched two straight kills for the blue and white, giving it a 10-7 advantage early on. However, a 4-1 run from the Pioneers knotted things at 14-14 behind two kills from Mandic.

While neither team escaped the close set, Penn State took a 22-20 lead off an attack error. Harvey notched a kill to gain another two-point advantage, but Sacred Heart didn’t go away.

A double block from the front line of the Pioneers gave them a 25-24 lead and set point with a chance to take a 2-1 lead. However, Harvey quickly shut that down, but two straight points off kills from Mandic gave Sacred Heart the 27-25 win.

Just like Friday, Penn State was in a 2-1 hole, needing to be almost perfect to avoid an upset. However, the fourth set began with the momentum on the side of the home team.

An early 5-1 run to start the set put all the energy on the side of the Pioneers. While Penn State attempted to battle back in the set, the hitting from Mandic and Jack Krausz kept Sacred Heart at a comfortable lead.

Harvey put the team on his back late in the set with two kills and an ace, pulling the deficit closer. The Nittany Lions pulled ahead with a triple block, giving them a 23-22 lead.

Sacred Heart fought back with multiple points, giving it match point. Despite this, a Harvey kill and an attack error gave Penn State a 27-26 lead.

The blue and white survived the fourth set behind a triple block to end it, 28-26. The poise of the team was on showcase for the second straight day, as it sent the match to a fifth set again.

The final set was just like the other four. Both teams alternated points, with neither team gaining an advantage. Back-to-back kills from Harvey and Hassell gave Penn State a 10-7 lead, needing just five points to escape again.

Leading 12-8, Rose notched a solo block and just two points later, Wilson rose to the top to power the ball down for match point.

A double block ended the match, coming from the high-flying hands of Gage Gabriel and Hassell. For the second straight day, the Nittany Lions escaped in Connecticut.

Up next

Penn State will return to Rec Hall for the final series of the regular season. The Nittany Lions will take on George Mason with the first match beginning at 6 p.m. Friday. They’ll return to the court at 3 p.m. Saturday to close out the season.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball suffers run-rule defeat in second game of Northwestern series
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
Following Penn State’s first loss of the series against Northwestern. The Nittany Lions returned to Nittany Lion Softball Park at Beard Field to host their Family and Friends appreciation game and Bark in the Park game. For Saturday’s game, the blue and white turned to pink and black.

Pink and black suffered their second consecutive loss of the series with a 13-4 loss.

Coach Clarrisa Crowell made a few adjustments for Saturday’s game. Giving freshman Allison Oneacre the start at third, then adding freshman Kalli Karwowski to the lineup, giving her the shortstop slot.

Junior righty Bridget Nemeth got the start in the circle. Northwestern continued its momentum from Friday's 8-2 victory. Getting bases loaded with only one out, a wild pitch from Nemeth gave the Wildcats the first score of the game.

Senior Bridget Donahey singled to left field, which gave the second run of the game, then Nemeth hit a Wildcat batter. With bases loaded once again at the top of the first, Nemeth was able to secure her first strikeout of the game to close things off before more damage from Northwestern; the Nittany Lions trailed 2-0.

For the Wildcats, they gave junior right-hander Riley Grudzielanek the start. Nothing good for Penn State in the bottom of the first, a quick 1-2-3 inning by Grudzielanek, getting her first strikeout of the game.

The top of the second was a tough one for Penn State; errors from the defense gave the Wildcats two more runs on the board.

Freshman Gabby Gradishar started things in the bottom of the second by getting walked. A wild pitch got Gradishar on second base, sophomore Natalie Lieto and freshman Breanna Hanik were walked as well.

With bases loaded and no outs, junior Michela Barbenante stepped into the batter's box and got a single straight to Northwestern's shortstop. Getting their first run on the scoreboard, junior Jordan George was also walked by Grudzielanek.

The Wildcats made a change in the circle, giving right-handed Signe Dohse an inning with bases loaded and only one out. Nemeth stepped into the batter's box and walked again, giving the Nittany Lions their second tally of the game.

Another walk and a hit by a pitch by Dohse gave Penn State another run on the board, and the Wildcats were able to close out the inning. Northwestern up 4-3.

As the Nittany Lions' defense came out for the top of the third, junior Brooke Klosowicz was on third and Oneacre moved to shortstop. The pink and black gave up three runs, a 7-3 lead for the Wildcats.

Only a single from Hanik in the bottom of the third for Penn State.

Senior Kansas Robinson started things off for Northwestern with a double to right-center. Then Donahey continued the momentum with another double. An error from the outfield was the second drop ball by the Nittany Lions’ defense, giving the Wildcats two more runs on the scoreboard.

The pink and black were able to close out the top of the fourth, trailing 9-3.

In the bottom of the fourth, Penn State looked good as three batters drew walks, a run scored and the Wildcats turned to sophomore righty Emma Blea. The Nittany Lions left the three runners on base as the Northwestern defense was able to close out the inning before any more damage, 9-4.

Freshman righty Mackenzie Duncan entered the circle at the top of the fifth. She surrendered her first walk of the game to the top Northwestern order. Multiple errors from the pink and black defense gave the Wildcats a 13-4 lead.

The Northwestern defense was able to close out the game, winning 13-4 in five innings.

Up next

Penn State faces the Northwestern Wildcats for its third and final game of the series. The first pitch is set for Sunday at 11 a.m. at Nittany Lion Softball Park at Beard Field.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State football draft profile | Perfection the standard for top-3 LS prospect Tyler Duzansky
author: Avery Hill | Football Editor
description:
Long snapper Tyler Duzansky could be weeks away from a dream come true.

Duzansky capped his five-year career by becoming a finalist for the Patrick Mannelly Award. He got three years of starting experience at Penn State, and his efforts were rewarded by an invite to the 2026 NFL Combine Specialist Showcase in Indianapolis.

“I was really happy that I was able to go there, one, and that I was able to perform at a high level,” Duzansky said.

Duzansky was also a participant at Penn State Pro Day, particularly impressing with 20 reps on the bench press.

If he’s drafted or picked up by a team, he’ll follow in the footsteps of his predecessor, Seattle Seahawks snapper Chris Stoll, who won a Super Bowl with the team in February. The two remain close, as Duzansky typically sends the snapper some film and bounces ideas off of him as he tries to leverage a career in the NFL.

But the draft preparation as a long snapper is a lot simpler than other positions.

Simply put, your job is to be perfect — or else.

“Just as, like a specialist, and as somebody with a position where you only get to go out there like 10 or 15 times, you definitely have to be a low-maintenance, high-production guy,” Duzansky said of his conversations with teams. “Like as a snapper, if you mess up one time out of 10, you're getting cut.”

Metrics

Height: 6-foot-3

Weight: 234 pounds

Bench press: 20 reps

Broad jump: 9-foot-7

40-yard dash: 4.97 seconds

Projections

Projected round drafted: 6th round-undrafted

Player comparison: Joe Cardona

Scouting report

Duzansky has all of the physical tools of your modern-day snapper, which could be considered to be a hybrid linebacker build, in comparison to what used to be a position for former linemen.

His 4.97-second 40-yard dash is just beneath Los Angeles Rams long snapper Joe Cardona, who clocked a 4.91. Additionally, relative to your typical snapper, the two are comparable in strength, as Cardona notched a remarkable 30 reps on the bench press in 2015.

Cardona makes his mark as not just an accurate snapper, but a quality player in punt coverage, and Duzansky’s experience gives him all the tools to do so as well.

Notably, Duzansky notched a season-best in tackles at six in 2023. For comparison, top snapper prospects in BYU’s Garrison Grimes and Oregon’s Luke Basso have never eclipsed more than three tackles in a season.

But a snapper’s skillset can also be measured by the success of his kicker and punter. 2025 is the best case for his resume, as kicker Ryan Barker went 18-of-19 on field goals and 44-of-45 on extra points. Fellow draft prospect and punter Gabe Nwosu also had a big year with a 46-yard punt average and a career-high 68-yard boot.

Best team fit: Philadelphia Eagles

A long snapper’s stock in the NFL draft varies based on need and special teams coaches. But for historical’s sake, Duzansky may not want to hear his phone ring until after the draft concludes.

There have been three snappers drafted in 2021, with the first two, Camaron Cheeseman and Thomas Fletcher, not lasting more than three seasons in the NFL.

The most recent, New England Patriots snapper Julian Ashby, was selected in the seventh round of the 2025 draft, but New England signed defensive end-turned long snapper Niko Lalos after the season, so Ashby could be stuck in a position battle over the summer.

But even as an undrafted player, Duzansky should be one of the first snappers called. ESPN’s Mel Kiper lists him as the No. 2 snapper in the draft, while Field Yates slots him in at No. 3.

A team calling him could be the Philadelphia Eagles, as the squad currently sits snapper-less and reportedly invited him to local pro day.

Eagles special teams coach Michael Clay has been with the team since 2021. He inherited long-time Eagles snapper Rick Lovato, who measured up similarly to Duzansky during his pro day in 2015, but ran a 5.17 40-yard dash.

But Lovato wasn’t extended for the 2025 season, and his successor, 35-year-old Charley Hughlett, is no longer listed on the roster. It’s likely Clay wants to get younger and lighter at the snapper position, so Duzansky could be one of the first names he calls.

Otherwise, an opportunity exists for Duzansky to replace Cardona on his previous team, the Miami Dolphins. Miami has signed Taybor Pepper, who spent 2025 out of the NFL.

There’s also a sort-of dream scenario, where Duzansky is signed or drafted by the Tennessee Titans, likely replacing five-time Pro Bowl snapper Morgan Cox, who will turn 40 the day after the draft.

title: No. 8 Penn State men’s lacrosse falls to Michigan on Senior Day despite fourth-quarter comeback bid
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 8 Penn State returned to Panzer Stadium for the first time since beating then-No. 11 Maryland in the White Out game, following an 11-10 road defeat to then-No. 16 Johns Hopkins. Families and friends of the blue and white gathered to watch the Nittany Lions face Michigan Saturday at 1 p.m. for the team’s annual Senior Day game.

Penn State struggled on offense for the first three-quarters, and despite a fourth-quarter surge, the Nittany Lions fell, 9-8, to the Wolverines, falling to 6-5 overall and 2-2 in the Big Ten.

"I thought (goaltender Hunter Taylor) played really well,” coach Jeff Tambroni said. “It's two weeks in a row that the goalie really got in our heads a little bit in terms of the way we were shooting.”

First half

Hunter Aquino wasted no time getting back on the scoreboard in his first game back from injury. He received a pass from Andrew Beard on the doorstep and beat Taylor to open the scoring four minutes into the game.

After Jon King gained possession from an out-of-bounds play, the Wolverines hounded Alex Ross on the ride and won a 3-on-1 on Patrick Keenan. Alex Lobel shot underhand past goaltender Preston Hawkins to tie the game with 7:51 remaining in the quarter.

Michigan carried the momentum into their next possession as Alex Gatto came over the top of the box and fired a shot-arm shot under Hawkins’ stick to take a 2-1 lead.

Out of the timeout, Bo Lockwood found the bottom-right corner of the cage on a long-range overhand shot, taking a two-goal advantage with less than five minutes on the clock.

Liam Matthews cut Penn State’s deficit to one with 13:24 remaining in the opening half, taking a pass from Luke Walstrum on the crease for his 12th goal of the season.

Because both teams struggled to effectively clear due to trading shots at each other’s open nets — caused by mirrored 10-man ride formations — scoring slowed considerably.

However, at the eight-minute mark, the Wolverines broke through. Nick Roode was left all alone in front of the crease for a routine strike before Josh Ford scored in transition to take a 5-2 lead.

At the end of the first half, Taylor repeatedly shut down Penn State’s offense. Jack Iannantuono rounded the cage and had the best opportunity to bring energy back to his team. Taylor sealed off his five-hole with his stick and came up with the save. Taylor made seven saves on nine shots in the first half.

“I’ve just got to give Michigan's defense credit,” Tambroni said. “They were on our gloves, making the shots more challenging and I thought (Taylor) responded really well behind them.”

Second half

Penn State’s attacking woes continued in the third quarter, as Michigan’s pressure and relentless ability to lure the blue and white into double or even triple-coverage caused the Nittany Lions to scramble on offense, miss passes and give up possession.

Similarly, Penn State’s defense and goaltending largely held up. Hawkins came up with key stops to maintain the three-goal difference.

Then, King got the ball in transition with under two minutes remaining in the quarter. The captain sprinted down the center of the box and delivered a blistering shot to cut the lead to two, sparking an eruption among the Penn State faithful at Panzer Stadium.

“I was proud of the way that our guys responded (to King’s goal),” Tambroni said. “We shot the ball with much more confidence later in the game, especially in that fourth quarter. I feel like we've done a pretty good job in our shooting efforts throughout the course of the year, and we got to figure it out here before we get into Rutgers next Friday."

Immediately afterward, the Wolverines took a pair of 30-second penalties. Eliot Dubick was caught for delay of game while Michigan’s bench was flagged for illegal conduct, giving the Nittany Lions a two-man advantage.

Michael Faraone scored on the man-up with a heavy side-arm shot to bring Penn State within seeing distance of a tie heading into the fourth quarter.

Walstrum tied the game 35 seconds into the final frame, diving into the crease but scoring before he landed on the turf. However, a blown coverage on the other end allowed Roode to regain the lead.

Aquino answered with a missile from long-range, striking the top left corner past Taylor to tie the game, 6-6. Chase Robertson quickly followed with a goal of his own to take a 7-6 lead for the first time since the opening tally, assisted by Faraone.

Walstrum’s day continued to improve; he scored a close-range goal with 9:22 remaining in the game to extend Penn State’s lead to two, its largest gap of the game. Three minutes later, Roode secured a hat trick in the game on a wide-open streaking shot, now a one-goal game.

After a tug-of-war for possession, the Roode tied the game for the Wolverines with 1:43 remaining in regulation. Then, with 12.3 seconds remaining in the game, Luke Shannehan dealt the dagger, seizing victory over Penn State.

“It was unfortunate, especially in that second half, most of those goals were right on top of (Hawkins) and Michigan’s got some good shooters,” Tambroni said. “I don't know if we're ever going to get over the hump with just Preston. He's also going to need help from his defense and seniors and the veterans.”

Up Next

Penn State is set to travel to Piscataway, New Jersey, for its regular-season finale against No. 19 Rutgers at 8 p.m. on Friday.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State women's basketball assistant coach Natisha Hiedeman signs with Seattle Storm
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Natisha Hiedeman's professional career is taking another turn.

Hiedeman announced on Saturday that she signed with the Seattle Storm of the WNBA, marking her third team in eight seasons.

It’s T time. ⛈️The Seattle Storm has signed guard Natisha Hiedeman. pic.twitter.com/EKF2QsLoBK— Seattle Storm (@seattlestorm) April 11, 2026

The Marquette native played under former Penn State coach Carolyn Kieger during her collegiate career, finishing as the Golden Eagles' third-highest scorer of all time.

After graduating, she was drafted 18th overall to the Minnesota Lynx, before being traded back and forth with the Connecticut Sun and Atlanta Dream.

Hiedeman played five seasons with the Sun before joining the Lynx, where she spent the last two years. In her first year in Minnesota, Hiedeman and the Lynx fell to the New York Liberty in the WNBA finals.

In 2023, Hiedeman was hired as the director of player development for the Lady Lions before becoming an assistant coach the following year.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State women’s basketball freshman Rachael Okokoh transfers to Ole Miss
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After just one year at Penn State, Rachael Okokoh has found a new home.

Okokoh announced her transfer to Ole Miss on Saturday, becoming the third former Lady Lion to commit to a new school.

Ready to have Rachael at the Sip! 🔵🔴#HottyToddy x #NoCeilings pic.twitter.com/TWUpUDicPX— Ole Miss Women's BB (@OleMissWBB) April 11, 2026

Okokoh entered the transfer portal on March 31 after the hiring of new head coach Tanisha Wright in early March.

With the Lady Lions, Okokoh played a smaller role during her freshman year, playing in 25 games with 1.1 points and 1.6 rebounds per game.

In Year 2, Okokoh will suit up for coach Yolett McPhee-McCuin and the Rebels, who are coming off a 24-12 record last season with a second-round appearance in the NCAA Tournament.

title: Former Penn State women’s basketball guard Shayla Smith transfers to Temple
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After an underwhelming freshman season at Penn State, Shayla Smith decided to take her talents elsewhere.

Smith committed to Temple on Saturday, returning home where she grew up.

The Philadelphia native entered her freshman year as the highest-scoring player in Philadelphia high school history, but saw just two starts with 9.9 points per game.

Smith averaged 2.6 points and 2.6 rebounds per game as she looks to increase her playing time with the Owls, who are coming off a 15-17 record last year.
