title: Penn State men's volleyball survives upset threat, defeats Sacred Heart in 5 sets
author: Liana Tiamzon | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State arrived at the William H. Pitt Athletic & Convocation Center to battle it out against Sacred Heart. Followed by their dominant five straight sweeps against Charleston, Ohio State and NJIT, the Nittany Lions were setting up and ready for more EIVA play.

The Nittany Lions came into the match with momentum, but it didn’t come easy. Penn State set the tone early, yet Sacred Heart kept it close throughout. It turned into a hard-fought, five-set battle to keep their winning streak.

Penn State got on the board early after an attack error by Sacred Heart’s Aaron Johnson, the blue and white took a 5-0 lead and forced an early Pioneer timeout. A service ace sparked the run before Matthew Luoma and Sean Harvey, both set up by Michael Schwob, each notched kills to extend the advantage.

Sacred Heart continued to struggle early, falling 7-0 after another attack error by Zach Flynn. Owen Rose, who’s ranked No. 1 in blocks per set in the NCAA, recorded back-to-back kills off assists from Schwob and kept the Nittany Lions ahead.

This cushion did not cause the Pioneers to wither, as they made a push late in the set, they cut into the deficit behind a kill from Beranek and a service ace, forcing a Penn State timeout. Penn State held onto a 16-14 lead.

The Pioneers made one final push late in the set, closing the gap 23-21 after a Flynn kill and forcing another Penn State timeout. The Nittany Lions sealed the first set with authority as Harvey delivered a service ace to make it 25-21 before they finished it off with a kill.

Sacred Heart came out stronger in the second set, and it kept things tight early and even. The teams traded points back and forth, kills from Harvery and Bennett Wilson, while Beranek countered with points of his own, giving the Pioneers a 10-8 lead, causing an early Penn State timeout.

Sacred Heart pulled ahead midway through the set and built a 14-10 lead behind strong offense and a service ace, while Penn State tried to stay close with a kill from Owen Rose.

It was a back-and-forth set between the Nittany Lions and the Pioneers, with Penn State not being able to get ahead at all midway through the second set. The Pioneers extended their lead every chance they got, 22-18, forcing the Nittany Lions to another timeout.

Sacred Heart closed out the second set with a successful late push, securing it 25-22 behind a kill from Popp. Penn State made a brief run with back-to-back service aces from Rose, but the comeback fell short as the Pioneers held on to even the match.

Penn State bounced back strong to open the next set; they jumped out to a 6-2 lead behind early control at the net. Harvey led the charge with multiple kills as the Nittany set the tone early and Sacred Heart struggled to keep up this early into the set.

Despite a brief Sacred Heart run that included back-to-back service aces, kills from Gabriel, Luoma and Harvey helped the Nittany Lions stay in control 18-16.

The Pioneers took late control of the set, pulled ahead 24-21 behind a strong play by Mandic. Penn State answered with a block from Luoma and Rose and a kill from Harvey, but the Pioneers kept the momentum to stay in front.

Sacred Heart closed out the set with a 25-21 win, and finished the run behind a kill from Beranek and a service ace. Penn State couldn’t recover late and fell short.

​​

The fourth set didn’t slow both teams down, as it stayed tight early on with both teams trading points and neither able to create separation. The Pioneers stayed to keep the lead 10-8.

Sacred Heart held a slight edge midway through the set, but a pair of service errors helped Penn State chip away at their deficit. They stayed within reach despite a kill from Breanek.

Penn State battled back to tie the set 17-17 behind a strong stretch from Harvey, who delivered multiple kills and sparked the run for the Nittany Lions and forced a Sacred Heart timeout.

The Nittany Lions kept their audience on their toes and pulled away late behind a dominant run from Harvey. He went back-to-back again with service aces before Wilson added a kill to extend their lead 24-18.

Harvey capped the stretch with another kill off a set from Schwob and kept Penn State in control; they won the fourth set 25-19.

The match headed to a fifth set after both teams battled through four tightly contested sets. Rose, who recorded multiple kills, earned the first kill for the Nittany Lions. The Pioneers stayed right there behind Beranek and Flynn, and kept the set even at 6-6 as neither side lost their A game, both teams trading a lead.

Penn State finally created a separation midway through the fifth set, building an 11-8 lead behind the net. Rose and Luoma combined for key blocks. Luoma added two kills to keep the Nittany Lions in front and grew the lead even further, creating a five-point advantage with a Luoma ace, ending the match in the Nittany Lions’ favor.

Up Next

Penn State faces Sacred Heart again, and the Pioneers and Nittany Lions return to the court at 3 p.m. Saturday.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball’s bats go cold in series-opening loss to Northwestern
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State entered its Friday night matchup against Northwestern with a lot of momentum on its side, having swept its last two Big Ten series. The Nittany Lions had been playing with lots of consistency, largely being led to victory through strong and productive performances at the plate.

Penn State was overwhelmed and outpaced in the series opener, falling to Northwestern 8-2.

In its last six conference games against Rutgers and Maryland, the blue and white flashed its offensive prowess by scoring at least seven runs in all but one of those contests. However, the Penn State bats went cold on Friday night, and the Nittany Lions were ultimately no match for Wildcats ace Marina Mason.

“When we were effective in the box, we were swinging at pitches in the zone,” coach Clarisa Crowell said. “When she (Mason) was effective we were chasing balls out of the zone, whether it was a rise ball or offspeed.”

As a result of Mason’s command, the blue and white struck out 11 times and only managed to produce two hits against the Northwestern right-hander, and couldn’t find any rhythm or consistency in the box.

Penn State didn’t find a breakthrough against Mason until the fifth inning when sophomore center fielder Natalie Lieto launched a solo home run for her eighth of the season. The Monroe, Connecticut, native’s long ball kept the Nittany Lions from being run-ruled after they previously trailed 8-0 before the home run. The shot over the center field fence also extended Lieto’s on-base streak to 11 games.

The blue and white’s only other hit of the night also came in the fifth inning, when sophomore Macy Chamberlin ripped a one-out single down the left field line.

“We just have to do a better job of barreling up on pitches that are in the zone,” Crowell said. “And I thought we did a better job of that in the middle of the game.”

The fifth and sixth innings were the most productive for Penn State as the Nittany Lions managed to work walks against Mason, but ultimately left three runners on base between the two frames.

Notably, the Nittany Lions' top two bats, junior utility player Brooke Klosowicz and freshman infielder Allison Oneacre, were uncharacteristically silent in Friday night's outing.

This marked just the third time all season that both Klosowicz and Oneacre went without a hit in the same game. The only other two occurrences were on Feb. 8 against Auburn and March 13 in Penn State’s Big Ten opener at Oregon.

Mason accounts for 96.2 percent of Northwestern’s total 231.2 innings pitched, and has appeared in at least two games of every Big Ten series for the Wildcats this season. Given this, it's very likely that Penn State will have to face off against the freshman at least once more this weekend.

This means that there’s time for Penn State to make needed adjustments, even with a quick turnaround to Saturday's 2 p.m. contest.

“We had our opportunities, I thought we had some pretty quality at-bats,” Crowell said. “I know we’ll be ready to go tomorrow.”

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's hockey star freshman Gavin McKenna earns All-American honors
author: Chase Fisher | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has an All-American.

Nittany Lions forward Gavin McKenna was named a CCM/AHCA Second Team West All-American on Friday, which was announced during the Hobey Baker Award ceremony in Las Vegas.

🚨 ALL-AMERICAN ALERT 🚨Freshman Gavin McKenna becomes the fourth Nittany Lion all-time, first freshman, to earn All-American status‼️McKenna joins Aiden Fink (2025), Cole Hults (2020) and Nate Sucese (2020) as Penn State All-Americans. 😤#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/m1ZhYt2oHN— Penn State Men’s Hockey (@PennStateMHKY) April 11, 2026

McKenna finished second in the country in points per game with 1.46, while becoming the first Penn State player to win the Big Ten scoring title, amassing 38 of his 51 points in conference play.

The freshman ended his campaign first in the Big Ten and second in the nation in points among rookies. He was also a two-time National Rookie of the Month and set nine different program records, most notably points per game.

The 18-year-old joins former Penn State skaters Aiden Fink, Cole Hults and Nate Sucese to earn All-American honors. McKenna is the top-rated North American skater in the 2026 NHL Entry Draft, per nhl.com, and will likely be the first-overall pick in June.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball falls in series opener against Northwestern behind sluggish offense
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
After sweeping Maryland last weekend, Penn State stayed home and looked to carry its positive momentum into the series opener against Northwestern. Unlike most games this season for the Nittany Lions, there wasn’t much scoring in the first few innings.

Penn State couldn’t produce much offense and suffered a blowout 8-2 in the series opener to Northwestern.

“We’ve got to have a better plan against them, and we’ve got to make some better pitches, like some of the balls,” coach Clarisa Crowell said. “We limited the damage at the bottom of the order. The goal is always to contain the top, and we didn’t.”

Neither team recorded a hit through the first two frames as right-hander McKenna Young shut down the Wildcats’ lineup and fellow right-hander Marina Mason swiftly retired the Nittany Lions’ batters. However, Northwestern broke through in the third and plated five runs.

Young hit leadoff Tru Medina, and Grace Nieto tapped a bunt back to the mound and reached base safely. Sensing some offensive momentum, Kaylie Avvisato launched a three-run dinger to right center and gave the Wildcats a 3-0 lead.

The pressure mounted for Young when Emma Raye knocked a single through the left side and then quickly stole second. A few batters later, Young was one out away from limiting Northwestern to just three runs in the frame, but she extended the inning and walked Kelsey Nader.

Nader stole second and distracted catcher Madison Seidel from Raye, who stole home at the same time, extending the Wildcats’ lead 4-0. The bleeding didn’t stop there as Isabel Cunnea roped an RBI single into center and put Northwestern ahead 5-0.

Crowell turned to the bullpen and brought in left-hander Brevyn Kellepouris to stop the damage. The freshman successfully retired the next Wildcat batter on a flyout, but Penn State now faced a steep five-run deficit.

Despite Northwestern’s offense waking up, the blue and white still struggled to crack Mason as the Nittany Lions were still held hitless through three innings.

“I think when we were effective in the box, we were swinging at pitches in the zone,” Crowell said. “When she was effective, we were chasing balls out of the zone, whether it was a rise ball or off-speed. So, I think we have to just do a better job of barreling up on pitches that are in the zone.”

While Penn State couldn’t cut into its hole, the Wildcats went right back to work in the fourth and tacked on three more runs.

Nieto tapped a perfect bunt for an infield single, and then Avvisato hit a routine grounder, but first baseman Gabby Gradishar committed an error, allowing another runner to reach. The long ball propelled Northwestern’s offense all game, and it came through again.

Raye powered a three-run home run over the center field wall to put the Wildcats out of striking distance, 8-0.

“We just let one pitch get away from us, and then after that, she really settled down,” Crowell said. “So, I thought her stuff was really good.

The Nittany Lions were still held hitless until the fifth when their offense finally got going.

Center fielder Natalie Lieto smothered a solo homer to right center and put Penn State on the board 8-1. Frankie Flesher also walked, and Macy Chamberlin singled in the frame, but the Nittany Lions left them stranded, keeping the score at 8-1.

Kellepouris ran into a little trouble in the sixth. Nieto knocked a leadoff single to right and Raye pulled a double down the left field line with just one out. However, Bridget Donahey lined into an inning-ending double play and Kellepouris escaped the jam.

The Nittany Lions plated another run in the sixth to make it 8-2, but couldn’t rally in the seventh and finished with just two hits. Penn State dropped to 26-13 on the season with the loss.

Up Next

Penn State faces Northwestern back at Beard Field for Game 2 of the series at 2 p.m. Saturday. The game will be streamed on Big Ten Plus.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: 'Clothing does not equal consent' | R-VOICE hosts annual 'What were you wearing?' art exhibit
author: Kyla Jones I The Daily Collegian
description:
Content warning: This story discusses sexual violence. Resources and support are available through Penn State’s Counseling and Psychological Services.

As part of the Relationship Violence Outreach, Intervention and Community Education Center’s (R-VOICE) annual “What were you wearing?” art installation, clothes draped partitions in the HUB-Robeson Center with lived stories of survivors of sexual assault.

The installation is one of a series of events R-VOICE holds or participates in every year for Sexual Assault Awareness Month, recognized annually in April.

Sophia Mills, associate director of the R-VOICE, said she helped coordinate the event. She said the purpose of the exhibit is to break down rape culture, myths and victim blaming that can sometimes follow an assault.

“By having something like this in a very prominent place in the HUB, it really draws people to learning that clothing does not equal consent, it doesn't matter what someone was wearing,” Mills said.

The display was held in the seating area near Alumni Hall and featured pieces like sweatpants, athleisure wear and a child's pink and purple princess night gown. Individuals were able to come and go as they pleased, and many stopped to read the stories detailing each survivor's experience above the clothing.

Mills said she recreates the outfits based on the description given on an online anonymous questionnaire form.

“We’ve been very intentional about finding those exact clothing items to represent the story accurately,” Mills said.

Along with the displays, R-VOICE and Center Safe had resource tables. Volunteers offered fidget toys, candy, resource pamphlets and other assistance for individuals who may find the exhibition triggering.

Cara Schmitt, who has volunteered with Centre Safe for about two years, said her story was one of many that were being displayed. As she began to share she began getting emotional. On the night she was assaulted, she was wearing layers of clothing — thermal leggings, dark blue jeans, a long-sleeved shirt, a turtleneck, a shawl and two pairs of socks.

“It’s not about the clothes, it's about what they want from the survivor,” Schmitt, a third-year biochemistry and molecular biology student, said. “When you really talk to someone who’s experienced it, it becomes more real. Otherwise, it's just statistics, another number to people.”

Rebecca Zoshak, a volunteer with Centre Safe, said the exhibition can aid public awareness. Having difficult conversations and people willing to listen to survivors’ stories is a huge part of processing it, she said.

“To have something tangible as this display shares makes the stories of the survivors much more and accessible,” said Zoshak. “The message behind the clothes speaks really loudly.”

MORE CAMPUS COVERAGE

title: State College ranked best small college town in America by USA Today 10Best
author: Saxtyn Lutz | For the Collegian
description:
Readers of USA Today 10Best ranked State College as the No. 1 small college town in America in its Best Readers’ Choice Awards.

With roughly 41,000 people residing in Happy Valley, the downtown scene is packed with a variety of cafes, shops, restaurants and bars.

Readers noted activities, places to eat and hotels that made State College the best. Featured activities include walking through Penn State’s Arboretum, football tailgates, visiting the Palmer Museum of Art and country night at Bill Pickle’s Tap Room.

Highlighted places to eat and drink include Champs, Berkey Creamery, Tadashi, Bagel Crust and The Field Burger & Tap. Places to stay include the Nittany Lion Inn, Wyndham Garden and Hyatt Place.

MORE NEWS CONTENT

title: Penn State baseball sparks comeback, opens series with win over Ohio State
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
The Nittany Lions were back on the road after winning their sole home game of the week against FDU on Tuesday. Penn State looked to use this momentum to get back on the winning side of conference play against rival Ohio State.

After falling behind 5-0 after the second inning, Penn State used 10 hits to mount a comeback, opening the series with a 10-6 win against the Buckeyes.

Colin Fitzgerald took the bump for Penn State after a quick three-and-out at the top of the first. The Lititz, Pennsylvania, native fell behind early after a solo shot from Noah Furcht gave the Buckeyes an early 1-0 lead.

Ohio State began to threaten once again in the second. After a Lee Ellis double off the right field wall, Maddix Simpson was able to get him across the plate with a single into left field. The bleeding would only get worse. Miles Vandenheuvel launched a three-run homer over the left field wall, ending a four-run inning.

The Nittany Lions needed a spark from their offense early after falling behind 5-0 headed into the third inning. Signs of life came off the bat of Kevin Karstetter after he tripled off the right-center wall. Avery Smith was able to score him from third off a sacrifice fly into center field.

Fitzgerald was able to escape a jam with runners on first and third. After his third strikeout of the game and a flyout into left, the senior was able to escape unscathed, keeping the deficit at four runs.

Penn State led off with an extra-base hit for the second-straight inning in the fourth. Michael Anderson doubled down the left field line. Bryce Molinaro followed suit with a two-run homer over the center field wall, cutting the Buckeyes' lead to two runs. The Drums, Pennsylvania, native is now tied for third in program home runs.

A momentum shift towards the Nittany Lions continued into the home half of the fourth. After facing another threatening situation with runners in scoring position, the defense was able to get out with any additional damage, keeping it a two-run game into the fifth inning.

Anderson brought Penn State’s deficit to only one run after a towering 438-foot solo home run cleared the center field wall. The Havertown, Pennsylvania, native now leads the Big Ten in homers with 14.

Fitzgerald’s day came to a close in the sixth inning as Ben Demell relieved the starter. The Concord, Ohio, native was able to keep momentum on the side of the Nittany Lions after pitching a scoreless inning.

What was once a 5-0 deficit for the blue and white was completely erased in the seventh inning. After a game-tying RBI single from Smith, Anderson came to the plate with bases loaded. The heavy-hitting righty made no mistake as he hit a line drive into left, scoring two. The Nittany Lions crawled their way back and took a 7-5 lead.

Ohio State immediately threatened to answer the three-run inning from Penn State. Reliever Kyle Emmons was brought in after a wild pitch from Dimond Loosli brought the Buckeyes within one. Emmons entered the game with bases loaded and two outs. The Pasadena, Maryland, native finished the inning with a strikeout.

The Nittany Lions used the eighth inning to add some insurance runs. Karstetter cleared the center field wall with a three-run home run, his first as a Penn Stater.

Penn State entered the home half of the eighth with a commanding 10-6 lead. Emmons remained in the game and was successful in retiring the Ohio State batters in order.

Anderson reached second off an error on a throw to first. However, three straight strikeouts ended the top of the ninth. Penn State headed into the bottom of the ninth, three outs away from completing a comeback.

Emmons stayed on the mound to close out the game. The reliever made quick work of the remaining Buckeyes batters and completed a 10-6 comeback win for Penn State.

Up next

Both teams return to Nick Swisher Field at Bill Davis Stadium for Game 2 tomorrow at 3 p.m.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: ‘It feels like home’ | State College celebrates first-ever Arab American Heritage Month
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
Community members came together in the Woskob Family Gallery on Thursday to participate in State College’s first-ever Arab American Heritage Month Celebration, with more events occurring Saturday.

National Arab American Heritage Month was first observed in 2017 by Arab America and the Arab America Foundation before being officially recognized by former President Joe Biden in 2022.

Manal Elfakhani said she joined forces with other Penn State faculty to plan the events after learning about national celebrations years ago.

“We discovered that we've never had a celebration in State College before, so we're like, ‘We need to do something about this,’” Elfakhani, an assistant teaching professor in the department of nutritional sciences, said.

She said there are many misconceptions and stereotypes about Arab culture, particularly within media portrayals. Community outreach, Elfakhani said, is a way to combat them.

“In movies, it's always someone who's a terrorist, which is very sad that people equate terrorism with our culture, so we're just trying to break through those misconceptions,” Elfakhani said.

The event began with a speech from Samar Farage, an associate teaching professor in the College of the Liberal Arts, who recounted the history of Arab Americans in the United States and their impacts. State College Mayor Ezra Nanes also made a proclamation declaring April 2026 as Arab American Heritage Month in State College.

Novelist Ghassan Abou-Zeineddine also spoke to attendees about how he translates his experiences as an Arab American into his writing. The event concluded with Malikat al Dabke, an all-women Dabke troupe, performing the Levantine folk dance before teaching attendees.

Monther Al Siyabi said he was happy to see this event, as it’s valuable but rare.

“It was an outstanding event,” Al Siyabi, a fifth-year studying electrical engineering, said. “Often, we don't see a lot of our culture showcased here at campus.”

He said he could see there being future events, should there be support from the State College community.

“There's so much enthusiasm, and we had a full house, so I think definitely the community would value something such as this,” Al Siyabi said. “Maybe more involvement with the undergrad student base, I think, could also be valuable, because there's a lot of undergraduate Arab students who look for stuff similar to this.”

Gaga Almutairi said the event was a way to connect with her culture despite being far from her home country of Saudi Arabia.

“It's not like home, but it feels like home,” Almutairi, a second-year studying criminology, said.

She said holding more events highlighting Arab culture would make Penn State feel more welcoming to potential students.

“It’s really hard to be around people who you have to speak in another language 24 hours,” Almutairi said. “It's really nice to come for a couple of hours, speak your own language, eat your own food and have a nice night."

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: Former Penn State women's volleyball setter Addie Lyon joins Liberty coaching staff
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After finishing her collegiate career with Penn State last season, Addie Lyon has taken on a new role in her volleyball career.

Lyon was named an assistant coach at Liberty on Wednesday, joining a squad that went 20-10 last season.

Welcome to the Mountain 🔥We are excited to have Addie Lyon join us as a new Assistant Coach!📰 https://t.co/aE1eShZTW4 pic.twitter.com/Ma8QOOGjUT— Liberty Volleyball (@LibertyWVB) April 9, 2026

title: Former Penn State women's basketball chief of staff Pam Brown hired at Florida

The first of former Penn State coach Carolyn Kieger's staff has found a new home.

Pam Brown was hired as the chief of staff at Florida after spending seven years at Penn State.

title: Penn State women's lacrosse to battle Northwestern on home turf with positive momentum
author: Aeryn Ajot | For the Collegian
description:
Thursday, Penn State continued its momentum and won against Oregon, 19-9, moving it from its No. 21 ranking last week to No. 18, before this weekend's game.

With an 11-3 winning streak, Penn State climbs as a tough competitor in the Big Ten, ranking No. 5 against teams with near-perfect seasons like Maryland.

This upcoming game on Sunday marks warm weather and a change of tide for the Nittany Lions; momentum is regained, and the conference is not lost.

Northwestern is one of the last games of the season, and the blue and white are looking for more wins before the end of the year, but Northwestern might be the school to ruin the momentum.

Ranked No. 4 in the nation, the school has beaten No. 1 Maryland and No. 2 North Carolina, proving the Wildcats are worthy competitors and the most challenging opponents yet.

The Wildcats have rankings on their side, proving to be a tough matchup for the Nittany Lions.

History

Penn State has competed against Northwestern since 1984, when the Nittany Lions defeated the Wildcats, 16-6.

However, since then, in 39 competitions, the blue and white have lost 29 times, creating a 10-29 record against Northwestern.

This past history is very relevant to Penn State’s success: in the past six years, the team has won only once, by a single point. Indicating that no current player has ever succeeded in defeating the Wildcats, making this anticipated game even more exciting.

Shared opponents

Penn State and Northwestern have run into the same crowd this year, sharing multiple competitors.

During this past season, they have both competed against Ohio State, Oregon, USC and Maryland.

Oregon proved to be a competitor that both teams beat, with the Nittany Lions winning 19-9 and the Wildcats winning by a more impressive margin of 17-3.

Unranked USC also fell by both schools, an 11-point loss against Penn State and a six-point loss against Northwestern.

Against Ohio State, Penn State won 18-9, whereas Northwestern lost by a small margin of 15-16. This result is not the only comparison in which the two schools have different outcomes.

No. 1 Maryland is the other competitor that makes predicting this game confusing. Against it, the Nittany Lions were defeated by a mere one point in College Park two weeks ago. While the Wildcats had the opposite fate of a one-point win against the No. 1 team in the nation on Thursday.

These narratives all come to a head, but the one regarding who will win this weekend and how rankings will change will be answered on Sunday at Panzer Stadium at 11 am.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s hockey earns elite commitment from Jaxon Cover
author: Chase Fisher | The Daily Collegian
description:
After a near mass exodus of its future roster, Penn State has finally earned a commitment to add to next year’s team.

Jaxon Cover, a left wing on the London Knights of the OHL, announced his pledge to the Nittany Lions on Instagram on Friday.

The 18-year-old played in 67 games for the Knights in 2025-26, posting 52 points, 20 goals and 32 assists. He is a 4-star recruit, per Puck Preps, and is expected to be a high pick in the 2026 NHL Entry Draft.

“I am proud and honoured to announce my commitment to play division 1 hockey at Penn State university,” Cover said on Instagram. “I would like to thank my family, friends, coaches and advisors.”

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Column | What to make of Penn State men’s basketball’s transfer portal exodus
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
A transfer portal exodus might be a dramatic term, but that’s happening in Happy Valley this offseason as seven Penn State players are searching for a new school.

To put it in perspective, this is 82.3% of the team’s scoring and 79.8% of the team’s minutes that are departing the program when also factoring in forward Josh Reed being out of eligibility. Furthermore, there could still be some more departures as 7-footer Ivan Jurić is the only player who’s officially confirmed he’d return.

With that said, what’s to make of all this roster turnover?

Starting from square one

Penn State only has five players left who’ve yet to depart the program: Jurić, forward Tibor Mirtič, forward Saša Ciani, guard Reggie Grodin and forward Chris Lotito. This group consists of a starting center in Jurić, a pair of rotational forwards in Mirtič and Ciani, a rising redshirt freshman in Grodin and a walk-on in Lotito.

In other words, the Nittany Lions are essentially starting from scratch, or as close as they could realistically be to it. Besides Jurić, they’re going to have to replace four starters, and ideally a team doesn’t want to lose cornerstone players like guard Kayden Mingo, who averaged 13.7 points, 3.5 rebounds, 4.3 assists and 2.1 steals per game.

Mingo leaving through the transfer portal marks another offseason where Penn State is seeing its best player leave. Last year, 7-footer Yanic Konan Niederhauser had a successful pre-draft process, which led to him getting selected in the first round of the 2025 NBA draft.

Along with Mingo, the Nittany Lions are also losing guard Freddie Dilione V, who led the team in scoring with 14.0 points per game. Complaints can be said about Dilione’s defensive efforts, but regardless, Penn State is still going to have to replace his scoring.

Another portal departure that should sting for the Nittany Lions is forward Mason Blackwood. The freshman made some flashes of his potential throughout the 2025-26 season, and I could’ve easily envisioned Blackwood starting next season.

Clean house might not be the worst thing

Considering Penn State had one of its worst seasons in program history in 2025-26, all this roster turnover might not be the worst possible outcome. Again, you’d ideally want to retain players like Mingo, but at the same time, a fresh start could be an advantageous opportunity.

As the roster stands now, the Nittany Lions have up to seven spots available for next season, along with the international additions of Roko Prkačin and François Wibaut and incoming freshman Jamison White. Additionally, there likely should be a good amount of NIL money available with all the departures.

For the record, Mike Rhoades said on Feb. 26 that he feels he has the financial resources necessary to acquire the talent needed for Penn State to take a step forward in the 2026-27 season.

“That's what's been great about talking with Pat Kraft and having great conversations with him and his staff and all that stuff,” Rhoades said on Feb. 26. “Pat wants to do right by us, and then wants to help us, and that's the direction we're trying to push to go to.”

With the transfer portal open, it seems like the Nittany Lions have the opportunity to recruit players who could make an immediate impact next season.

Needs for next season

As mentioned, Penn State is already set to add Prkačin, Wibaut and White. So for now, the roster for next season consists of Jurić, Mirtič, Ciani, Prkačin, Wibaut, White, Grodin and Lotito.

Looking at those players, the needs for the Nittany Lions are pretty clear. Penn State needs more guards, as Grodin is the only guard on the roster, which is also where the need for more shooting comes in.

The Nittany Lions currently lack a point guard but have reportedly reached out to IUP transfer Dallis Dillard, Central Connecticut transfer Jay Rodgers and Buffalo transfer Daniel Freitag. All of them enjoyed productive seasons with their respective teams in 2025-26.

Elsewhere, Penn State has Wibaut and White at the three, but neither of them has collegiate experience. Meanwhile, at power forward, Prkačin looks to have a chance to slide right in as the starter with Mirtič and Ciani providing rotational roles.

Center is the only position where I’d say Penn State is set up well. The Nittany Lions have their starter in Jurić, and then Mirtič and Ciani could split time behind him as backups. However, another center to replace 7-footer Justin Houser wouldn’t hurt.

Otherwise, work is needed for Penn State in the coming weeks if it wants to improve from last season.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: ‘Get sleep’ | Professors’ tips on how to deal with end of semester burnout, stress
author: Serina Chan I For the Collegian
description:
As Penn State students approach the end of the spring semester, maintaining good academics, extracurriculars and a social life all become overwhelming. Many students begin to experience burnout, mental exhausation caused by an overload of work, and few know how to properly handle it.

However, students aren’t the only ones who become stressed, as many campus professors feel the same. With many of the same priorities as students — grades, assignments and attendance — semester burnout affects those teaching, as much as those learning.

Matt McAllister said protecting priorities is beneficial to long-term health and preventing burnout.

“Making sure that you get sleep, your nutrition and your own downtime should be priorities,” McAllister, graduate programs chair in the Bellisario College of Communications, said. “Also amping up your organization. Map out your deadlines and not think of one project as a big deadline, but a series of smaller deadlines to make things seem more manageable.”

Yujin Heo expressed similar ideals, explaining that simple shifts in habits can make big differences.

“It is important to focus on finishing rather than perfecting,” Heo, an assistant professor in the Department of Advertising and Public Relations, said. “At this stage, trying to make everything flawless can actually slow you down and increase stress. A more realistic goal is to get your work to a solid, submit-ready level.”

According to Heo, another aspect of habit building also includes the elimination of daily choices that are made, because reducing unnecessary choices can help conserve mental energy.

“Even small decisions, like what to wear or what to eat, add up over time,” Heo said. “During the last few weeks of the semester, it can be helpful to simplify routines and limit daily choices so you can focus your energy on assignments and exams.”

Other simple habits that can be incorporated into daily routines, according to Sarah Kollat, are simply focusing on little things such as breathing exercises or going for a walk which can lead to reduced burnout rates.

Kollat, a teaching professor of psychology, emphasized the importance of therapy in helping to mitigate stress. While it's crucial to attend the doctor for yearly checkups or teeth cleanings at the dentist, maintaining mental health is just as important.

To prioritize mental wellness, other professors, like Qian Chen, promoted students and faculty to reserve moments for hobbies outside of work.

“I try to protect my me time,” Chen, an assistant professor of supply chain and information systems, said. “On the weekends, I try to make sure I have half a day to do something I want to do, not for my work, but just for myself.”

Chen also gave advice to students who are high achieving and try to maximize all the opportunities on Penn State’s University Park campus.

“Students typically have this mindset at the beginning of the semester,” Chen said. “At the end of the semester, the mindset needs to shift. Instead of maximizing everything, the priority should be to get things done. Get one thing done and move onto the other. When you have so many things in front of you and energy levels are lower, you don’t need everything to be perfect. You just need to get it done.”

Additionally, Denise Woodward said the most prominent ways she sees students feeling burnt out is directly from academic and social pressures. Her advice was to switch mindsets and focuses.

“Once you get into your job field, people care far more about the skills you bring in and the content knowledge you bring, rather than the grade you got,” Woodward, a teaching professor of biology, said. “Instead of saying I want to get an A on the final, say that you want to understand the content as much as possible.”

Most of all, professors follow the same standards of wanting the best for students. Woodward said communication between professors and students is vital for success during the school year, while McAllister said he aims to create an environment that ensures each person is earning their grade by equal treatment.

“Finding a good social support system is important,” Woodward said. “It’s more important to care about the opinions of people that you trust, love and value, than random people who don’t know you as well.”

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: Textures, bold colors, comfort | Summer style predictions
author: Suzain Khan | The Daily Collegian
description:
Summer style always tells you where people’s heads are at, and this year, it’s very clear: no one is trying hard. At least, not in the obvious way.

Recently, there’s been an energy shift from overstyling to understated confidence. It’s less about building an outfit and more about having a look — something consistent, personal and a little bit addictive to come back to.

With a few weeks left in the semester, here are my predictions for what styles will dominate this summer.

The “uniform” look is winning

Instead of constantly reinventing outfits, people are repeating combinations that work. Let’s be honest, when it’s too warm to function outside, the last thing you want to spend brain power on is your outfit. Staples such as gold hoops, thin chain necklaces and leather belts will stay in rotation.

An easy way to add flair is with a structured tote bag. The Cyme Mini from Polene is the perfect edition, but if you’re looking for something more affordable, the Maxi Shopper Bag from Zara is a great pick.

The outfit formula is simple, but specific: black tank tops, white baby tees, straight-leg jeans and oversized linen button-downs worn open. Pieces from Calvin Klein and Massimo Dutti carry this vibe effortlessly.

Hair and beauty are doing more of the heavy lifting. Think blowouts, slick low buns, tortoise shell printed claw clips, glasses (if that’s your cup of tea) and glossy lips paired with dewy skin.

It’s not effortless, it just looks like it is.

One statement color at a time

Bold, bright and unexpected pops of color are making a comeback but in a way that’s intentional, not chaotic.

We’re seeing butter yellow dresses, cherry red tops, cobalt blue skirts and citrus-tones bikinis take over. Instead of full color-blocked outfits, most looks rely on one standout piece, like a bright shoulder bag, bold mini skirt or colored sneakers paired with neutrals. If you’re going for that laid back, vacation look — Sabo does it well.

Patterns are slipping in subtly: horizontal stripes, polka dots and soft florals are everywhere, but they’re balanced rather than overpowering.

Model off-duty but slightly real

The “model off-duty” aesthetic is still here, just less polished.

Slick buns are being replaced with messy claw clip updos and loose, brushed out waves. Structured outfits are giving way to oversized zip-ups, boxy graphic tees and baggy or low-rise denim.

Accessories still matter — oversized sunglasses, canvas totes and chunky sandals are staples, but the overall look feels more lived in. You still look like the main character but you’ve been chilling, too.

‘90s staples, reworked

The recent ‘90s fashion revival isn’t going anywhere, but it’s softer now, less costume, more everyday wear. This one’s perfect for all of us who have been inspired by Carolyn Bassete’s outfits from “Love Story.”

Key pieces for this look include satin slip dresses layered over white T-shirts, mini skirts with oversized blazers and thin-strap camisoles paired with tailored trousers. Footwear can help to pull it altogether. Think chunky platform sandals, kitten heels and delicate strappy flats.

Everything is nodding to the ‘90s without screaming “throwback.” It’s the perfect balance of messy and polished to emulate your new summer vibe.

Personal style over micro-trends

Fast fashion is losing its grip — or at least its urgency.

Instead of chasing every new drop, people are building their own personal look from repeat pieces: vintage Levis, a go-to bandeau top or, for a classier look, a cardigan tied around your shoulders. Brandy Melville’s cardigans can offer the right type of feel for the style.

Cool and trendy summer looks are being built from carefully curated trips to thrift stores and vintage markets rather than last minute hauls from Shein. We’re bringing back graphic tees, patchwork pieces and unique outerwear. Style feels more curated, less disposable and more reflective of actual personality.

Textures are the new detail

Fabric and accessories are having their moment. Linen trousers, satin skirts, crochet tops, eyelet dresses and lightweight knits sets are showing up everywhere. Summer isn’t about a pattern overload. But it’s about touchable, interesting textures that make an outfit feel complete without extra effort.

Adding a silk scarf or dupatta elevates your look without overwhelming the outfit. If that’s not your style, incorporating big sunglasses, layered chains, tiny handbags or even a bold shoe can elevate a simple outfit. Accessories are no longer optional — they’re the thing that makes your repeated outfit feel fresh every time.

Comfort has become the look

It’s no longer about wearing what’s hot but what’s bearable. Staples include: flowy maxi dresses, loose trousers and airy tops, oversized button downs from Ralph Lauren or a more affordable option like Tommy Hilfiger, proving that being comfortable doesn’t make you look lazy. It means you know your style enough to wear it well without thinking.

Summer 2026 isn’t about chasing every trend. It’s about refinement, individuality and owning a look that’s recognizably you.

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: Preview | No. 8 Penn State men’s lacrosse looks to bounce back against Michigan
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 8 Penn State has had an up-and-down season in 2026, and the confusion continued last week as No. 10 Johns Hopkins defeated the blue and white in Baltimore, Maryland.

Despite leading by five goals in the third quarter, the Nittany Lions failed to hold onto their advantage, allowing the Blue Jays to score seven straight goals on their way to an 11-10 upset victory.

That slip gave Penn State its first Big Ten loss of the year, and fourth overall through 10 games.

With just two games remaining in the regular season, time is of the essence for the blue and white to get back on track before a season-closing matchup with No. 19 Rutgers.

A date with Michigan this Saturday could be the opportunity that the Nittany Lions need in order to do so. The Wolverines are 4-7 on the season, though they did pick up a ranked win last week against the Scarlet Knights.

It’s been a long year for the maize and blue. It rattled off three nonconference wins over Mercyhurst, Marquette and Detroit Mercy to begin the year, but then entered a seven-game losing streak leading into its upset win over Rutgers.

In defense of Michigan, however, the Wolverines have played a gauntlet of a schedule. While their first loss to Lehigh isn’t great for their resume, Michigan’s other six defeats have all come to ranked squads, including No. 2 Notre Dame, No. 6 Harvard and No. 9 Maryland.

The Wolverine offense largely runs through attackmen Nick Roode and Luke Shannehan, as their point totals rank fourth and sixth in the Big Ten, respectively.

Roode, a 5-foot-11 junior out of Peterborough, Ontario, has scored 33 points on the year. His mark of 28 goals is tied with two other players for most in the conference, and is a top-30 total nationwide.

Shannehan follows close behind with 22 goals. The Fairfield, Connecticut, native’s 31 points lead all Big Ten freshmen.

The other side of the ball is a totally different story, as the Wolverines have allowed a conference-high 122 goals on the campaign. Senior goaltender Hunter Taylor, who 119 of those shots sailed past, has posted a save percentage of just .487, a mark that ranks 53rd nationally out of 68 qualified netminders.

Penn State’s high-powered offense, currently leading the Big Ten with 13.2 goals per game, is poised to capitalize on that shaky defense on Saturday, especially with a conference-best shooting percentage of 35.3%.

The Nittany Lions have been historically strong against Michigan, holding an all-time record of 12-4. In recent years, it’s been a closer series, with the teams trading victories in the last four seasons.

The blue and white beat the Wolverines 11-8 in Ann Arbor last season, but the last time a team won consecutive games in the series was between 2021 and 2023, when Penn State took three straight matchups.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Review | Manna Korean BBQ & Hotpot is ‘souperb’
author: Rebecca Fein | The Daily Collegian
description:
Asian food is a delicacy, especially when you are in the middle of Pennsylvania, so when I heard about Manna Korean BBQ & Hotpot’s grand opening, I knew I had to try it.

Manna Korean BBQ & Hotpot is located at 542 Westerly Parkway, about an eight-minute drive from campus. They’re open from 12 p.m. to 10 p.m. Monday through Thursday, and 12 p.m. to 11 p.m. Friday through Saturday.

Let me say I went to review the food, but before diving into the dishes I feasted on, I have to compliment the atmosphere and what they did with the space.

You’d never think this small store on the corner of the strip would be so massive, but once you step inside, you are transported to a new world. Not only are there multiple seating areas, but it’s quite spacious, so you’re not dining on top of other guests.

With the welcoming and beautiful atmosphere, I was ready to eat. Here are some things I ordered that were “un-broth-leavibly” good.

To begin, you must pick your soup base. The options were endless: miso soup, herb broth, tomato broth, tomyum broth, tube bone soup, spicy Szechuan broth, three delicacies broth and golden fish and maw chicken broth.

I went with the tube bone, which is essentially just bone broth. I wanted a plain base so I could experiment with the different food options.

Next, I had to choose my meat. I ordered a variety: chicken, pork belly, lamb and rib eye. They bring your chosen raw meats out on a tray and set them beside your bubbling broth.

After the “ordering” section, you are allowed to get up from your seats and scour the buffet.

There were four different types of pastas, including ramen and udon, and bundles of vegetables, like bamboo, mushrooms, corn, lettuce, cabbage, kimchi, seaweed and cucumber salad. Not to mention, the buffet also featured beef balls, quail eggs, lobster balls, tofu and imitation crab.

Once my food was picked out from the buffet, I headed to the sauce bar to concoct a dressing for my cooked meats and veggies.

I was lost, with so many sauce ingredients, I didn’t know where to begin. Thankfully, there were signs right above suggesting different mixtures to make, with instructions on how to do so.

A picky eater's nightmare, but a real eater’s dream.

Next was the DIY (do it yourself) stage. The broth was steaming, the food was selected and now it was time to cook.

Don’t get me wrong, I love a good interactive meal, but if you are looking for a sit-down restaurant where you can lie back and relax, this isn’t the place for you.

Once cooked in the broth, the meat was tender and juicy. My udon and vegetables soaked up the flavor of the bone broth, and the suggested dipping sauce added the perfect amount of spicy garlic flavor.

After finishing the meal, there was a small ice cream and dessert bar available, but if you took full advantage of what this place has to offer, you probably wouldn’t be hungry for dessert.

Let’s talk about pricing. The hotpot meal, including full buffet access, two to three meats of your choosing, the broth base and the dessert bar comes out to around $35 to $40. I must add that you only get two hours of access to the buffet.

Definitely not a once-a-week-visit type of restaurant, but it’s a great environment to catch up with friends and eat yummy food, if you’re willing to shell out the money.

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: Penn State football draft profile | LB Dom DeLuca relying on special teams experience ahead of draft
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has had a few walk-ons see success throughout their careers, and linebacker Dom DeLuca was the latest example.

DeLuca joined the Nittany Lions in 2021, carving out a role for himself on special teams before becoming a crucial piece of Penn State’s linebacker room. He started 14 games across the last two seasons and played a big role in the program’s first College Football Playoff win.

While DeLuca is likely going undrafted, he had the chance to prove himself to NFL scouts at Penn State Pro Day on March 18. He said there were few workouts he’d like to “have back,” as he was focused on trying to set himself up to live out a childhood dream.

“I'm really just trying to show them I can play,” DeLuca said at pro day. “I have the ability to play a lot of special teams and be reliable on defense when needed, just doing anything I could to make a team really and just be trying to be that guy.”

Metrics

Height: 6-foot-1

Weight: 228 pounds

40-yard dash: 4.63 seconds

Vertical: 32.5 inches

Broad: 9-foot-8

Bench press: 11 reps

Projections

Projected round drafted: Undrafted

Player comparison: Curtis Bolton

Scouting report

A three-year captain, DeLuca established himself as one of the main leaders of the locker room during his time at Penn State. He was put on scholarship after the Nittany Lions’ 2023 Rose Bowl win, much to the joy of his teammates, showcasing his importance to their locker room.

DeLuca mostly played and made a name for himself on special teams during his first few years at Penn State, but he carved out a bigger role in 2024 after Abdul Carter’s move from linebacker to defensive end.

The Pennsylvania native finished his Nittany Lion career with 164 total tackles, 17.5 tackles for loss, 4.5 sacks, five interceptions, two forced fumbles and two defensive touchdowns, one of which was his iconic pick six in Penn State’s playoff win over SMU.

DeLuca was one of two starting linebackers for most of the 2025 season after Tony Rojas’ season-ending injury. But he often struggled in the WILL linebacker role, failing consistently to plug his gap and stop the run.

He also struggled in coverage, allowing 17 completions on 22 pass attempts this season, per PFF.

DeLuca had some bad and some good moments in Happy Valley, but he was still an inspiration for many as a former walk-on who became a starting linebacker and made history as just the third three-time captain.

Best team fit: Philadelphia Eagles

DeLuca said at Penn State Pro Day that he had a local visit lined up with the Philadelphia Eagles, a destination that would actually make sense for DeLuca as an undrafted free agent.

The Eagles lost a big piece of their linebacker room in Nakobe Dean, who signed with the Las Vegas Raiders in free agency, leaving Philadelphia with a need for more linebacker depth.

DeLuca likely won’t be one of the top guys in the rotation if he ends up with the Eagles, but he could learn from Zack Baun and linebackers coach Bobby King, making it a desirable destination for the Pennsylvania native.

title: Local talent sparks the debut of Mojofest in Happy Valley
author: Brennan Kramp I The Daily Collegian
description:
Local Mojo, a student-run promotional organization that helps connect local musicians to their communities, is planning its first Mojofest at Tussey Mountain on April 18. This is the first time the festival has come to Penn State since the founding of the organization in Amherst, Massachusetts in 2021.

Mojofest is a festival defined by the local community — local music, local art, local food. Mojofest will host bands and artists from State College and outside areas like Sarasota, Noah Richardson, Mellow Honey, Morrissey Blvd along with many more.

Local Mojo was founded by Charley Blacker during his sophomore year at University of Massachusetts Amherst. After arriving at college, he took a heavy interest in the area's local music scene and wanted to find ways for other community members to easily know when their next favorite local band would be playing.

“Our roots will always be: uplift local musicians, local artists and create community among people,” Blacker said.

Penn State’s chapter of Local Mojo is one of six chapters. Its creation came after Ava Plachter and a friend reached out to the directors of the UMass chapter last year, inquiring if they would consider creating another branch of Local Mojo. Now, serving as managing director, Plachter and company are helping craft the first Mojofest outside of Boston since 2025.

Plachter, a third-year studying architectural engineering and structural design and engineering technology, is one of four executive members who run the Penn State chapter and enjoy the comradery that comes with the Local Mojo mission.

“Having people that are equally passionate about it and are hardworking, cool people who you can rely on on your team is important, and that’s what we have at Mojo,” Plachter said.

From football games, to THON, to the Movin’ On music festival, Penn State has numerous unique cultural phenomenons. By putting on this festival, the Local Mojo crew strives to add to Penn State’s campus culture in a connective and collaborative way.

For students like Caya Kassaraba, the work is fueled by their love of local artistry as well as the possibility of longevity within the community.

“It’s crazy to think that this hasn’t happened at Penn State, like this could be something that becomes tradition. That’s just so mindblowing,” Kassaraba, a second-year studying elementary and early childhood education, said.

The first Mojofest was put on through a hard-driven grassroots campaign by Blacker and company, in the backyard of a rented house in Amherst.

According to Blacker, the festival consisted of a stage made of wooden pallets, a friends speaker system and decorations that were essentially bed sheets hanging from railings and anything else they could find that would hang from tree branches. A spectacle comparative to the youthful party film, “Project X,” but in a much more controlled fashion.

“We had, legitimately, no money at all to put this thing on,” he said. “But I think that’s what made the personality of it so cool … it was actually a really big success.”

In keeping with the do-it-yourself spirit, the core group of Local Mojo have embraced the collective struggle that comes along with setting up a whole music festival, while continuing to hold down jobs and class work.

Many of its dedicated members like Shane O’Mara, the team manager, find that while the work can be stressful, it’s also brought them together as colleagues and friends.

“We’re coming together a lot … I love the community Mojo has given me, we’ve all experienced and put in a good share of work,” O’Mara, a first-year studying business administration and management, said.

This small and tight knit group of students, founded under traditions of spirited resilience and gutsy ambition, plan to offer Penn State the festival of a lifetime. Their dedication to local talent, community building and the enjoyment of good company have given them the ability to do so.

While Local Mojo strives to build as large of a community as they can, Blacker said they’re blind to any perceived self-doubt or worries of being seen as sell-outs due to their growth as an organization. He said no matter what their core values of togetherness and casual fun, keep them going even through the occasional deflating effects of their hard work.

“We’re not answering to anybody except ourselves,” Blacker said. “I think that’s the beauty of it and we’re gonna keep going down that line. We’re doing this because we love it.”

MORE LIFESTYLE COVERAGE

title: ‘Getting as fast as she can’ | Boh Ritchie makes name for herself in 1st year with Penn State track
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
At 19 years old, Penn State freshman Boh Ritchie has done something very few people in the track community get to experience.

Following a record-setting indoor season, Ritchie took her talents to the 2026 World Athletics Championship, competing for her home country of New Zealand.

“She’s already making so many money moves,” teammate Hayley Kitching told The Daily Collegian. “I’m excited to see what she can do.”

While a world championship appearance was the cherry on top for Ritchie’s historic freshman indoor season, there were plenty of other reasons to celebrate the rising star.

Ritchie graduated from high school in New Zealand in December and, less than a month later, made the long trek to the United States to begin her freshman year in Happy Valley.

“It’s been a lot of change, but everyone’s been so welcoming,” Ritchie told the Collegian. “I’m training with (coach Ryan) Foster, so it kind of felt a little bit like home. I’ve enjoyed the first week.”

Coming into Penn State, Ritchie had a lot of experience competing on the world stage. The 19-year-old became one of the best 800m runners in New Zealand history, holding multiple 800m records for various age groups.

Despite this, Ritchie’s teammates knew little about her when she came to Penn State, and her quiet demeanor continued in her first weeks with the team.

“Honestly, I had no idea who she was when she was being recruited,” Kitching said. “On her visit, she was pretty quiet, so I didn’t really know what to expect.”

While that was the commentary from her teammates, Ritchie’s coaches saw the potential and skillset from her early on in her recruitment and first weeks in Happy Valley.

“What’s really impressed me is her general demeanor towards sports; she’s very composed,” Foster told the Collegian. “She’s got some really good training habits and has been very easy, which is great having someone who’s low maintenance and high performance.”

After less than one week of practice with the Nittany Lions, Ritchie took the track for the Nittany Lion Challenge, competing in the 600-meter for her first-ever indoor race.

“I kind of just wanted to see what I could do,” Ritchie said. “I didn’t really have any expectations going out there, just to enjoy myself and run fast and see what I could do.”

Ritchie’s first collegiate race kickstarted her path to fame, as she won the 600m with a time of 1:28.21 — the second-best mark in school history, behind Kitching.

“We’re super excited about (her performance),” Kitching said. “For her to come in and do that straight off the bat, it’s really exciting for the future at our school.”

Two weeks later, Ritchie’s success continued as she set the No. 2 time in school history in the 800m, running 2:01.60 for another mark just behind Kitching. Despite the early success, those top marks weren’t what Foster and the Nittany Lions were looking for from Ritchie.

“One thing we’ve talked about is just keeping the pressure off,” Foster said. “As talented as she is and as well coached as she’s been, the level of the NCAA is super high, so we’re not putting a lot of pressure and expectations yet.”

As Ritchie continued her freshman year campaign, she’s stood out for more than just her work on the track. Ritchie’s mindset proved to be next-level and was something even upperclassmen on the team noted.

“She’s honestly very mature as an athlete,” Kitching said. “She’s come in and she definitely has that elite mindset that athletes need to have to thrive.”

While the New Zealand native didn’t earn a personal best at the 2026 Husky Classic in mid-February, she came within 0.05 seconds of a new mark. However, she helped push Kitching to her first sub-two-minute 800m in part of a stacked race.

“It’s just such a healthy competition vibe we’re having as we strive to be better ourselves, but also helping each other,” Kitching said. “Having Boh there, she’s pushing me to be better, and then I can help her get better. It’s the best sort of environment to be in.”

Ritchie’s first-year success earned her a spot in the postseason, as she finished fourth in the conference in the 800m and helped the Nittany Lions to a bronze medal in the distance medley relay.

Following the Big Ten Championship, Ritchie was ranked 15th in the country in the 800m, earning a spot at the NCAA Championship. There, she finished in 11th place, earning All-American second team honors in her first collegiate season, beating out Kitching.

“She’s just super keen to get as fast as she can,” Kitching said. “She’s constantly trying to beat me.”

While Ritchie’s collegiate indoor season was over, she still had one last chance to race indoors at the 2026 World Athletics Indoor Championship, an opportunity presented through her growth at Penn State.

“The main reason I came over here was to be competitive with such a competitive organization,” Ritchie said. “I want to come over here and train with good people so I can better myself.”

Ritchie ultimately capped off her indoor season, finishing 25th in the world with a time of 2:03.28 in Round 1 of the championships.

While she’s yet to compete during the 2026 outdoor season, teammates, coaches and fans are excited for her potential in her next three years.

“I think there will be plenty of moments in her career where she has the pressure and expectations,” Foster said. “Right now, it’s just going to see what she can do, but quietly, I’m hoping that’s some pretty good things.”

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse dominates Oregon under the lights
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Under Thursday night lights, Penn State returned home to Panzer Stadium for the first time since mid-March.

The Nittany Lions hosted Oregon at the halfway point of their Big Ten schedule, dominating the Ducks 19-9.

Despite statistically looking significantly stronger, Penn State let Oregon take an early lead. Junior Lexi Jenkins found the back of the net less than a minute into the game, taking a 1-0 lead.

“Credit to Oregon, I thought they played excellent tonight,” coach Kayla Treanor said. “They played really physical tonight, brought the intensity and gave us a challenge.”

However, freshman Rocquette Allen continued doing what she’s done all season, taking the lead back with two straight goals, one being assisted by Kelly MacKinney.

Just over a minute later, Penn State’s lead continued to grow as Delaney Radin scored her 19th goal of the season to take a 3-1 lead. That didn’t last long, as redshirt-senior Emma Ing scored to bring it back to one goal.

After a few minutes of action and a green card on Oregon, senior Emma Kelly scored a man-up goal for the Nittany Lions, but was once again canceled out by Jenkins’ second goal of the day.

The blue and white found itself back on the free position line after another penalty by Oregon, taking a 5-3 lead into the quarter after Payton Wainman’s first goal of the day.

Taking the possession out of the break, Radin earned her 44th assist of the day, finding Wainman for her second goal of the day. A little over a minute later, MacKinney found the net again for another goal off a free position attempt.

“It’s a really balanced unit; it could be anybody’s night,” Treanor said. “We have so much depth offensively, which I think makes us a really difficult unit to stop.”

MacKinney’s success continued with another free position goal for her 34th of the season and 62nd point of the year.

Allen’s dominant freshman season marked another career milestone, recording her 50th career point as she completed a hat trick early in the second quarter.

“Rocq causes so much attention, being that she’s Rookie of the Week every single week and gets the top matchup,” Radin said.

Following a change at goalie for the Ducks, Penn State’s offensive attack continued with Radin’s second goal of the game, assisted by MacKinney.

After nearly 10 minutes without a score from Oregon, sophomore Lilly Fortin stopped Penn State’s run with her first goal of the game. However, the blue and white were back on attack with a highlight reel play from Radin, who dove forward and scored behind-the-back for her second hat trick of the season.

With just over a minute left until halftime, the Ducks brought it back to a six-goal game with Anna Simmons’ first goal of the day.

Coming out of the half, it was Oregon first on the board again with another goal from Simmons, dropping goalie Sydney Manning’s save percentage to 40% on the night.

After over seven minutes without any scoring from Penn State, MacKinney became the third Nittany Lion with a hat trick on the night, taking a 12-6 lead.

“(Kelly’s) just grown so much this year as a player and as a person, our captain,” Treanor said. “She’s getting better every game and is so coachable.”

The blue and white went on another scoring run with Ho, who scored back-to-back goals for her 20th and 21st goals of the season. However, Oregon’s Avery Young stopped the bleeding with her first goal of the day, which was quickly canceled out by MacKinney’s fourth goal of the game.

While a challenge by Treanor overturned what would’ve been another goal for the blue and white, Radin assisted Allen for her fourth goal of the game, as Radin recorded her 44th assist on the season — the second-most assists in a single season in program history.

“I couldn’t have done any of it without my teammates,” Radin said. “They set me up for success every day, so I really just have to give all things to them.:

Following a free position shot by Ho that was initially ruled no goal, Treanor challenged the ruling again, which was overturned for her third goal of the day.

After three straight goals from Penn State, Oregon earned a pair of goals, including Ing’s second goal of the day and a free-position goal by Jenkins.

However, Penn State was back to scoring as Allen recorded her fifth goal of the game, assisted by Kelly. On the following possession, Kelly recorded her 100th draw control of the season.

“Emma was really focused, and we ended up winning draw controls tonight,” Treanor said. “We certainly needed that tonight.”

Following a timeout by the Nittany Lions, Kelly recorded her second goal of the day with an assist from sophomore Maggie Rezza, which prompted a timeout from Oregon.

The Nittany Lions ended the game with a solid showing from Manning, Makenna Gagliardi and Emily Bette, moving to 11-3 on the season.

Up next

The Nittany Lions will host No. 4 Northwestern at 12 p.m. on Sunday.

title: Penn State TE room ‘coming together great’ despite multiple injuries
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
The Penn State tight end room entered spring ball as a thin unit.

Andrew Rappleyea and Gabe Burkle are out for spring practice, making Benjamin Brahmer the only player with significant game experience available. As a result, there have been increased opportunities for Cooper Alexander, Finn Furmanek and Brian Kortovich, who missed practice on Thursday but spoke to the media.

“I think when the reps are at such a high level, it forces you to put those guys in positions to go fail or challenge them mentally, where it's not so physical,” offensive coordinator Taylor Mouser said. “We were running out there as coaches trying to give them different looks, to force them to communicate. So I think regardless if we had three or if we've had four, the hard part is being able to challenge the guys that are out there.”

Of the six-person room, three of the tight ends are from last year’s roster in Rappleyea, Furmanek and Kortovich. Brahmer, Burkle and Cooper all followed Matt Campbell from Iowa State to State College.

Despite the unit coming together from two different places, the processing of building a bond and meshing together happened quickly.

“The position room’s coming together great,” Kortovich said. “Three Penn State guys, three Iowa State guys, came together and ever since Day 1 we've been talking, having dinners together, stuff like that, and couldn't have blended better.”

Alexander saw the field in all 12 games as a redshirt freshman, seeing expanded work due to Burkle’s ACL tear. Furmanek, who was put on scholarship this year, played in all 13 games last year on special teams, but only appeared in two contests through three seasons prior to that. Kortovich didn’t see the field at all, but made the travel roster twice for away games.

When Week 1 arrives against Marshall, the Nittany Lions are rolling out Brahmer, Rappleyea and Burkle assuming all works out health wise, but for now the other three tight ends are preparing like starters and using the increased workload to get better.

“We don't have a whole lot of tight ends right now, so we're all getting a ton of reps,” Alexander said. “So I think it's been awesome for the guys that are healthy right now.”

It’s a revamped tight end room, but Campbell and the rest of the staff are aware of the history at the position at Penn State — Kyle Brady, a letterman at the position, was around the program this week.

The new regime is prepared to add to the long list of NFL tight ends that the Nittany Lions have produced like Tyler Warren, Theo Johnson, Brenton Strange, Mike Gesicki and Jesse James.

“We'll work really hard to continue that lineage of success here,” Campbell said. “That's something that we believe in. All the way back to our days in Toledo, we've been fortunate to coach and have great tight ends in our offense, and obviously, with the history of this place, you hope any great tight end would want to be a part of what we have going on.”

While the room isn’t operating at full strength, the bond has come together quickly. They’re having a great time off the field, but by the time it’s time to suit up for game action and everyone is healthy, the group is ready to carry over that energy into contributing in games.

“The tight end room is really fun. We're all great friends, and it's a great time in there,” Brahmer said. “And it's also fun when we get to be on the field and making plays and having fun with each other. So it's a good relationships we got going.”

title: Reviewing Penn State’s progress through midway point of spring ball | The 1-0 Podcast
author: The Daily Collegian Football Staff
description:
The “1-0 Podcast” co-hosts Avery Hill, Lexie Linderman and Lyle Alenstein hit the studio hours after Penn State’s spring practice viewing session to discuss the day.

The conversation started with a look at the biggest storylines, including Rocco Becht returning to play as quarterback.

The trio also previewed players looking to return to action, and reviewed the impact of said players’ absences thus far.

title: Penn State notebook | OC Taylor Mouser talks TE room, spring surprises
author: Avery Hill | Football Editor
description:
The Master’s started on Thursday, so Taylor Mouser will likely be glued to the TV. But first, he has to take care of business.

That business is his career, which is now being Penn State’s offensive coordinator and tight ends coach.

While he gave his thoughts on some expected standouts on the golf course this weekend, he also talked about the progress his new squad has made at the midway point of spring ball during his Thursday post-practice presser.

Here’s what Mouser had to say.

Tight ends

Matt Campbell and company re-acquired Iowa State’s top tight ends in Benjamin Brahmer and Gabe Burkle upon the move to Penn State, and they also renewed the services of Andrew Rappleyea, along with others.

It’s a room with a good chunk of experience, but both Rappleyea and Burkle have been absent all spring, and redshirt freshman Brian Kortovich was absent on Thursday, though he spoke with the media after practice.

While he wants his players healthy, Mouser knows this has given other guys a chance to get accustomed.

“A guy like Finn Furmanek gets more reps than he ever would have gotten in the room in the spring. And same with Ben, there was a lot that I challenged him to grow in the run game,” Mouser said. “So I think when the reps are such at a high level, it forces you to put those guys — and we can give different looks — put those guys in positions to go fail or challenge them mentally.”

Furmanek was placed on scholarship after being a walk-on from State College under James Franklin. The review on Furmanek from Mouser was positive, saying he looks like a “veteran out there.” Mouser was also impressed with Brahmer and redshirt sophomore Cooper Alexander, specifically noting their strides physically.

“The tight ends have been consistent,” Mouser said.

Learning scheme

This seems to be the case every year in Happy Valley recently, but Penn State has been focused on installing its new offensive scheme.

To the benefit of Mouser and the offense, a lot of his players are already accustomed to it as they followed him from Iowa State, but there are plenty of players new to the offense as well.

Mouser isn’t singling out the non-former Cyclones in the process, however. Along with his NFL and football clinic tapes, he said their film sessions include bad tape from Iowa State.

“Sometimes you learn more, like, when you're installing it, those guys learn more from when it gets messed up,” Mouser said.

Previously, Brahmer said Mouser wanted to talk to former Penn State tight ends to get involved with the program. That has played a part in teaching the unit the offense, as Mouser said he’s put on now-Jacksonville Jaguar Brenton Strange’s blocking tape for his offense.

With a look at some of the best examples along with some of the worst, Mouser’s trying to keep his unit as prepared as possible.

“I want to make sure those Penn State guys can go and be able to get it right, versus the wrinkles that are going to be different,” Mouser said. “It's not going to be just a 4-2 box. There's going to be backside pressures and front side pressures.”

Surprises

With a new coaching staff, there’s naturally going to be players who impress more than the coaches or the general public expect.

Along with the aforementioned Furmanek, Mouser has been impressed with a ton of players this season. Offensive tackle Malachi Goodman is likely at the top of his list of surprises.

“Malachi Goodman’s one of the most talented guys I've ever seen with my own two eyes,” Mouser said. “So I could go on all day about the raw talent of him.”

Goodman returned to Penn State after not playing his freshman season. He was listed as the No. 56 player nationally, per the 247Sports Composite rankings, but was just 17 years old when he arrived on campus. Mouser is impressed by his natural ability, intelligence and movement for his size.

Other players Mouser noted include center Brock Riker, quarterback Connor Barry, wide receiver Amarion Jackson, running back Quinton Martin Jr., wide receiver Karon Brookins and wide receiver Koby Howard.

Of the list of players, just Howard, Barry and Riker saw significant playtime last season, as Howard notably caught seven passes for 133 yards and a touchdown. Mouser has been impressed with his efforts thus far.

“He's a guy that's work ethic challenges everybody in the room,” Mouser said. “Not in a bad way, but he's a guy that never leaves the Jugs machine. He's a guy that's around the facility all the time. He's a guy that's asking me questions, and when you prepare like that, it forces everyone else to prepare, or you stand out.”

title: ‘They've been helping me a ton’ | TE Brian Kortovich adapting to new Penn State staff this spring
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
After tight end Brian Kortovich met with Matt Campbell and Taylor Mouser following the coaching changes at Penn State this offseason, he made a quick decision about his future.

Kortovich said he couldn’t say no to returning to the Nittany Lions when they described what their plan was for him. Now, with the team in the midst of spring ball, Kortovich explained how he’s acclimated to his new coaches and teammates following Thursday’s practice.

“It's been pretty awesome just in the playbook and everything,” Kortovich said. “We've been meeting a ton. They've been great helping me. I'm still kind of a young guy, but they've been helping me a ton.”

In addition to the coaching staff’s pitch, Kortovich said Happy Valley is a special place for him. The Willoughby, Ohio, native said being close to home also helped him decide to remain a Nittany Lion.

While Kortovich was on the travel rosters for Penn State’s matchups with Ohio State and Rutgers last season, he kept his redshirt since he didn’t appear in any games. He was a 247Sports composite 3-star and the No. 531 player nationally in the 2025 class.

During his recruitment, Kortovich was originally committed to Purdue before flipping to the Nittany Lions. His first year at Penn State didn’t go as expected however, as James Franklin was fired after six games.

“It's just crazy,” Kortovich said. “I think it's all in God's hands. Everything I believe happens for a reason, and it was crazy, that turn of events when I ended up committing here right before signing day. It was pretty cool, just how the timing worked out and stuff like that.”

After all the dust settled this offseason and Penn State got its tight end room set in stone, the team had three returnees in the position group — Kortovich, Finn Furmanek and Andrew Rappleyea. The Nittany Lions brought in three transfers from Iowa State as well.

Kortovich said he’s helped the new tight end additions adjust to playing and living at Penn State. After practice No. 8 out of 15 in the spring, he thinks the blend of returnees and transfers has gone well.

"It's been great since day one.
